Society for Industrial Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States This panel session will touch on what nudging is; ways to create and implement nudges to drive actions, tips, considerations; the role of technology and nudges in transforming the follow-up process; and new frontiers for nudging in organizations.

title: Optimizing AI Adoption: Measuring Sentiment and Productivity Across Industries - (Session ID 925)
speakers: Caitie Jacobson Mikulis | Kelsey Kline | Sami Nesnidol | Ketaki Sodhi | Jeremy Watson
description: Authors: Watson, J. (Chair), Sodhi, K. (Panelist), Kline, K. (Panelist), Nesnidol,S. (Panelist), & Jacobson, C. (Panelist) (2025). Optimizing ai adoption: Measuring sentiment and productivity across industries [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, Colorado, United States. As AI tools, particularly generative AI, become more common, companies face challenges in measuring their impact on employees, culture, and business outcomes. This panel brings together leaders from Target, Vaya Group, Intel, Dell Technologies, and Microsoft to discuss their approaches to measuring AI's influence on key metrics and employee experiences. Panelists will share best practices, early data insights, and offer participants a chance to explore specific areas, ask questions, and brainstorm solutions for their own organizations.

title: Pathways to Impact: Job Crafting Through Alternative Leadership Roles in Higher Ed - (Session ID 262)
speakers: Wendy Bedwell-Torres | Deborah DiazGranados | Lisa Finkelstein | Julia Fullick-Jagiela | Kiku Jones
description: Authors: Fullick-Jagiela, J. M. (Co-Chair), Bedwell-Torres, W. L. (Co-Chair), DiazGranados, D., Finkelstein, L., & Jones, K. (2025). Exploring pathways to impact: Job crafting and alternative leadership roles in higher ed [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. This panel explores job crafting through alternative higher education leadership roles, such as faculty senate, center directorship, student program development, employee resource group leadership, and executive education. It highlights diverse pathways for faculty to create meaningful work and positive impact in academia. Attendees will gain insights into job crafting, learn from leaders’ experiences, and discover strategies for implementing job crafting to enhance career satisfaction and meaningful impact.

title: Personality and Leading for Creativity: Looking Through the Reputation Lens - (Session ID 1074)
speakers: Robert Kaiser | Averie Linnell | Nadine Maliakkal | Kevin Mitchell
description: Authors: Mitchell, K. S. (Co-Chair), Maliakkal, N. T. (Co-Chair), & Hogan, R. (Discussant) (2025). Personality and Leading for Creativity: Looking Through the Reputation Lens. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Maliakkal, N. T. (2025). Creative leadership: A meta-analytic examination of leaders’ bright-side personality. Kaiser, R. B. (2025). Personalities of creative leaders and leaders of others’ creativity. Linnell, A. E., Reiter-Palmon, R., & Keller Hansbrough, T. (2025). Intimidating innovators: Follower perceptions of creative yet abusive leaders. Creativity is vital for businesses to survive, thrive, and compete. Though researchers and practitioners know the key role leaders play in attaining creative outcomes, several questions remain: What characteristics help a leader direct a creative enterprise? What characteristics help a leader facilitate others’ creativity? Do we expect these characteristics to differ? How impactful is a leader’s creative reputation? This symposium applies a reputation lens to unpack these questions regarding creative leadership, offering theoretical, practical, methodological, and future research implications.

title: Poster Sessions: Strategic HR/Utility/Changing Role of HR, Counterproductive Behavior/Workplace Deviance, and Teaching I-O Psychology/Student Issues/Professional Development
tracks: Strategic HR/Utility/Changing Role of HR | Counterproductive Behavior/Workplace Deviance | Teaching I-O Psychology/Student Affiliate Issues/Professional Development | Poster Sessions

title: Practical Solutions to Common Challenges in Impact Measurement - (Session ID 1154)
speakers: Heather Champion | Christina Fleck | Chia-Lin Ho | Jeff Kosovich
description: Authors: Ho, C.-L. (Chair), Champion. H., Fleck, C., & Kosovich. J. J. (2025). Practical solutions to common challenges in impact measurement [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Drawing on the collective knowledge and experience of practitioners and researchers in impact measurement, this session will explore common challenges in measuring the impact of learning and development initiatives; introduce three practical, under-valued solutions to help address those challenges; and demonstrate the application of the solutions with actual data and tools. Panelists will share insights to demonstrating the value of learning and development through impact measurement.

title: Show Us the Money: Need for Further Research and Educational Support in Compensation - (Session ID 784)
speakers: Lynn Beer | Krista Carter | Lisa England | Leah Klos | Sarina Maneotis | Julio Manso
description: Authors: Maneotis, S. M. (Chair), Beer, L., Carter, K., England L., Klos, L. S., & Manso, J., (2025). Show us the money: The need for further research and educational support in compensation [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Employee compensation is a critical talent management function and yet an area where research and education often leave practitioners underprepared. A panel of experts with a variety of degree types (e.g., MS, MBA, PhD) and experience (higher education, public and private sector industry) are assembled to discuss how they use research (or not) to inform compensation decisions within their work roles and how their graduate educations support (or did not) their ability to interface with compensation decisions and data. Implications for researchers, practitioners, and educators are discussed.

title: Surprising Research Insights & Practical Paths for Addressing The Social Capital Imperative
speakers: Kristin Cullen-Lester | Greg Pryor

title: The Impact of Nontraditional Work on Workers, Organizations, and Society - (Session ID 811)
speakers: Gabriela (Gabby) Burlacu | Russell Cropanzano | Steven Hunt | Amy Pytlovany
description: Authors: Burlacu, G., Cropanzano, R., Hunt, S., & Pytlovany, A. (2025). The impact of non-traditional work on workers, organizations, and society (an alternative session). Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Technological advancements, remote work models, and increasingly turbulent markets have created the possibility for more nontraditional talent models (i.e., gig labor, freelancing, and other modes of work that do not require a traditional 9-to-5 full time employment contract). This panel will bring together researchers and practitioners to discuss the current and potential impact of nontraditional work on people, organizations, and our workforce and society at large.

title: The Internship Equation: Intern and Supervisor Perspectives for a Successful Internship - (Session ID 628)
tracks: Teaching I-O Psychology/Student Affiliate Issues/Professional Development
speakers: Erica Barto | Ashley Biswell | Lindsay Bousman | Mary Chacko | Lesly Krome | Abigail Burt | Miguel Gonzalez
description: Authors: Chacko, M., Krome, L. R. (Chair), Cox, C. B., Bousman, L., Barto, E., Biswell, A., Gonzalez, M., & Burt, A. (2025). The Internship Equation: Intern and Supervisor Perspectives for a Successful Internship [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. The purpose of this panel is to share internship experience through the lens of the student-university-employer internship effectiveness model. Featuring pairs of interns and their supervisors, this panel will provide insights on the preparation, execution, and evaluation phases of the internship process. This session will present insights and recommendations tailored for 3 key groups: practitioners who lead interns, students in search of internship opportunities, and academics who assist students throughout their internship journey.

title: Understanding Neurodiversity at Work: Perceptions, Intersectionality, and Interventions - (Session ID 1477)
speakers: Debra Comer | Debora Gottardello | JeongJin Kim | Joie Magalona | Rebecca Mintz | Lida Ponce | Narda Quigley | Sarah Wittman
description: Authors: Ponce, L. P. (Co-Chair), Mintz, R. M. (Co-Chair), & Wittman, S. (Co-Chair). (2025). Understanding Neurodiversity at Work: Perceptions, Intersectionality, & Interventions [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO. United States. Recognition of neurodiverse individuals in the workplace has grown as part of recent efforts to improve inclusivity overall. However, stereotypes still abound. This symposium contains papers that provide updated perspectives on neurodiversity at work, expound upon the additional challenges faced by neurodiverse individuals with additional minority identification, and present interventions that may improve perceptions of neurodiverse individuals at work. The aim is to advance scholarship on the unique experiences of this group, with the hope of advancing workplace inclusion for neurodiverse individuals.

title: What a Difference an Election Makes: EEOC/OFCCP Practitioner Update - (Session ID 855)
speakers: Michael Aamodt | Joanna Colosimo | Laura Davis | Michelle Duncan | Dr. Katey E. Foster
description: Authors: Colosimo, J.L. (Chair), Aamodt, M.G., Davis, L., Duncan, M., Foster, K.E. (2025). What a Difference an Election Makes: EEOC/OFCCP Practitioner Update. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Organizations are under increased scrutiny from the EEOC, OFCCP, and private litigants concerning affirmative action, nondiscrimination, and DEI efforts. Federal agencies and contractors must adjust to shifts in political leadership and regulatory efforts at the science–law intersection. This panel will update the I-O community on EEO trends, current enforcement by agencies, DEI initiatives, pay equity, and AI regulation, with insights into potential impacts of the U.S. presidential election. Experts including legal professionals, practitioners, and employers will share their perspectives.

title: ¡Para la Cultura! Embracing and Leveraging Latine Culture in Industry and Academia - (Session ID 1136)
speakers: Aylime Bueno | Samantha Erskine | Jenn Feitosa | Bernardo Ferdman | Patricia Guerrero | Zytlaly Magaña | M. Gloria González-Morales
description: Authors: Guerrero, P. (Co-Chair), Bueno, A. (Co-Chair), Solis, L., Roberson, A., Delgado, P. (Panelist), Erskine, S. (Panelist), Feitosa, J. (Panelist), Ferdman, B. (Panelist), Gonzalez-Morales, M.G. (Panelist), & Magaña, Z. (Panelist) (2025). ¡Para la cultura! Embracing and leveraging Latine culture in industry and academia [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. The increasing number of Latine professionals in both industry and academia highlights the need for deeper exploration into their experiences navigating workplace dynamics influenced by cultural identity. Despite their growing presence, many face challenges such as cultural stereotypes and feelings of marginalization. These barriers often hinder their ability to fully express and embrace their cultural heritage while striving for professional advancement. This panel aims to offer insights into fostering inclusive, multicultural environments that support the identities of Latine professionals.

title: General Conference Registration
AI-Assisted Applications: Rethinking How We Apply, Hire, and Connect (Session ID 199)
speakers: Kimberly Silva | April Cantwell Holland | Cat Yaris | Lauren Wegmeyer | Ngoc Duong
speaker_affiliations: Talogy | Harver | Capital One | PepsiCo
description: Silva, K., Cantwell, A., Duong, N.S., Wegmeyer, L., & Yaris, C. (2026, April 30 – May 2). AI-assisted applications: Rethinking how we apply, hire, and connect [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI is reshaping the way candidates search, apply, and polish application materials—and companies are struggling to keep pace. This session explores how and why job seekers turn to AI, the challenges it creates for employers, and how both sides can adapt. Panelists will share practical advice for candidates on using AI thoughtfully without losing authenticity, and for companies on evolving hiring practices to focus on skills, fairness, and human connection.

title: Blending Kirkpatrick and Psychometrics to Evaluate Leadership Development Impact (Session ID 577)
speakers: Stewart Desson
speaker_affiliations: Lumina Learning
description: Desson, S., & Cannon, J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Blending Kirkpatrick and Psychometrics to Evaluate Leadership Development Impact. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Evaluating leadership development needs more than ROI metrics. Two large-scale programs with Lumina Learning used an integrated framework combining psychometrics, experiential learning, and Kirkpatrick’s model. Among 72 leaders, results showed significant gains in reflective communication (+16%), resilience (+14%), and purposeful argumentation (+6%), alongside stronger inclusion and psychological safety. This multimethod, human-centered approach captures the true complexity of leadership impact beyond linear or financial measures.

title: Community of Interest: Bricolage in I-O: How to Do More With Less in a Changing World (Session ID 1432)
speakers: Jonathan Kochert | Matthew Christensen | Roza Jankovic | Kyle Benbow
speaker_affiliations: U.S. Army Research Institute for Behavioral and Social Sciences | Deloitte Consulting | Culture Amp | Army Research Institute
description: Benbow, K. L., Christensen, M., Jankovic, R., & Kochert, J. (2026, Month TBD, Day TBD) Community of Interest: Bricolage in IO: How to do more with less in a changing world. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Pressures from emerging trends are forcing organizations to do more with less. Are I-Os addressing these demands in a purposeful manner? The concept of bricolage provides a framework for addressing these trends. How can the systems and personnel characteristics that will lead to success in this environment be identified? How can the “do more with less mentality” be discarded and this as an opportunity to be adaptive, creative, and playful be embraced? This will be an audience participation session; presenters are eager to hear how attendees are/would respond to these trends.

title: Creative Micropractices: Cultivating Reflection, Connection, and Workplace Well-Being (Session ID1069)
speakers: Patricia Friberg
speaker_affiliations: Patricia Friberg Coaching
description: Friberg, Patricia (2026, April 30-May 2). Creative Micro-Practices: Cultivating Reflection, Connection, & Workplace Well-being {Master Tutoria} Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Relational well-being is recognized as essential for workplace well-being and overall human flourishing. However, many organizations lack practical methods to cultivate them. This interactive workshop introduces micro-arts practices, which involve short sessions, such as music, movement, drawing, or virtual museum visits, to promote reflection, empathy, perspective taking, and trust. Based on current research, this beginner-level, participatory session equips participants with actionable tools to integrate creative reflection into coaching, leadership, and team dynamics.

title: Decoding AI: Amplifier of Belonging or Architect of Inequity? (Session ID 989)
speakers: C. Malik Boykin | Christine Smith | Stephanie Tavarez | Taiwo Togun
speaker_affiliations: Brown University | The Culture Backbeat | University of Hartford | SeqHub AI Academy
description: Smith, C.R. (Co-Chair), Boykin, C.M. (Co-Chair), Monroe-White, T., Togun, T.A, & Tavarez, S. (2026, April 30-May 2). Decoding AI: Amplifier of Belonging or Architect of Inequity? [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI shapes how people are hired, developed, and heard at work, but are these technologies amplifying inclusion or quietly rewriting inequity into systems? This panel brings together academics and practitioners to “decode” how AI affects access, belonging, and trust in organizations. Panelists will examine the hidden assumptions within algorithms, discuss AI’s impacts on equity at work, and offer practical recommendations for creating inclusive and emancipatory systems. Attendees will leave with insights to help organizations align technological innovation with equitable culture.

title: Future-Proofing Selection Tools for Diverse Minds and Emerging AI (Session ID 649)
speakers: Ana Garcia | Viena Cao
speaker_affiliations: Cappfinity
description: Cao, V., Rowe, E.-L., Garcia, A., Mueller, S., Dovey, H., Deakin, P. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Future-Proofing Selection Tools for Diverse Minds and Emerging AI [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This intermediate-level session explores the dual challenge facing talent assessment today of ensuring fairness for neurodiverse candidates while maintaining assessment integrity in the age of GenAI. This tutorial explores how inclusive design and AI resilience can be addressed together, not in conflict. Featuring a real-world case study from Cappfinity, attendees will gain insights into reimagining traditional assessments such as situational judgement tests (SJTs) to be more secure, inclusive, and future-ready, without compromising psychometric rigor.

title: Generative AI Adoption: Competency Framework and Organizational Applications (Session ID 589)
speakers: Kate Bravery
speaker_affiliations: Mercer, A Marsh Business
description: Bravery, K., Srirangam, B., Agrwal, K., Golcha, S., Bailey R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Generative AI Adoption: Competency Framework and Organizational Applications [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Successful AI adoption requires not only technical skills but also behavioral and cognitive competencies. The tutorial will present the development and validation of an AI readiness framework measuring behavioral competencies (e.g. innovative mindset and openness to adopting new technologies), plus skills like proficiency in AI tools use, and cognitive abilities such as abstract reasoning. The tutorial will cover how organizations can adopt the Mercer AI Readiness Framework to support talent development and selection, fostering effective and equitable adoption of GenAI technologies.

title: Human-Centered Personas at Scale: Using LLMs and RAG to Build Personas Using Mixed Me (Session ID 1316)
speakers: Alice Choe | Diana Wolfe | Chelsea Homann
speaker_affiliations: University of Toronto | Avanade | Kyndryl | Open to Work
description: Wymer, C., Wolfe, D., & Choe, A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Human-Centered Personas at Scale: Building LLM-Based Personas Using Mixed Methods Data [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizations navigating disruption collect vast employee feedback, yet traditional qualitative methods struggle to scale. This advanced tutorial shows how large language models (LLMs) can be embedded in reproducible, mixed-method pipelines to transform employee voice into actionable personas. Participants will learn to integrate HRIS and text data via ETL and RAG workflows, apply grounded theory coding and clustering, and address ethical concerns through bias auditing and oversight. Materials and code will be shared to support adoption of scalable, transparent methods.

title: Interdisciplinary Collaboration Guided by a Case Study and Applied Exercise (Session ID1034)
speakers: Cristina Banks | Haley Cobb | Rebecca Brossoit | Ryszard Koziel
speaker_affiliations: University of California, Berkeley | Louisiana State University | Rice University | Saint Louis University School of Medicine
description: Thomas, C. L. (Chair). Banks, C. G., Cobb, H. R., Brossoit, R. Koziel, R. J. (2026). Interdisciplinary Collaboration Guided by a Case Study and Applied Exercise [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The landscape of the working world is increasingly complex. One way to adapt, make a positive impact, and thrive as a field is to embrace interdisciplinary approaches in work. This session uses a case study to provide researchers and practitioners an opportunity to actively learn about how to successfully implement interdisciplinary projects in organizations. Attendees will learn how to conduct interdisciplinary work by engaging in an exercise where audience members can compare their own interdisciplinary planning skills compared to a successful interdisciplinary case.

title: Leadership/Leader Development Posters
Moving Beyond Benchmarks: The Role of I-O Psychology Expertise in Total Rewards (Session ID 1271)
speakers: George Montgomery | Alec Munc | Nathan Bjornberg | Timothy Willis | Taylor Scotese
speaker_affiliations: Willis Towers Watson | Eli Lilly | Meta | Humana | WTW
description: Bjornberg, N., Montgomery, G., Munc, A., Myer, A., Scotese, T., Willis, T.. (2026). Moving Beyond Benchmarks: The Role of I-O Psychology Expertise in Total Rewards (Compensation and Benefits). Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will explore the significance of I-Os to the field of Total Rewards (TR). Panelists will share their applied and theoretical perspectives on best practices, challenges, and the unique positioning of I-Os in this field. Points will center around defining TR, the value I-Os can bring to these roles, the power of data-driven strategies, the impact of analytics technologies, and managing stakeholder resistance to change. The session aims to highlight the value of I-O psychology in enhancing TR strategies and opportunities for influence within this area.

title: On the Spectrum All My (Work) Life: Autistic Experiences Before and After Hire (Session ID 688)
tracks: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Cassidy Gaddie | Hudson Barthes | Mark Burnard | Jeffrey Olenick | Maira Ezerins | Sara Gutierrez | Tim Vogus

description: Barthes, H. (Co-Chair), Gaddie, C. (Co-Chair), Olenick, J. (Co-Chair), Burnard, M. (Panelist), Ezerins, M. (Panelist), Gutierrez, S. L. (Panelist), Vogus, T.J. (Panelist) (2026, April 30 – May 2). On the spectrum all my (work) life: Autistic experiences before and after hire [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session of academics and practitioners will present insights spanning autistic individuals’ employment experiences at critical stages of the work journey. Topics will include invisible disability disclosure, reactions to selection methods, the accommodation process, autistic burnout in the workplace, and how academic theories relate to lived experiences.

title: Pay Equity in Trump 2.0: Enforcement, Litigation, and Organizational Response (Session ID 539)
speakers: Lisa Harpe | Quenton Wright | Scott Kelly
speaker_affiliations: DCI Consulting Group, Inc. | Charles River Associates | Ogletree
description: Colosimo, J.L. (Co-Chair, Non-Speaking Contributor), Harpe, L. (Co-Chair), Kelly, T.S., Wright, Q. (2026). Pay Equity in Trump 2.0: Enforcement, Litigation, and Organizational Response. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores pay equity enforcement under the new administration, featuring insights from an economic consultant, labor and employment attorney, and I-O psychologist. Topics include federal rollbacks, state-level transparency laws, litigation trends, and organizational investments in audits and compliance. Panelists will share what employers are doing to manage employment-related risk. Attendees will gain practical strategies for navigating enforcement uncertainty and building defensible pay equity programs in 2026 and beyond.

title: Rethinking Generational Differences in the Age of AI (Session ID1086)
speakers: Cody Cox | Brian Ruggeberg | David Costanza | Haleema Sadia | Joel Nadler
speaker_affiliations: St. Mary's University | Spencer Stuart | University of Virginia | Northwell Health | AOA Consultants and University of Indiana
description: Cox, C. B. (Chair), Caputo, A., Costanza, D., Nadler, J., Ruggeberg, B., Sadia, H., Sherman, R. (2026). Rethinking Generational Differences in the Age of AI: Stereotypes, Change, and the Future of Work [Panel]. Society for Industrial-Organizational 2026 annual conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As AI transforms the workplace, it is also altering perspective on generational differences. This panel brings together researchers and practitioners for a conversation about how AI may reinforce or challenge generational stereotypes and explore new frameworks of workforce diversity in an AI-driven world. In this interactive session, attendees will be invited to contribute questions and observations and will gain a better understanding of both the scientific and practical perspectives, along with useful takeaways for addressing employee needs without relying on generational stereotypes.

title: SIOP Executive Board: SIOP and the Shifting Higher Edu Landscape: How Should the Profession Navigate? (Session ID 1444)
speakers: Dr. Christopher Wiese | Christopher Cunningham | Sophie Kay | Clair Reynolds Kueny | Richard Landers | Stephanie Payne

EB Session: SIOP & the shifting higher edu landscape – How should the profession navigate? [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session highlights challenges and opportunities currently facing I-O psychology programs, including a discussion on how SIOP as an institution, and its members, can help programs navigate the shifting higher education landscape. Experts representing SIOP leadership, doctoral programs, master's programs, and practitioners will briefly comment on the trends they see. Then, each expert will lead a roundtable discussion on identifying solutions and methods to help I-O programs ensure a sustainable future. Ideas will be shared in real-time and retained for future SIOP strategy development.

title: Scientist–Practitioner Perspectives on Impactful Org Belonging Research (Session ID 891)
speakers: Robert Bullock | Elizabeth Conjar | Laura Brooks Dueland | Natalie Perez | Salvatore Leone
speaker_affiliations: T-Mobile | Amazon | Inclusion Analytics, LLC
description: Bullock, R. (Chair), Leone, S. A., (Panelist), Perez, N., (Panelist), Brooks-Dueland, L., (Panelist), & Conjar, E. (Panelist). (2026). Scientist-Practitioner Perspectives on Applications for Belonging Research in Organizations: IGNITE + Panel Session Combo [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States This session convenes scientist–practitioners from T-Mobile, Amazon, and Inclusion-Analytics for an IGNITE-style chaired panel on progressing impactful belonging research in organizations. Presenters will outline practical approaches to designing, measuring, and communicating the business value of belonging across different contexts. After a series of planned discussion questions, attendees will have the opportunity to ask the panel questions on strategies or approaches for presenting the business case for progressing a culture of belonging.

title: Seeing and Being Seen: Expanding Award Access for Women and Racial Minorities (Session ID 949)
speakers: Ny Mia Tran | Angela Grotto | Julie Olson-Buchanan | Kisha Jones | Mateo Cruz, Ph.D. (he/him)
speaker_affiliations: Salem State University | Montclair State University | California State University, Fresno | Florida International University - College of Business | Bentley University
description: Gadles, M. (Chair), Cruz, M., Grotto, A. R., Jones, K. S., & Olson-Buchanan, J. B. (2026, April–May). Seeing and being seen: Expanding award access for women and racial minorities [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Awards are career resources that open doors to collaborators, funding, and visibility—yet women and racially/ethnically minoritized colleagues remain underrecognized, and recipients can face backlash or devaluation after a win. This session will share real stories of what helped and what didn’t, point out where the process can feel unfair, and offer practical tools, such as how to apply, how to ask for strong letters, and how to use recognition to grow your career.

title: Selling I-O Without Selling Out: A Vital Skill for All Practitioners (Session ID 1313)
speakers: Dr. William Shepherd | Dr. Emilee B. Tison | Justin Hess | Kim Macdonald | Ryan O'Leary | Ben Porr
speaker_affiliations: Elevation Leadership, LLC | Meta | Capital One | Tru-Stamp (in Stealth Mode) | PDRI | Harver
description: Shepherd, W. J. (Chair), Hess, J., Macdonald, K., O’Leary, R., Porr, B., & Tison, E. (2026). Selling I-O without Selling Out: A Vital Skill for All Practitioners. Panel. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychology delivers extraordinary value to organizations; however, its ROI can be unclear to organizational stakeholders. The panel will focus on the roles external and internal I-Os and salespeople play in communicating the value of I-O. Attendees will leave with concreate suggestions on how to “sell I-O” without selling out.

title: Setting a Cut Score Without Bleeding: Methods for Practitioners (Session ID1073)
speakers: Allie Wehling | Ashlie Plants | Kama Dodge | Morgan Tillery | Alexander Stemer
speaker_affiliations: Cisco | Merck | HackerRank | AT&T | Amazon
description: Dodge, K.D., Plants, A., Stemer, A., Tillery, M.G., & Wheling, A. (2026). Setting a cut score without bleeding: Methods for practitioners [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Use of expert judgment about minimal competence to distinguish between passing and failing performance is a bastion in I-O psychology. In the modern testing era, factors like expert time, test design, sample size, AI availability, and other factors influence how a test cut score is set and maintained over time. This fast-paced ignite session will feature speakers from internal and external I-O psychology roles who have set cut scores using a variety of methods. Though Angoff (1971) set the gold standard for cut score methodology, this practitioner focused session will feature methodologies for solution oriented professionals.

title: Skills, Career and Leadership Development: Perspectives and Approaches (Session ID1014)
speakers: Lauren Jeffery-Smith | Tiffany Shortridge, Ph.D. | Heather Ureksoy | Tyler Lewis
speaker_affiliations: Saville Assessment | WTW | Verizon | Northside Hospital

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Jeffery-Smith, L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Making skills development more powerful through personalization. Ureksoy, H., Quinn, J., Caldwell, C., & McDaniel, M. J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). From Principles to Practice: Linking Assessment Feedback to Continuous Development. Lewis, T. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Adopting Data-driven Development at Northside and Evaluating Outcomes. This symposium explores personalized approaches to skills, career, and leadership development. Presenters share research and case studies from Saville Assessment, Verizon, and Northside Hospital, highlighting how personality insights and data-driven strategies can enhance development outcomes. Topics include tailoring development approaches, scaling leadership programs, and evaluating feedback and the success of interventions.

title: The 2026 SIOP Machine Learning Competition (Session Id 1465)
speakers: Yizhen Egyn Zhu | Ivan Hernandez | Isaac Thompson
speaker_affiliations: Amazon | Virginia Tech
description: Hernandez, I., Thompson, I., Zhu, E. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The 2026 SIOP Machine Learning Competition. [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The SIOP Machine Learning Competition, since its 2018 inception, has become a hub for the I-O psychology community to showcase data science prowess. A diverse mix of data scientists, researchers, and students gather to tackle AI challenges. In a highlight session, top teams will exhibit their winning solutions and share insights from their competitive journey. This celebration of innovation and teamwork culminates in the recognition of their achievements, underscoring their contributions to the evolving field of AI and psychology.

title: The New Psychological Contract: From Loyalty to Learnability (Session ID 1057)
tracks: Organizational Processes/Development/Culture | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Jennifer Cullen | Kristen Williamson | Talya Bauer | Jordan Hartley | Caroline Deck

The new psychological contract – From loyalty to learnability [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores the evolving psychological contract between employers and employees, shifting from loyalty-based models to adaptability and skill development. Featuring leaders from Cisco, Visa, Dell, and Culture Amp, the session will unpack how organizations are redefining trust, performance, and retention in a skills-first world. Designed for an intermediate audience, the session blends research, practice, and humor to challenge outdated assumptions and offer actionable insights.

title: Training AI Skills in the Classroom: Embedding AI Skills in Graduate I-O Training (Session ID 578)

Training AI skills in the classroom: Embedding AI skills in graduate I-O training [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA., United States. This session explores how I-O psychology programs are developing AI-relevant competencies through curriculum design and classroom activities. Panelists will share strategies for teaching critical behaviors (e.g., evaluating AI outputs, applying domain knowledge, and upholding ethics) to better prepare graduate students for AI-integrated workplaces. Emphasis will be placed on philosophies, practical assignments, and aligning instructional goals with industry needs.

title: Where Can Bots Take Us Next? Novel Applications of Generative AI in Item Generation (Session ID 151)
speakers: Nicholas Baldwin | Ben Hawkes | Averie Linnell | Seren Ozkum
speaker_affiliations: DCS Corporation | The Hiring Science Studio | University of Nebraska at Omaha | Illinois State University
description: Baldwin, N. (Chair), and Hawkes, B. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Where can bots take us next? Novel applications of generative AI in item generation [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Baldwin, N. and Ngyuen, L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Can AI make us funnier? Using genAI to create humorous personality items. Linnell, A., Laverghetta, A., Luchini, S., Beaty R., and Reiter-Palmon, R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Advancing creativity tests with AI-generated vignettes for scalable use. Özkum, S. B., Budik, E. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Development and validation of an AI-based personality test. This symposium provides examples of new directions to take in the use of generative artificial intelligence (genAI) when creating scales and measurements. Three empirical studies will be detailed, including injecting humor into items, using genAI for different purposes and stages developing a measure of creativity, and theory-driven iterative procedures in personality item development. title: Where Did All of the SIOP Training Sessions Go? Is T and D Still Important? (Session ID 1227)

Panelists will answer questions regarding the importance of T&D research, changes, trends, and needs across science and practice, and challenges in conducting and sharing research in T&D at SIOP and beyond. title: Who’s Accountable in the Age of AI? Redefining Accountability and Leadership Identity (Session ID 259)

description: Pineault, L. A., Sandell, K. J., Garcia Marquez, C., Stern, R., & Gibbard, K. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Who’s Accountable in the Age of AI? Redefining Accountability and Leadership Identity [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel examines how AI adoption reshapes accountability in organizations, blurring responsibility once tied to leaders and teams. Panelists will explore evolving accountability structures, risks of overconfidence and shadow AI use, and shifts in leadership identity when authority is shared with algorithms. They will highlight both empirical research and applied case examples, showing how I-O psychologists can draw on their full toolkit to guide organizations in designing, sustaining, and legitimizing accountability in an AI-enabled world.

title: “The Past Is Prologue:” Studying Old Leadership Lessons Using New Behavioral Methods (Session ID 1087)
speakers: Marcus Dickson | Andrew Loignon | Nissa Gongora | Richard Guzzo

This symposium highlights research examining the causes and effects of user attitudes toward AI tools in organizations.

title: Advancing the Science and Practice of Situational Judgment Tests With AI (Session ID 978)
speakers: Harrison Kell | Michelle Martin-Raugh | Morgan Young | Emily McKissick | Jayne Allen | Patrick Rolwes | Yuxin Wang

description: Martin-Raugh, M. P. (Co-Chair) & Kell, H. J. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Advancing the Science and Practice of Situational Judgment Tests with AI. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Situational judgment tests are widely used to assess decision-making, interpersonal skills, and applied competencies, yet traditional development remains costly and resource intensive. This symposium presents 5 innovative studies highlighting how artificial intelligence can streamline test construction, supplement subject matter expertise, generate novel stimuli, and enable automated scoring. Collectively, the papers examine tradeoffs in efficiency, validity, and fairness, offering a view of the possible future of SJTs.

title: Award: Lessons From the "Firing Line": What Four Decades of Teaching Have Taught Me (Session ID 1463)
tracks: I-O Psychologist Career Development/Teaching | Special Session | Award Winner
speakers: Philip Podsakoff
speaker_affiliations: University of Florida
description: Podsakoff, P. M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Lessons From the "Firing Line": What Four Decades of Teaching Have Taught Me. [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Success in the classroom comes from lessons learned serving in leadership positions before the author became an academic and as a professor during the past 4 decades in the classroom. Although some of these lessons were learned the hard way through trial and error, many of them were the result of observing the effects that the author's behavior (and the behavior of others) has on students. In this session, they will discuss these lessons, provide examples of them, and explain how they have applied the principles learned from them to teaching students at all levels.

title: Award: Predicting Consequential Economic & Social Outcomes: A 50-Year Longitudinal Study (Session Id 1466)
tracks: Selection/Assessment/Individual Differences | Special Session | Award Winner
speakers: Camilla Benbow | David Lubinski
speaker_affiliations: Vanderbilt University
description: Global economies are conceptual economies—advanced by the quality of ideas. SMPY’s 50-year longitudinal study of thousands of intellectually prodigious participants (identified at age 12) shows how individual differences within the top 1% (one-third of the ability range) form contrasting constellations of specific abilities, interests, and values that predict consequential and creative outcomes in business, health care, the humanities, and STEM. The higher the abilities the more likely consequential outcomes emerge; interventions in adolescence provide a boost. In uncompensated ways, intellectually prodigious populations also contribute inordinately to the social good.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Six panelists from academia and practice challenge prevailing I-O psychology perspectives. This interactive session invites audience responses, aiming to spur debate by critically examining dominant viewpoints. Join the debate to reshape I-O's future trajectory.

title: Coaching Across Contexts: How I-O Psychologists Adapt as Coaches in Diverse Systems (Session ID 1289)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel brings together 5 I-O psychologists who are also professional coaches to explore how coaching practices adapt across organizational contexts and systems. Panelists will share case examples and frameworks illustrating how I-O-informed coaching enhances individual, team, and enterprise outcomes while maintaining ethical rigor. Attendees will gain practical insights into contextual coaching and systemic impact.

This COI explores how I-O psychologists can partner with communities (e.g., immigrants, farmworkers, unhoused individuals, people living with chronic illness, long-term HIV survivors) to design research and interventions for enhanced impact. In this session, authors will come together to consider ways to build stronger partnerships, with a focus on generating ideas to advance community equity, health, and well-being through collaborative fieldwork. This session will be participatory in nature, featuring meaningful discussions and opportunities for attendees to develop partnerships.

This research symposium explores how work design, organizational practices and environmental factors foster inclusive employment for neurodivergent employees and employees with disabilities. Collectively, the presenters provide a multilevel view of the contextual factors that influence stakeholder experiences with disability-inclusive employment. By combining theoretical and conceptual frameworks, qualitative and quantitative research, and applied perspectives, the symposium provides actionable insights for scholars, practitioners, policy makers, and organizational leaders.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Within graduate programs, there can be many challenges to well-being. This alternative session looks at some of the common challenges impairing well-being in graduate programs with the goal of examining how expertise as a field can be well-equipped to handle these challenges. Both current students and faculty will speak on some of the challenges they’ve faced and some of the potential solutions. This session will end with discussion focused on brainstorming and implementing solutions into graduate programs.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Numbers alone rarely spark organizational change; it requires stories. This session brings together experts to highlight best practices for turning analysis into impactful results that drive action. Topics will include what makes for a good a story, advice on presenting results, and what can be learnt from other fields in this context.

Tkach, T., & DiGirolamo, J. A. (2026). The coaching differentiator . Black, H. T., Ammons, G. M., DiGirolamo, J. A., Haider, S., & LaHuis, D. M. (2026). Validation of a competency model with an associated behavior measure . Showcasing I-O psychologists’ expanding process- and outcome-focused contributions to coaching research and theory building, this session highlights (a) development of a coach behavioral taxonomy through AI-based analyses of critical incidents, (b) potential synergies between a coach’s domain-specific expertise and traditional coaching approaches, and (c) a large-scale study of coaching’s impact linking client-rated coach competency assessments to a variety of outcomes.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In this high-energy IGNITE session with automatically advancing slides, practitioners will pitch the metaphors they use to explain job architecture to employees and business leaders. With a diverse slate of presenters from Lightcast, LinkedIn, Netflix, APTMetrics, and LSG Sky Chefs, and a wide range of metaphors from the yellow brick road, painkillers versus vitamins, pizza slices, shopping in grocery aisles, and selling the model home, authors know attendees will walk away with some new ideas for explaining the complex but foundational work I-O psychologists do in modern organizations. After the presentations, audience will be asked to pitch the best metaphors and wrap with a discussion of key takeaways.

title: Lead the World: Global Perspectives on Personality and Effective Leadership (Session ID 942)

Leading through social change: How big five traits predict leaders' strategic positioning and adaptability. Harms, P. D. (2026). What if we started taking personality seriously? Leadership is critical to successful organized human conduct. However, the characteristics that help one achieve leadership status do not always help one succeed as a leader. Thus, identifying individual differences that uniquely contribute to leadership expectations and outcomes across countries and contexts promises practical insights for organizations, leaders, and aspiring leaders. This symposium contributes to leadership literature by uncovering personality characteristics that shape leader expectations, effectiveness, firm risk taking, and strategic orientation in different contexts.

Together, they examine who thrives through personality, how education and experience shape leadership certification success, whether regional contexts shift competency expectations, and when and why stressors undermine safety performance. An expert discussant will integrate these findings for an intermediate audience, offering actionable implications for advancing research and strengthening the healthcare workforce for practitioners.

title: Learning Together: Lessons From the Work Futures Initiative’s Sandbox Conversations (Session ID 1106)

Work Futures Initiative: Lessons Learned from the Sandboxes Discussions [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology 2026 Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. With the increasing complexity of the modern workplace, there is a strong need for interdisciplinary approaches to organizations. To do this, I-Os must first develop skills in interdisciplinary learning. In this session, authors provide research in the Work Futures Initiative as an example of how this can look and give space to collectively practice collaborative learning together. Lessons learned from 16 Sandbox discussions with professionals from diverse backgrounds will be shared and attendees are invited to try out a mini-Sandbox themselves.

(Facilitator). (2026). More Than Salary: Snapshot & Historical Trends in SIOP Members’ Income and Employment [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The Income and Employment Survey is one of the most valuable benefits to SIOP members. It provides snapshot estimates and historical trends of the employment situations of SIOP members, giving insight into shifting economic and employment contexts. This alternative session will include a brief presentation of 2026 results, including new special topics (e.g., RTO, AI, workforce management). Participants in the session will break into groups to discuss/request additional analyses/reports and shape future survey content. Insights from a salary negotiation expert will also be shared.

Moving beyond pigeonholes and taking flight: Advances in autism at work research [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Although organizations have launched dedicated programs to support autistic employees, there are still pigeonholes that not only constrain autistic employees’ growth but degrade their work experience and well-being. This IGNITE! session leverages novel research to confront the typecasts, stereotypes, stigmas, neurotypical norms, and barriers faced by autistic employees in organizations. Presenters illuminate opportunities for organizational change in work design, communication practices, talent acquisition, and leadership that enable autistic employees—and their organizations—to thrive.

title: Occupational Health/Work and Family Posters
Partner Showcase: Valence - Beyond Adoption Metrics: ADI's Power User Index for Measuring AI Coaching Effectiveness
speakers: Jennifer Carpenter | Ellie Wildman
speaker_affiliations: Valence
description: Organizations evaluating AI coaching typically measure adoption: logins, session counts, satisfaction. But does usage alone predict impact? This session presents findings from a study of 45,000+ AI coaching sessions at Analog Devices, a global semiconductor company with 26,000 employees across 30+ languages. Researchers developed a Power User Index measuring frequency, breadth of use cases, and conversation quality. Key findings: power users were 28% more likely to move up a performance band; manager usage doubled team adoption; and women were overrepresented among power users by 68%. Jennifer Carpenter (VP Global Head of Talent, ADI) and Ellie Wildman (Valence) will share the methodology and implications.

title: Proactive People Analytics Solutions: Addressing Tomorrow’s Problems Today (Session Id 844)
speakers: Aniqa Hatem | Colby Nesbitt | Danilo Le Sante | Yael Oelbaum | Zachary Rosch
speaker_affiliations: JetBlue Airways | Netflix | Google | JetBlue | The Farmer's Dog

Proactive People Analytics: Proactive People Analytics Solutions: Addressing Tomorrow’s Problems Today [IGNITE] Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As organizations face pressure to do more with less, people analytics must evolve from reactive reporting to proactive problem solving. This IGNITE session showcases how practitioners leverage problem construction skills and predictive analytics to anticipate workforce needs before they arise. Through specific, organizational use cases, authors demonstrate how proactive, user-centered analytics products improve talent acquisition, employee development, and succession planning—delivering impact before problems arise.

title: Recruiting Research and Analytics: Tales From the Field (Session ID 519)
speakers: Nathan Mondragon
speaker_affiliations: ProboTalent

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA United States. The goals of the session are to (a) share insightful applied research conducted across the recruiting and analytics space and (b) highlight different ways recruiting-focused research teams are structured and approach work within organizations, with special emphasis on contributions outside of traditional interview and assessment development areas. Each presenter will share a research endeavor that informs new directions for recruiting or selection practices within their organizations and will also describe their teams, the projects they take on, and how they use their research to drive action.

title: Religious Inclusion in the Workplace: Mega Threats, Accommodations, and ERGs (Session ID 853)
speakers: Annie Kato | Helen Chung | Denise Daniels | Humaira Hatif | Josh Nagel
speaker_affiliations: Seattle Pacific University | Wheaton College | George Mason University | New York State Unified Court System
description: Kato, A. E., (Co-Chair), Chung, H. H. (Co-Chair), & Daniels, D. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Religious inclusion in the workplace: Mega-threats, accommodations, & ERGs [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Despite growing employee interest in expressing religious identities at work, religion remains an understudied aspect of workplace diversity. This symposium features 3 papers that contribute to the understanding religious employees’ experiences, including the impact of mega-threats to religious identity, coworker reactions to religious accommodations, and the effects of religious employee resource groups. Collectively, this research offers new insights into how organizations can support religious employees and foster an inclusive environment for a religiously diverse workforce.

title: Righting the Ship: Reorienting Publishing Toward Solving Important Issues (Session ID 675)
speakers: Ryan Grant | Spencer Garcia | Charles Calderwood | Cort Rudolph | Fadel Matta | Lillian Eby | Steven Rogelberg
speaker_affiliations: University of North Carolina at Charlotte | Georgia Institute of Technology | Virginia Tech | Wayne State University | University of Georgia
description: Grant, R. (co-chair), Garcia, S. C. (co-chair), Calderwood, C., Eby, L., Matta, F. K., Rogelberg, S. G. & Rudolph, C. (2026). Righting the ship: Reorienting publishing toward solving important issues [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel addresses ongoing concerns about the state of theory in the field—specifically, the lack of theory aimed at solving real-world problems (Tihanyi, 2020). Authors unpack why theory building often prioritizes novelty and “theory for theory’s sake,” examine factors fueling these trends, and discuss how this limits the field’s practical value. Panelists will outline concrete steps editors and reviewers can take to shift incentives toward theories that both advance science and address pressing organizational challenges.

title: SIOP Special Session: Revenge at Work: The Psychology of Getting Even (Session ID 1447)
speakers: Nathan Bowling | P. Jewel Morrison | Thomas Tripp | Xin Wei Ong | Ashlyn Kurtz
speaker_affiliations: University of Central Florida | New College of Florida | Washington State University | The University of Akron | Clemson University
description: Morrison, P. J. (Co-Chair), Ong, X. W. (Co-Chair), Bowling, N. A., Kurtz, A., & Tripp, T. M. (2026). Revenge at Work: The Psychology of Getting Even [Invited Alternative Presentation]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference. New Orleans, LA, United States. Revenge at work doesn’t always look like a dramatic blow-up. It is distinct from workplace aggression as it involves action to restore justice to another party (Bies & Tripp, 2005). In today’s organizations, it often hides in the shadows: quiet quitting, malicious compliance, withholding information, or subtle sabotage towards the company or coworkers (Alter, 2015; Mahand & Caldwell, 2023). This rotating roundtable session explores how employees attempt to restore justice through retaliatory behaviors—and what that reveals about the “failure of workplace systems to offer meaningful repair."

title: Supporting Targets of Sexual Harassment in the Workplace (Session ID 590)

speaker_affiliations: George Mason University | University of Connecticut | University of Texas at Arlington | Women in Work Lab, IU Indianapolis
description: Stockdale, J. (Co-Chair) & Liang Y. (Co-Chair), Magley, V. J. (Discussant). (2026). Supporting targets of sexual harassment in the workplace [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United State Chaubey, H., Stockdale, M. S., Kiebler, J., Kimble-Hill, A.C. (2026). Enhancing sexual harassment training outcomes through empathy training: A comparison of modalities Korsak, M. A., Bernard, L., & Martinez, L. R. (2026). A trauma-informed approach to managing sexual harassment incidents at work Liang, Y., Sun, T., McCauley, R., & Zhu, Z. (2026). Access to collective representation, customer sexual harassment and gig work Sexual harassment in the workplace has detrimental effects on targets’ well-being and work outcomes, yet little research has examined how to mitigate these impacts when harassment occurs. This symposium aims to advance scholarly understanding of how to better support sexual harassment targets. Empirical and conceptual papers highlight different approaches—training programs, organizational trauma-informed approaches, and collective representation—that can provide critical support for targets across various work settings.

title: Telling Your Story: Translating and Communicating I-O Skills (Session ID 958)
speakers: Chanda Murphy | Courtney Keim | Ashley Nixon | David Earnest | Julianne Brown-Meola | Adriane Sanders
speaker_affiliations: Mindful in Memphis | Bellarmine University | Willamette University | Austin Peay State University | Bath & Body Works
description: Keim, A.C. (Co-Chair), Murphy, C. (Co-Chair), Anderson, Z., Brown, J., Earnest, D.R., Nixon, A.E., & Sanders, A.M.F. (2026). Telling your story: Translating & Communicating I-O Skills [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychology professionals and students must overcome a translation barrier between the technical language of SIOP competencies (e.g., psychometrics, job analysis) and the NACE career readiness competencies (e.g., communication, leadership) often used by employers. This panel of academics and practitioners will engage in a lively discussion on how to translate I-O expertise to bridge the gap, ensuring graduates and early career folks are marketable, able to advance through generative AI screening processes, and recognized for their valuable talent.

title: The AI Scorecard 2.0: AI’s Potential Beyond Efficiency (Session ID 977)
speakers: Katherine Gibbard | Andrew Evans | Caitlynn Sendra | Gabriela Burlacu | Lindsay Horst | Dr. Neil Morelli
speaker_affiliations: SAP SuccessFactors | Upwork | Plexus Corp. | Salesforce
description: Gibbard, K. (Chair), Sendra, C., Horst, L., Morelli, N., Burlacu, G., Evans, A. (2026). The AI Scorecard 2.0: AI’s potential beyond Efficiency [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI adoption is accelerating across organizations, however business gains are often underwhelming and only measured as time savings. This narrow measuring of AI impact only in terms of efficiency risks overlooking critical dimensions of organizational health, employee experience, and people sustainability. In this session, applied researchers and practitioners will present practice-based insights into how AI is shaping the workplace and discuss why organizations must measure the people impacts of AI solutions to capture business gains, and I-O psychologists can lead this charge.

title: “So, Are You a Workplace Therapist?”: Marketing I-O to Organizations (Session ID 815)

I-O psychology plays a vital role in improving workplace outcomes, yet the field remains underrecognized in much of the business world. This alternative session explores how I-O practitioners can better communicate the value of the field to organizational leaders. This session highlights real-world strategies, challenges, and lessons learned in marketing I-O within organizations. The session will provide practical guidance for both practitioners and students seeking to increase the visibility and influence of I-O psychology in their work contexts.

This career showcase session highlights how I-O drives performance in high-stakes space operations and invites you to rethink where your expertise can take you.

In addition to providing value for fellow internal practitioners, this session will also be insightful for those employed by external assessment vendors.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel offers critical insights into the evolving legal landscape of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) programs after Executive Order 14173, which emphasizes merit and seeks to eliminate unlawful DEI practices. Panelists will share strategies for evaluating DEI programs through a multidisciplinary lens. I-Os need to understand best practices for qualitative policy reviews that align with current law, quantitative audit techniques that evaluate the implementation of written policies through workforce analytics, and guidance for cultivating a legally defensible DEI culture.

Coaching as a tool for thriving: Unlocking growth, resilience, and well-being at work. Session submitted to the 2026 SIOP Conference, New Orleans, LA. Employees today must adapt to extraordinary volatility, often pushed to their cognitive, emotional, and physical limits. Surviving in a chronically hypervigilant, activated, or exhausted state is unsustainable for both people and organizations. One high-impact tool for shifting from surviving to thriving is coaching. In this session, academics and practitioners who have studied and practiced coaching and thriving for combined 70+ years convene. This session will illuminate the impact that coaching has for individuals and organizations for promoting thriving, well-being, and also performance.

However, limited empirical guidance is available to confidently select support strategies for these employees. This panel will highlight insights into the effective and ineffective sources of support for employees with disabilities. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This intermediate/advanced session addresses unprecedented organizational transformation—companies now implement 9+ major changes annually versus 2 pre-2020—whereas traditional approaches yield disappointing results. Participants explore a framework positioning agility and resilience as distinct yet interdependent capacities essential for thriving amid perpetual change. Moving beyond episodic models, presenters define core constructs, outline a conceptual model, and discuss tools for application. Participants actively apply the framework to real-world scenarios and share insights.

title: Partner Showcase: CodeSignal - Amplifying I-O with AI: Scaling Simulation Best Practices
speakers: Seterra Riggs | Nathan Hundley
speaker_affiliations: CodeSignal
description: Work simulations are among the strongest predictors of job performance, yet for non-technical roles they have historically been difficult to scale. Unlike coding simulations, which can be delivered and scored automatically, non-technical simulations often rely on assessment center methods such as roleplays, behavioral interviews, written exercises, and human raters. These approaches are job-relevant and effective, but also high-touch, labor-intensive, and typically reserved for executive or other high-value hiring contexts. This presentation explores how AI can serve as a force multiplier for industrial-organizational psychologists, scaling assessment best-practices without abandoning rigor that underpins them. Working in lockstep with I-O expertise, AI agents can be trained as role players, structured interviewers, and rubric-based graders using the same principles I-O practitioners have long applied to human assessors. We will discuss how AI, guided by I-O science, can support realistic, standardized, and scalable simulations for non-technical roles, and share emerging research from CodeSignal’s AI simulation products.

description: Morrison , P. J. (Co-Chair), Vaidya, H. (Co-Chair), DeKay, N., Kurtz, A., Silverman, A. (2026). Well-Being Fatigue: When Employees Stop Believing the Company Cares [Invited Roundtable]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference. New Orleans, LA, United States. Employee burnout has long been a focus of organizational research (Maslach et al., 2001). Well-being programs have been proposed to combat burnout, but employees now report fatigue from both work and wellness (Hart et al., 2018; Beames et al., 2023). Indeed, Gallup shows engagement at a 10-year low despite interventions (Harter, 2025). This session explores well-being fatigue (i.e., the distrust in wellness efforts that overlook workload, equity, and leadership) and introduces terms like carewashing and quiet cracking. Attendees will learn strategies to restore trust and tackle root causes.

title: Say NO to Overwork and YES to I-O Career Sustainability! An Overdue Discussion (Session ID 979)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychologists improve the work life of countless others, but how are they doing in their own lives? Most have experienced pressures to work excessively or impression-manage how busy and stressed they are to keep up with the crowd. This session is about self-reflection and personal resistance to norms of overwork and stress as a badge of honor. Four experts with a passion for confronting overwork culture in their research and practice will facilitate small group discussion about how to say "no" to overwork and "yes" to sustainable careers for themselves and the workers they care about.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Skills-based approaches to talent management have taken hold in the past several years, with companies reorienting how they hire, develop, and place key talent. However, for decades, I-Os have viewed skills as one part of a complement of capabilities required for successful performance alongside requisite knowledge, abilities, and behaviors. I-O expert practitioners will discuss whether skills are sufficient for driving high impact talent management in organizations or if traditional KSAO view needs to be reasserted in organizations to maintain core talent practice integrity.

As organizations expand their use of digital information, from cybervetting job candidates’ social media to monitoring employees through people analytics, questions of fairness, ethics, and equity are increasingly urgent. This Community of Interest will explore the boundaries of digital data use, focusing on individual rights, organizational responsibilities, and best practices. Participants will discuss where transparency ends and intrusion begins, how to minimize bias and misuse, and what policies and practices can sustain organizational effectiveness and trust.

Diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives have recently incurred outsized backlash, with some cautioning that such initiatives may result in unintended consequences both for dominant groups who may feel excluded by them and for minoritized groups to whom they may send unwanted signals. This symposium includes 4 papers that seek to determine if, when, and for whom DEI initiatives backfire and the potential downstream implications. The goal is to better inform organizations about the potential costs and benefits of DEI initiatives.

Companies invest in people analytics, yet insights often fail to drive action. By combining technical skills, business acumen, storytelling and executive communication, internal consultants, often named People Analytics Business Partners (PABPs), bridge the gap between technical teams, HRBPs, and leaders to drive real impact. A cross-industry panel from athenahealth, Disney, HP, Pure Storage, and Coca-Cola will provide practical information such as competencies necessary for the job, success stories, and lessons learned as well as interactive discussion with the audience.

Agentic AI moves beyond single LLMs to goal-directed systems that plan, use tools, and act with bounded autonomy. Despite their popularity, guidance on key design and implementation decisions is scarce. This master tutorial presents a cross-industry framework uniting AI, software development, and I-O best practices for high-stakes workflows. We’ll demo an Agentic AI tool built with this framework and help attendees leave with a use case for their own work, tying design choices to psychometric evidence and implementation guardrails.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The purpose of this introductory tutorial is to highlight the important role that replication studies play in advancing I-O psychology research and offer advice on the best practices for choosing studies to replicate, designing replication studies, and publishing them. Replication studies are those that test the same hypotheses in a previously published article largely following the same methods, but with new data and may include a few constructive modifications. The tutorial will provide participants with a “checklist” on best practices for designing and publishing replication studies.

title: GenAI and the Death (Or Rebirth) of Management (Session ID 339)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA., United States. Authors prognosticate how the impetus of generative AI will first threaten, then dismantle, and ultimately remake the manager’s role. They discuss implications of forces such as multimodal AI, agents, and context engineering on human-AI teaming, performance evaluation, and a new form of systems thinking overseen by a reconstituted manager profile. They present previously unshared (even to each other!) predictions for GenAI's impact on management over 3, 5, 10, and 25-year time horizons and debate wildly contrasting views stated with unflinching, unwarranted confidence.

This panel examines how people leaders are rethinking data use, skill priorities, and motivation while adapting hiring, mobility, and development amid AI, evolving skill requirements, and economic volatility. By sharing candid lessons and linking them to concepts like unlearning and bounded rationality, panelists will show how to question outdated tools, avoid missteps, and make evidence-based, defensible talent decisions under real-world constraints.

title: Is ROI Dead? Debating the Value of Its Impact in Outcome Measurement (Session ID 1165)

(Non-Speaking Contributor). (2026, April). Is ROI Dead? Debating the Value of Economic Impact in Outcome Measurement [Debate]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Return on investment (ROI) has long been the business standard for judging the impact of leadership and talent initiatives. Critics argue ROI oversimplifies complex outcomes—such as trust, equity, and team effectiveness—that organizations increasingly prioritize. Supporters contend ROI remains the only universal denominator, because every outcome ultimately shapes costs and benefits. This debate will stage both perspectives, invite audience challenges, and explore whether the future of measurement lies in refining ROI or moving beyond it.

title: Navigating Headwinds to AI in Assessment (Session ID 328)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools are quickly expanding in workplace and educational settings, including their application in assessment. Although AI can enable efficiencies, scalability, and innovative approaches, users encounter challenges due to regulations and laws, the need for human oversight and governance, and complexity in evaluating the effectiveness and risks associated with AI tools. This session explores considerations for effectively navigating these challenges, drawing from legal precedent, guidelines, and examples in talent assessment and credentialing.

title: Partner Showcase: Valence - From Chatbot to Coach: Designing Systems That Actually Develop People
speakers: Elizabeth Ritterbush | Beth Shipman | Julia Walsh | Ellie Wildman | Rob Lewis
speaker_affiliations: The Home Depot | Corning Incorporated | General Mills | Valence | ADP
description: The rapid proliferation of large language models has created a misleading assumption: that any AI system capable of conversation is capable of coaching. But I-O psychologists know that effective coaching requires context on organizational culture, individual goals, team dynamics, performance frameworks, and continuity across interactions. This panel brings together I-O leaders from Fortune 500 companies who are actively deploying AI coaching in their organizations. Panelists will explore what distinguishes a purpose-built AI coaching system from general-purpose conversational AI and the organizational design implications of scalable coaching. Whether your organization is evaluating AI coaching vendors or building internal capabilities, this session offers a practitioner-grounded framework for asking the right questions.

Automation of some of the tasks that are currently the bread and butter of I-O careers will necessitate changes both in how I-Os train the next generation and how they retrain themselves. Participants will learn when and how to apply each method, as well as the critical assumptions and potential pitfalls associated with each.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Although I-O psychologists typically develop tests that others administer, many practitioners, especially those who partner with public safety agencies, wear both hats by administering the assessments they design. Understanding test administration realities helps assessment developers maximize the effectiveness of their tests. Panelists with experience developing and administering assessments will share strategies for maintaining objectivity, ensuring standardization, and responding resiliently to unexpected events while preserving test integrity—lessons applicable across all assessment contexts.

This session aims to inspire organizations to view internships as strategic tools for building stronger, more agile teams.

Invisible labor—tasks like mentoring, onboarding, and emotional support—sustains workplace performance yet often falls disproportionately on certain employees. Attendees will gain actionable insights to strengthen their programs and maximize organizational impact.

The discussion moves beyond typical panel presentations to explore how these leaders actually work together daily. Through candid dialogue and real stories from Louisiana's largest health system, they examine partnership models, trust-building in the C-suite, data-driven decision making, and more, offering practical insights grounded in both research and lived experience.

Through an Open Fishbowl structure, attendees will connect real-world examples with research on meanings work, callings, and occupational health.

Panelists will share applied research, organizational practices, and emerging approaches, to highlight strategies for balancing innovation, scientific rigor, and candidate experience in a rapidly evolving landscape.

Adding unique depth, a representative from SOHP’s pre-conference forum will deliver fresh takeaways, ensuring a lively, future-focused discussion that bridges research, practice, and action in today’s rapidly changing world of work.

Coachability—the willingness to accept feedback, reflect, and adapt—is a key predictor of leadership success, yet it is inconsistently measured across assessment methods. This session brings together researchers and practitioners to explore how coachability appears in simulations, psychometrics, and interviews, and what signals reveal a leader’s learning capacity. Panelists will share evidence and practical strategies to assess and develop coachability as a foundation for leadership adaptability and growth.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming learning and development at every level of organizations—personalizing learning, scaling leadership programs, and enhancing coaching. Yet, paradoxically, executive development remains largely untouched, still centered on high-cost, high-touch human interventions. This panel explores whether “high touch” must always mean “human touch,” and how AI can democratize, scale, and reimagine executive learning. Panelists will discuss key use cases and debate the opportunities and risks of applying AI at the top of organizations.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Much of the conversation around AI in organizations emphasizes its promise and potential, but real-world implementation often reveals serious pitfalls. From hallucinations and bias blind spots to privacy concerns and overreliance on tools, failures can undermine trust, fairness, and outcomes. This Community of Interest session invites participants to candidly share “AI gone wrong” experiences and surface lessons learned. Through discussion, participants will gain insights into recurring challenges and sharpen their awareness of how to navigate AI’s risks responsibly.

title: Demystifying Inclusion: Everyday Inclusion for the Masses (Session ID 1343)

Inclusion is widely valued but often misunderstood. Many employees want to contribute to inclusive workplaces yet lack clear, practical guidance. This panel, Demystifying Inclusion, brings together practitioners, scholars, and consultants to clarify what inclusion truly means, strip away its politicized framing, and highlight actionable behaviors that foster respect, fairness, and belonging. Through research and real-world examples, panelists will illuminate simple, scalable paths to inclusion.
