With the rapid advances in Generative AI (GenAI) capabilities over the last few years, job applicants are using widely available tools like ChatGPT to write resumes and cover letters, and to prepare for interviews. Some also attempt to outsource the assessment completion process to GenAI. This session brings together global perspectives on detecting the use of GenAI to cheat on assessments, as well as dealing with the consequences. Panelists from several multinational organizations will engage in a lively discussion on how to detect and cope with GenAI-facilitated cheating.

In an era of generative AI and global disruption, the psychological contract between employers and employees is being rewritten. This panel explores how organizations can evolve from static models to dynamic systems that emphasize employability, adaptability, and trust. Panelists will share research and practice insights on sustaining fairness, meaning, and human value amid technological transformation and shifting workforce expectations.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping employee listening by making surveys smarter, feedback more conversational, insights faster, and action more sustainable. From survey design and data collection to analysis and action planning, AI offers new tools to capture richer feedback and drive meaningful change. When paired with behavioral science best practices ensuring rigor, transparency, and human judgment, AI can amplify employee voice, close the gap between insight and action, and transform the employee experience.

Graduate students often experience various forms of remoteness during their training (e.g., distance from advisor, lab, or program). Yet, there is limited guidance on how students can effectively navigate remoteness. In this session, panelists will discuss their personal experiences with remoteness during graduate training, and offer actionable strategies for how to cope with this common but overlooked element of the student experience. Small group discussions with the audience will follow, where participants will collectively develop a comprehensive list of strategies.

This panel brings together assessment owners who have implemented security solutions across different organizational contexts and industries. Panelists will share real-world cases of successful implementation strategies, pitfalls to avoid, and emerging trends in assessment security.

title: Wait What!? Disparate Impact Theory and the Current Legal and Regulatory Landscape (Session ID 1278)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Disparate impact theory has been embedded in the legal and regulatory landscape for over 50 years. This legal doctrine has been upheld by court rulings at all levels and has broadened in application over the years. However, disparate impact theory as a guiding civil rights principal is being threatened by recent Supreme Court rulings, executive orders, employer confusion about “illegal DEI,” and other forms of pressure. Panelists will present the history of this theory, recent changes in the legal and regulatory landscape, effects on employers, and outcomes that are evolving as a result.

Leadership development (LD) is a multi-billion-dollar industry, yet programs often fail to deliver sustained impact. Leadership science has identified the traits and behaviors linked to effectiveness, but these insights are often lost before or during translation into practice. At the same time, research frequently overlooks the realities leaders face. This panel examines the systemic science–practice gap in leadership: why evidence is underutilized, where research lacks relevance, and how collaboration can bridge the divide between scholarship and LD practice.

Chronic Illness at Work: Driving Culture, Practices, and Change [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States This panel spotlights chronic illness as a widespread yet overlooked workplace challenge, reshaping how organizations think about inclusion, well-being, and performance. It brings together experts bridging research and practice to explore how chronic physical, mental, and neurodevelopmental conditions affect work experiences, culture, and outcomes. Through a solution-focused dialogue, panelists will share evidence-based strategies to create supportive policies, inclusive work design, and drive organizational change that allows chronically ill employees to thrive at work.

title: Designing Meaningful People Science and Analytics Internships: A 360° Perspective (Session ID 422)
speakers: Averie Linnell | Lauren Moran | Meredith Coats | Philip Simmons | Roni Reiter-Palmon | Salvatore Leone

Designing Meaningful People Science and Analytics Internships: A 360° Perspective [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Internships have long been central to graduate education. Yet as people science and analytics roles rapidly emerge within organizations, there is limited guidance on how to structure new internships. This panel bridges the academic–practitioner gap by bringing together key stakeholders from organizations and I-O programs to discuss meaningful internship programs. Authors provide a practical overview of how to advocate for internships, design them to be beneficial for all, and extend their benefits beyond a project. Attendees will leave with guidance from designing to evaluating internship programs.

Education, experience, and… hobbies? Hobbies’ role in workplace research and practice [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Balancing work and personal life is becoming increasingly challenging in today’s rapidly evolving work environment. As employees seek fulfillment beyond their jobs, hobbies play an important role in promoting well-being, creativity, and connection. This session explores the intersection of hobbies and work by categorizing various types of hobbies, assessing their relevance in the modern workplace, and discussing the benefits and potential drawbacks of these hobbies for both individuals and organizations.

Implicit voice theories (IVTs) expanded: Development and validation of lateral IVTs. Despite the importance of employee voice behavior for promoting innovation and solving organizational problems, research on voice has largely focused on micro-level predictors and outcomes related to the frequency of voice. This symposium presents novel perspectives on voice behavior by exploring how factors related to the voiced message influence the perception and outcomes of voice events, examining the macro-level predictor of industry uncertainty, and expanding understanding of lateral voice behavior.

Wait, Am I Actually Making It? A Discussion on Imposter Syndrome [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will provide multifaceted perspectives on imposter syndrome from I-O professionals across diverse career trajectories, offering research-based insights and practical tools for recognition and management. Panelists will share evidence-based strategies alongside personal experiences, discussing how to identify warning signs, implement coping mechanisms, and create organizational cultures that mitigate feelings of professional inadequacy. The discussion will address manifestations at different career stages while exploring systemic factors.

From Access to Advancement: Cultivating Career Pathways for Neurodivergent Employees. [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizations are beginning to recognize the strengths neurodivergent employees bring, yet most efforts stop at providing accommodations. The next challenge is talent development: creating pathways for growth, advancement, and long-term career success. This panel will explore how I-O psychology can shape strategies that move beyond access to focus on cultivating skills, preparing leaders, and fostering career progression for neurodivergent professionals. Attendees will gain practical insights on how organizations can better develop and retain this critical talent.

title: From Failed to Scaled: Crossing the GenAI Divide for a Successful Launch (Session ID1065)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. An MIT study reveals that 95% of Generative AI pilots fail. This panel brings together I-O psychologists who have successfully implemented GenAI solutions at Amazon, Takeda, and Humancore to share how they crossed "the GenAI Divide." Through discussion of research and piloting phases, panelists will provide practical strategies for identifying success metrics, managing organizational friction, and building learning-capable systems. Attendees will learn to avoid pitfalls and implement GenAI solutions that deliver value in areas like hiring, performance management, and employee development.

title: Going Public: Using Social Media to Elevate the Science of Work (Session ID 128)

Going Public: Using Social Media to Elevate the Science of Work [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychologists are urged to embrace social media as powerful vehicles for amplifying the science, translating complex research into accessible insights, and shaping public narratives about the workplace. This panel explores how professionals can ethically and effectively use platforms like LinkedIn and TikTok to expand impact, build community, and legitimize I-O psychology. Attendees will gain strategies, resources, and a clear action plan to elevate their voice and the discipline online.

M. (Presenter). (2026). High impact skills you don’t learn in grad school (But every I-O should know) [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Most I-O professionals ultimately pursue nonacademic careers, yet graduate students often have limited exposure to the skills and activities essential for success in applied roles. At the same time, there are valuable competencies that academics themselves could benefit from. In this IGNITE session, presenters will highlight key skills missing from current I-O curricula. A diverse group of professionals—both within and beyond academia—will offer practical insights on topics like influence, networking, project management, file organization, and stakeholder management.

title: Inside High-Performing Teams: Applied Methods to Measure and Build Effectiveness (Session ID 728)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session reviews different facets of team effectiveness that practitioners have implemented into their team sessions. Organizations have developed various models and have different strategies on measuring team effectiveness. Authors will highlight how different organizations approach team effectiveness, each being successful in their own way. Together, this group of panelists illustrate several unique and critical facets of team effectiveness.

title: Job Architecture in Practice: Internal Practitioners Tell All (Session ID 239)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel provides an internal practitioner perspective on job architecture projects, highlighting challenges and the crucial role of I-O psychologists. Diverse panelists from companies like Netflix, LinkedIn, J.B. Hunt, and Kenvue as well as those with both internal and external consulting experience (e.g., Korn Ferry, APTMetrics) will share insights. The session aims to share a view from the inside -- both why job architecture projects do not always follow best practices and what the panelists have learned along the way.

title: Job Insecurity in the Age of Automation: Individual, Team, and Global Perspectives (Session ID 342)
speakers: Andrea Bazzoli | Morgan Zipfel | Paisley Shultz | Hannah A. Crandell | Minseo Choi

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The rapid deployment of AI, robotics, and algorithmic systems has led to technology-led workforce reductions, reshaping not only headcount, but also how individuals view their futures, how teams function under pressure, and how global narratives on technology evolve. This symposium unites individual, team, and global lenses to explore workers’ experiences of job insecurity and technological change, highlighting evidence-based, theory-informed pathways for responsible deployment, targeted reskilling, and healthier, more ethical organizations in the age of automation.

Practitioners face growing challenges in generating and maintaining assessment validity evidence amid rising threats from plagiarism, AI misuse, limited performance criteria, and reduced SME availability. This panel explores strategies for navigating these constraints while maintaining rigor and integrity. Panelists will share what’s worked, what hasn’t, and how they manage the tension between real-world limitations and best practices. Attendees will gain actionable ideas for adapting validation in data-limited contexts.

title: Synthetic Personality: Conceptual and Research Encounters (Session ID 543)
speakers: Georgi Yankov | Anoop Javalagi | Bradley Pitcher | Pengda Wang
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessments | HumRRO | APTMetrics | Rice University
description: Yankov, G. P. (Chair) (2026). Synthetic personality: Conceptual and research encounters [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This symposium investigates whether large language models can emulate human-like personalities consistent with the 5 factor model. It showcases 4 studies examining the stability of LLM-based decision policies, the effects of personality manipulation in workplace negotiations, the validity of LLM-generated personality ratings compared to self-reports, and the fidelity of simulated profiles such as those of U.S. presidents. Findings suggest LLMs can exhibit stable, theoretically coherent, and psychometrically valid “synthetic personalities.”

This intermediate-level symposium explores how human–animal interactions (HAIs) shape employee well-being, motivation, and meaning at work. Across diverse contexts—from pet ownership and canine-assisted teams to animal-inclusive policies and slaughterhouse labor—the 4 papers illustrate both the enriching and distressing aspects of HAIs in work life. Together, they highlight the expanding relevance of animals in organizational science and occupational health psychology.

These experiences range from evaluating creative ideas based on domain expertise, utilizing perspective taking of adversaries, and the unique insight that stems from being a minority member. Further, strategies and insights that can empower organizations to embrace innovation and thrive in today's competitive landscape will be discussed.

This panel discussion explores the opportunities and challenges of leveraging non-HR data, defined as data existing outside traditional HR information systems, to enhance organizational effectiveness and business outcomes. Four practitioners will present diverse use cases demonstrating how non-HR data sources can help transform people analytics from a reactive service to a proactive strategic function. The presenters will address critical questions about data regulation, ethics, privacy, and the methodological challenges of connecting data sources housed by different business units.

title: The Impact of AI on I-O and Assessment: Futureproofing Our Skills and Identity (Session ID 127)
speakers: Kimberly Adams | Alexander Alonso | Beth Bynum | Liberty Munson | Tara Myers

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI is transforming assessment and redefining the roles of I-O psychologists. In this session, a panel of assessment experts will explore how AI is shifting professional identity, creating new career paths and opportunities (with the help of new skills), and raising challenges for I-O psychology as AI is used to perform the tasks of those new to the field (deskilling). Panelists will share real-world examples, ethical dilemmas, and actionable strategies to help attendees adapt and thrive as AI reshapes what I-Os do, how they do it, and the skills needed to be successful in our roles.

Experts will spar over engineering engagement, the utility of continuous listening, and whether AI or benchmarks add noise or clarity. The goal: provide research-anchored takeaways for programs that fit reality.

Zhou, L., Zhang, F., Parker, S., & Wang, M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Age differences in team leadership: A process model. Theory building in organizational psychology and behavior has advanced greatly over the last several decades but still needs to better uncover the processes driving phenomena over time. We argue for the ability to triangulate process knowledge through the combined use of qualitative and quantitative process data, along with computational process theories. Each of these approaches provides unique but meaningful insights into the nature of organizational processes to build better theories and accumulate knowledge. Authors demonstrate this tripartite approach across 5 studies of teamwork processes.

title: Career Open House
tracks: I-O Psychologist Career Development/Teaching | Events & Receptions
description: This networking event provides employer visibility to a variety of career personas. Students, early career, and recent graduates should plan to join from 9-10, and mid-career and longer tenured professionals, academics, and specialized (military, veteran, technology SMEs) should plan to join from 10-11.

title: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility Posters
tracks: Poster | Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility

title: From Service to Science: Veterans Navigating the I-O Psychology Pipeline (Session ID 855)
speakers: Abigail Burt | Esther Rodgers | Russell Thomas | Marla J Albertie PhD
speaker_affiliations: OpTech | St. Mary’s University | George Mason University | CTU
description: Burt, A. N. (Co-Chair), Rodgers, E. (Co-Chair), Albertie, M. J., Pearson, A. D., Estrada, A. X., Thomas, R., & Ramsey, R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). From Service to Science: Veterans Navigating the I-O Psychology Pipeline [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel will explore how military-affiliated students and professionals navigate the I-O psychology pipeline, from military service through graduate education to practice. Drawing on personal experiences and applied perspectives, panelists will discuss challenges such as stigma, cultural gaps, and underemployment, as well as the unique strengths and skills veterans bring to the field. This session will offer strategies for educators and organizations to better support this population and strengthen graduate programs and workplace outcomes.

title: Innovation in Skills Assessment Development: Open Space to Build Scalable Strategies (Session ID 832)
speakers: Lauren Park | Marian Pitel-Wali | Ruth Imose
speaker_affiliations: SAP | Workera | Johnson & Johnson
description: Imose, R. (Co-Chair), Lindgren, C. (Co-Chair), McDaniel, M., Park, L., Pitel-Wali, M. (2026). Innovation in Skills Assessment: An Open Space to Brainstorm Scalable Strategies [Alternative Session]. Society of Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This facilitator-led open space session aims to foster small-group discussions focused on developing effective skill assessment strategies and exploring how AI can support scalable assessment methods. Participants will rotate through 4 focused discussion tables, each led by a facilitator, in 15-minute rounds. Facilitators will synthesize insights, capture key takeaways, and at the session’s end, share recommendations across topics. The goal is to generate collaborative, actionable best practices to inform future efforts in measuring skills at scale.

title: Innovations in Public Safety Assessments: Best Practices and Lessons Learned (Session ID 654)
speakers: Julia Haas | Menucha Sperlin | Brandon Flores-Hutton | David Finch | Daniel King | Kristen Hargrave | Mark Frame | Todd Baker
speaker_affiliations: Carl Vinson Institute of Government, University of Georgia | Port Authority of New York New Jersey | IOS, Inc. | Talent Strategies Consulting | Arlington County Government | Middle Tennessee State University | HumRRO
description: Haas, J. B., Sperlin, M., Baker, T. A., Finch, D. M., Flores-Hutton, B., Frame, M., Hargrave, K., & King, D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Innovations in Public Safety Assessments: Best Practices and Lessons Learned [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Public safety agencies are under growing pressure to modernize how they select and promote personnel. This panel of experts will examine how technology, AI, and innovative assessment methods are transforming this traditionally change-resistant field. Panelists will share lessons learned and success stories from agencies that have evolved their practices, offering attendees with practical strategies to improve candidate experience, strengthen stakeholder engagement, and build more effective, future-ready public safety organizations.

title: Learning From Labor: (Re)Connecting Work Psychology With Unions (Session ID 764)
speakers: George Alliger | Leslie Hammer | Jason Phillips | Keaton Fletcher | Lisa Kath | Lauren Kuykendall | Michael Zickar | Peter McEachern | Ryszard Koziel
speaker_affiliations: Consulting Work Psychologist | Oregon Health & Science University | Baruch College & CUNY Graduate Center | Colorado State University | San Diego State University | George Mason University | Bowling Green State University | Saint Louis University School of Medicine
description: McEachern, P. J. (Co-chair), Koziel, R. J. (Co-chair), Alliger, G. M. (Co-chair), Broderick, G., Fletcher, K., Hammer, L., Harrison, R., Kath, L., Kuykendall, L. E., London, M., Phillips, J., Reindel, R., Zickar, M. J., & Catlin, M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). (Re)Connecting occupational health psychology with labor unions [Alternative session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. It is a little-known fact that the field of occupational health psychology was developed in part by I-O psychologists who studied and worked with labor unions. Although most of those connections have faded, interest in their reestablishment is increasing. This session will facilitate that process through roundtable discussions between session attendees, union leaders, and occupational health and safety researcher-activists. They will confront disconnects between I-O/OHP and organized labor, learn about what communities have to offer each other, and lay the groundwork for future collaboration.

title: Nudging Leaders Forward: Interventions to Enhance Organizational Decision-Making (Session ID 868)
speakers: Nandini Mehta | Alyssa Ortega | Amanda Julian | Bradley Brummel | Kaytlynn Griswold | Melissa Regester
speaker_affiliations: University of Houston | Vantage Leadership Consulting | Monark | PepsiCo
description: Mehta, N. M. (Co-Chair), Ortega, A. M. (Co-Chair), Brummel, B. J., Griswold, K. R., Julian, A., Lacerenza, C. N., & Regester, M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Nudging leaders forward: Interventions to enhance organizational decision-making [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The purpose of this panel discussion is to explore the utility of nudge interventions for enhancing leader development, drawing insights across practitioners and academics. Topics addressed include nudge theory and core components of nudging, current organizational applications, proposed applications toward leaders, and potential considerations and challenges in their future adoption. The overarching goal is to spark scientific inquiry into how nudges can be leveraged to enhance organizational decision-making among leaders.

title: Partner Showcase: Aon Talent Solutions - Work Rewired: AI's Impact on the Workforce, Talent Assessment, and the Candidate Experience
speakers: Ernie Paskey | Melissa Champine | Lauren Lilly | Maged Natanios | Scott Reithel
speaker_affiliations: Aon | Aon Talent Assessments | Marriott International
description: The showcase examines how Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping work—and how organizations can respond with intention rather than reaction. The session introduces an AI sensitivity–driven approach to understanding how tasks, skills, and roles are evolving, and how those insights enable job reimagination, targeted skill updates, and more informed workforce decisions. Building on this foundation, presenters explore how AI is transforming talent assessment and the candidate’s experience, shifting from traditional methods to more scalable, immersive, and engaging solutions. The session concludes with a real-world case study illustrating how AI can be used to design next‑generation assessment experiences, including the creation of dynamic video, audio, and 3D content—grounding emerging concepts in practical application for I-O and talent leaders.

title: Pushing the Boundaries of Personality Science in the Workplace (Session ID 525)
tracks: Selection/Assessment/Individual Differences | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Bart Wille | Brian Connelly | Samiat Ishola | Sang Eun Woo | Sofie Dupré | Theresa Leyens
speaker_affiliations: Ghent University | University of Toronto | Virginia Tech | Purdue University | Vrije Universiteit Brussel
description: Wille, B. (Chair), & Connelly, B. S. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Pushing the boundaries of personality science in the workplace [symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Ishola, S., Hickman, L., & Koutsoumpis, A. (2026). Paraverbal cues and personality: A review of organizational research and demonstration of modern methods. Bonner, C. V., Woo, S. E., & Waters, S. (2026). Validity considerations for narrative-based personality assessment. Leyens, T., Lievens, F., & Hofmans, J. (2026). Behavioral signatures across role-playing assessments in multiple, speeded assessments. Dupré, S., Wille, B., & De Clercq, B. (2026). Volitional personality change for professional growth: A multi-layered investigation via a digital intervention. This symposium presents 4 contributions which each introduce novel perspectives to the science of personality at work. These innovations involve (a) the use of paraverbal cues as relevant sources of information, (b) the value of life narratives for personality assessment in organizations, (c) the analysis and validity of within-person variability in personality characteristics across assessments, and finally (d) the effectiveness of an intervention aimed at intentionally changing aspects of personality in the context of professional development.

title: Recent Advances in Research on Careless Responding to Surveys and Tests (Session ID 888)
speakers: David Sexton | Mark Ramsey | Rusty Wilson | Cory Moore
speaker_affiliations: University of Central Florida | Research Scientist | Hirevue
description: Ramsey, M.C. (Co-Chair), & Sexton, D.L. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Recent Advances in Research on Careless Responding to Surveys and Tests. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Sawhney, G., Ramsey, M.C., Bowling, N.A. & Bijlani, A. (2026, April – May 2). Examining the Construct Validity of Item-Level Response Time as a Measure of Careless Responding to Surveys. Sexton, D. A., Schmidt, J., Bowling, N.A., Formica, O., Caccamise, J., Pelerez-Palacios, V., & Smith, C. (2026, April – May 2). What are You Looking at? Examining the Convergence of Measures of Careless Responding with Eye Tracking Indices Moore, C., Meade, A.W., DePue, W., Hobbs, M., Johnson, C., Inthavong, K, Udupa, S. & Wiederspiel, A. (2026, April – May 2). A Meta-analysis of Survey Careless Responding in I-O Psychology Ramsey, M.C. & Wilson, R. (2026, April – May 2). Examining the Interplay between Item Difficulty and Insufficient Effort Responding. This symposium presents recent discoveries in research regarding careless responding to surveys and cognitive ability tests. Specifically, this proposal contains 4 papers, including: (a) a construct validation of a new response time-based measure of carelessness to surveys, (b) a construct validation of an eye-tracking based measures of carelessness to surveys, (c) a meta-analysis on the prevalence of carelessness within different samples, and (d) a paper examining the effects of carelessness on data quality.

title: Rethinking Talent Management: Challenging Conventional Assumptions (Session Id 294)
speakers: Janine Waclawski | Allan Church | Gina Seaton | Seymour Adler | Steven Rogelberg
speaker_affiliations: Maestro Consulting LLC | Symbotic | Spencer Stuart | University of North Carolina at Charlotte
description: Waclawski, J. (Moderator), Adler, S., Church, A.H., Rogelberg, S.G. & Seaton, G. (2026). Rethinking talent management: Challenging conventional assumptions. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans LA, United States In today's fast-paced business landscape, where agility is paramount, organizations must regularly reassess their traditional views on how best to manage talent. Several of the core assumptions that have long guided talent management practices may no longer hold true. This session re-examines some key assumptions that organizations often make concerning talent management, drawing from findings emerging from I-O science and insights of leading scientist/practitioners. By reconsidering these assumptions, organizations can start to cultivate alternative, more effective, ways of managing talent.

title: SIOP Executive Board: AI in the Workplace: Reflections and Guidance From EEO Experts (Session ID 1459)
speakers: Charlotte Burrows | Emily Black | Jenny Yang | Christopher Nye
speaker_affiliations: New York University School of Law | New York University | Michigan State University
description: Nye, Christopher (Chair), Burrows, C., Yang, J., & Black, E. (2026). AI in the Workplace: Reflections and Guidance from EEO Experts [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly changing the workplace. For this session, Charlotte Burrows, former Chair of the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC), and Jenny Yang, former Chair of the EEOC and former Director of the Office of Federal Contract Compliance Programs (OFCCP), will discuss their views of the current legal landscape surrounding AI in hiring and how I-O psychology can contribute to this discussion. Then, along with a colleague, they will discuss approaches that computer scientists use to help prevent bias in AI models and the legal implications of these methods.

title: Secret Agents of DEI: Incorporating Best Practices Into Organizations (Session ID 967)
speakers: Alexandra Zelin | Kaitlin Ward | Nick Salter | Vivian Woo | Cindy Dziuba-Liu
speaker_affiliations: Wellstar Health System | Google | Hofstra University | Spellman High Voltage Electronics Corporation
description: Zelin, A. I. (Co-Chair), Salter, N. P. (Co-Chair), Dziuba-Liu, S.-N. C., Garland, E., Ward, K. P, & Woo, V. A. (2026). Secret agents of DEI: Incorporating best practices into organizations [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Amid anti-DEI backlash, organizations are reducing DEI efforts and devoting fewer resources to this area. However, there is still a need to advance this work. Authors propose that employees can be “secret agents” of DEI: individuals without formal DEI job titles who promote equity in various policies, procedures, and practices through their work. The session will feature practitioners who incorporate DEI into their daily jobs, followed by an academic discussing how scholars can conduct research to aid the work of DEI secret agents.

title: Stress as Offense to Self: New Understanding of Illegitimacy at Work (Session ID 1215)
tracks: Occupational Health/Work and Family | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Marcus Fila | Wiston Rodriguez | DuBois Barnes | Kelly Nguyen | Marcel Kern | Taylor Flinn
speaker_affiliations: Hope College | San Diego State University | Auburn University | Baruch College | University of Göttingen
description: Fila, M. J. (Co-Chair), & Rodriguez, W. A. (Co-Chair). (2026, April 30-May 2). Stress As Offense-to-Self: New Understanding of Illegitimacy at Work [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Illegitimate tasks are professional identity threatening stressors that violate normative prescriptions of what can be expected in a given role or position. By signaling a lack of respect towards employees and their roles, they leave employees feeling like “I should not have to do this!” Research on illegitimate tasks spans 17 years. However, new outcomes and boundary conditions continuing to be examined. Therefore, the purpose of this symposium is to highlight 5 novel, new findings in this research; including the first meta-analytic review of this construct, and a study of a new and related construct: the illegitimate absence of tasks.

title: Taking Action on Survey Data With a Deskless Workforce (Session ID 202)
speakers: Belinda Williams | Brian Jefferis | Drew Lam | Mallory Wright | Casey Witherspoon
speaker_affiliations: Southern Glazer's Wine & Spirits | Qualtrics | PepsiCo | SGWS | Target
description: Jefferis, B., Lam, A., Williams, B., Witherspoon, C., & Wright, M. (2026, April). Taking action on survey data with a deskless workforce [Alternative session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA. The purpose of this session is to learn from practitioners who work with deskless workers to take action on survey data to improve the employee experience. Although deskless workers make up the majority of the global workforce and are often included in employee listening surveys, this group is often overlooked in the postsurvey phase of taking action. This session will provide a platform for the panelists to share realistic learnings and solutions to real-world challenges. The audience will have opportunities to help us prioritize the direction of the conversation and ask direct questions.

title: Talent Hackathon AI Edition: Group Brainstorming for Talent Challenges in An AI World (Session ID 1207)
speakers: Erica Desrosiers | Matthew Dreyer | A. Silke McCance | Michael Ehret | Pamela Congemi | Seth Kamen
speaker_affiliations: Johnson Controls | Prudential Financial | Procter & Gamble | Johnson & Johnson | Advanced Sterilization Products | People Solutions
description: Marazita, M. (Chair), Chamber, R. (Panelist), Congemi, P. (Panelist), Desrosiers, E. (Panelist), Dreyer, M. (Panelist), Ehret, M. (Panelist), Kamen, S. (Panelist), Marcus-Blank, B. (Panelist), McCance, S. (Panelist). (2026). Crowdsourcing Success: Talent Hackathon for Real-World Problem Solving [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organization Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Many I-O practitioners face similar challenges within their organizations. Through audience participation, this alternative session will crowdsource solutions to pressing AI-related organizational issues, from onboarding to talent strategy. Senior HR Leaders from 8 companies will present an AI talent challenge they are experiencing and then lead roundtable solutioning discussions.

title: The Central Role of Communication in Teams: Performance, Adaptation, and Well-Being (Session ID 1023)
speakers: Allison Kom | Caton Weinberger | Cooper Drose | Cynthia Maupin | Kaushik Ram

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Weinberger, C. (2026). Leading through transition: The role of balance and positivity in team adaptation. Kom, A. J. & Fletcher, K. A. (2026). Participation equality: A systematic review. Kom, A. J., Chheda, K., Fletcher, K. A., Drose, C. (2026). Trait dominance composition, performance, and affective climate. Ram, K. (2026). Effective communication in a globally distributed team: An agent-based model. Maupin, C. K., Carter, D. R., Mohan, G., & Fitzhugh, S. (2026). Leadership responses to disruptive events: The moderating role of event characteristics. Communication is central to team functioning, shaping not only performance but also adaptation, well-being, and recovery. This symposium features 5 papers that explore diverse drivers of communication (e.g., goals, leadership, work location), as well as its wide-ranging effects. These studies use innovative perspectives to deepen understanding about how team communication patterns emerge, and the critical role they play in team functioning. The results from this symposium provide direction for future research and practical guidance for organizations.

title: The Heart of Leadership: Understanding How Leaders Shape and Carry Emotions (Session ID 631)
tracks: Leadership/Leader Development | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Jane Thomas | Courtney Williams | Alicia Grandey | Hector Madrid | Tiffany Paquin
speaker_affiliations: Purdue University Northwest | Grand Valley State University | Universidad Adolfo Ibanez, Chile | Toronto Metropolitan University
description: Williams, C. E. (Co-Chair) & Thomas, J. S. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). The heart of leadership: Understanding how leaders shape and carry emotions [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Emotions have become a central aspect of leadership in today’s evolving workplace. This symposium offers multidisciplinary perspectives on leadership as a nuanced emotional process. Four papers examine antecedents of leaders’ emotional influence, follower interpretations, relational dynamics, and the emotional costs leaders face when handling others’ emotions. Session concludes with a discussion of the future of leadership and emotion research in a rapidly changing work environment.

title: Transforming How We Study Safety: New Directions for the 21st Century Workforce (Session ID 787)
speakers: Ashley Haygood | Heewon Jang | Seth Kaplan | Michael Burke | Jin Lee | John Little | Madeline Wonders

Transforming How We Study Safety: New Directions for the 21st Century Workforce [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Wonders, M. E., Kaplan, S. A., Jang, H., Haygood, A. N., & Frerichs, V. (2026). To report or not to report? Insights from shipyard workers’ accident experiences. Little, J., Kath, L. Bayesian networks: Interpretable machine learning for unraveling complex interactions in workplace safety and health research. This symposium presents innovative and foundational approaches to workplace safety research, ranging from qualitative interviews to advanced methods such as machine learning, natural language processing, and Bayesian networks. Papers span themes of accident reporting, safety attitudes, and predictive modeling. Designed for an intermediate audience, this session highlights diverse, data-driven methodologies that advance both the science and practice of occupational safety.

title: What Is Your Dangerous Idea? Reimagining the Future of Employee Surveys (Session ID 507)
speakers: Desmond Leung | Julian Allen | Randy Lim | Nabila Sheikh-Hashmi | Payton Stewart | Ryan Horn, PhD | Scott Brooks
speaker_affiliations: Intuitive | Alnylam Pharmaceuticals | McKinsey & Company | Saks Global | Leadership Development Institute - LSU | OrgVitality | PespiCo
description: Leung, D. W. (Chair), Allen, J. B. (Presenter), Brooks, S. M. (Presenter), Horn, R. G. (Presenter), Lim, R. (Presenter), Sheikh-Hashmi, N. (Presenter), Stewart, P. (Presenter). (2026, April 30 – May 2). What is your dangerous idea? Reimagining the future of employee surveys [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Are I-Os too comfortable with the conventional employee survey playbook? This session brings together survey practitioners from diverse organizations to explore “dangerous ideas” that challenge long-held assumptions in employee surveying. These ideas provoke new ways of thinking and the unsettling possibility that they might be true. In this modified panel format, panelists will explore topics such as the role of surveys in the age of AI, the (over)emphasis on engagement metrics, and how scorecard incentives can distort data. This session invites attendees to reimagine the future of employee listening.

title: Will AI Save or Replace Assessment Centers? A Debate on the Future (Session ID 393)
speakers: Brad Chambers | Christina Norris-Watts | Doug Reynolds | Lynn Collins, Ph.D. | Michael Bazigos | Suzanne Tsacoumis
speaker_affiliations: BTS | Johnson & Johnson | APT | Columbia University in the City of New York | HumRRO
description: Chambers, B.A., Collins, L.G, Bazigos, M.N., Norris-Watts, C., Reynolds, D., & Tsacoumis, S. (2026, April 30-May 2). Will AI save or replace assessment centers? A debate on the future. [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming assessment centers, promising scalability, consistency, and predictive power. But can algorithms ever replace what makes ACs unique: observing live behavior, its consequences, and the powerful feedback and organizational learning that flow from it? In this debate, 4 panelists will take strong, opposing stances on motions such as “AI is more valid than human assessors” and “AI will render assessment centers obsolete.” Expect sparks to fly as authors confront validity, fairness, ethics, and the future relevance of assessment centers.

title: Withdrawal From Work: Contemporary Manifestations and Personality Predictors (Session ID 770)
speakers: Michael Hazboun | Deniz Ones | Brittany Mercado | Ziyu Ren | Stephan Dilchert
speaker_affiliations: University of Minnesota- Twin Cities | University of Minnesota · Full-time | Elon University | University of Minnesota, Twin Cities | Zicklin School of Business
description: Ones, D. S. (Co-Chair), Hazboun, M. K. A. (Co-Chair), & Viswesvaran, C. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Withdrawal for Modern Work: Contemporary Manifestations and Personality Predictors [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Hazboun, M. K. A., & Ones, D. S. (2026). Affective and Self-Evaluative Personality Traits Predict Withdrawal: A Meta-Analysis . Dilchert, S., & Stanek, K. C. (2026). Beyond Disengagement: A Multidimensional Framework for Understanding Quiet Quitting Mercado, B. K. & Dilchert, S. (2026). Cyberloafing: A Distinct Manifestation of Withdrawal Ren, Z., Ones, D. S., & Giordano, C. (2026). Emotional Intelligence and Employee Disengagement: A Meta-Analysis. When withdrawal was conceptualized, employees were co-located and physically present meant psychologically engaged. Not anymore. Authors introduce an expanded framework encompassing contemporary forms such as quiet quitting, cyberloafing, disengagement, particularly invisible in remote/hybrid work. This symposium features 4 studies exploring withdrawal as theoretically distinct from other counterproductive behaviors and highlighting its personality nomological net. Findings from the meta-analyses and primary studies in this session are essential for anyone addressing engagement and retention at work.

title: AI in Employee Selection Update III: State, Federal, and Litigation Updates (Session ID 462)
speakers: Bre Timko | Cliff Haimann | David Schmidt | Niloy Ray
speaker_affiliations: DCI Consulting | Morgan, Lewis & Bockius LLP | DCI Consulting Group, Inc. | Littler
description: Timko, B., Schmidt, D., Ray, N., & Haimann, C. (2026). AI in Employee Selection Update III: State, Federal, and Litigation Updates [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The past year has brought major federal deregulatory-focused changes, and states have responded with new AI laws. AI-focused litigation continues, exposing both vendors and users to legal risk. Navigating these evolving regulations and challenges in AI-based employee selection remains difficult. This session will provide updates on federal, state, and international regulations and recent case law. This is the third iteration of this session at SIOP. Attendees will leave with a thorough understanding of the legal landscape, with a focus on updates between the SIOP 2025 and 2026 conferences.

title: Agentic AI at Work: Augmenting the Nature and Future of Work (Session ID 534)
speakers: Sean Noble | Yizhen Egyn Zhu | Eric Sydell | Louis Hickman | Ben Porr | Weiwen Nie
speaker_affiliations: Capital One | Amazon | Vero AI | Harver | Hogan Assessments
description: Noble, S. M. (Chair), Zhu, Y. E., Porr, W. B., Hickman, L., Sydell, E., & Nie, W. (2026). Agentic AI at work: Augmenting the nature and future of work [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA. This expert-led session examines psychological and organizational dynamics as AI systems take on autonomous, decision-making roles at work. Panelists will explore how organizations prepare employees to collaborate with agentic AI, where to draw boundaries around decision-making authority, and how human–AI collaboration may evolve as autonomy increases. Authors will discuss potential psychological impacts of daily interaction with highly autonomous systems and offer strategies for maintaining human agency while leveraging AI capabilities.

title: Beyond Average: Advances in Team Composition and Approaches to Multilevel Data (Session ID 1188)
speakers: Alexandra Harris-Watson | Jeffrey Masser | Tiffany Bisbey | Dorothy Carter | Jordan Johnson | Quan Nguyen
speaker_affiliations: Purdue University | The George Washington University | Michigan State University | Clemson University | Pomona College
description: Bisbey, T. (Co-Chair), Masser, J. (Co-Chair), & Harris-Watson, A. M. (Co-Chair). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Beyond average: Advances in team composition and approaches to multilevel data [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Harris-Watson, A. M., Masser, J., Romero, M., & Bisbey, T. (2026, April 30 – May 2). From traits to trait preferences: The role of implicit teammate theories. Carter, D. (2026). Discussant Johnson, J., & Traylor, A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Proactive personality and team conflict: The moderating role of constructive controversy training in academic research teams. Nguyen, Q., Nguyen, J., Rousseau, M., Grossman, R., & Feitosa, J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Temporal and dyadic perspectives on diversity, trust, and performance. Despite decades of research on team composition, I-Os still lack consistent answers about how to assemble people into effective teams. This symposium goes beyond traditional approaches. Authors examine how personality dispersion influences conflict over time and whether training interventions help, explore temporal dynamics showing how surface and deep-level diversity operate differently than previously thought, and introduce teammate expectations as a missing link between traits and outcomes. These studies offer fresh perspectives on team composition with implications for research and practice.

title: Beyond Engagement: Tackling Organizational Disconnection in Modern Work Psychology (Session ID 1129)
speakers: John Cooper | Autumn Krauss | Adrian Furnham | John Weiner | Kira Van Niekerk | Stephen Cuppello

description: Cooper, J. (Chair), van Niekerk, K., Cuppello, S., Weiner, J., Krauss, A., Furnham, A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Beyond engagement: Tackling organizational disconnection in modern work psychology [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Despite digital connectivity and AI tools, employees report rising loneliness and disengagement. This panel explores why engagement surveys fail to address the root cause, organizational disconnection, and examines its impact on culture, performance, and well-being. Drawing on research in work psychology, hybrid work, and AI-mediated sociality, authors propose connection as a fundamental part of organizations’ employee value proposition (EVP).

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Occupational Health Psychology (OHP) is an interdisciplinary research area within I-O that draws from insights across multiple disciplines. In this IGNITE session, presenters will discuss how OHP, in combination with engineering and computer science, human factors, psychoneuroimmunology, counseling psychology, sport management, and economics can innovatively address issues concerning employee health, safety, and well-being. The session aims to strengthen research and practice by encouraging audience members to consider a broader interdisciplinary lens in their approaches to worker health and safety.

This panel will explore practical and innovative approaches to make feedback more impactful for both candidates and organizations. Authors discuss strategies for choosing the right feedback content and method of presentation as well as how to best incorporate technology (especially generative AI) to enhance feedback offerings while still maintaining authenticity and trust. Join us to learn practical tips for creating a valuable and supportive candidate feedback experience that resonates on a personal level.

As the I-O field grows and evolves, graduate training must prepare students to meet workforce demands. Yet, gaps remain in areas like people analytics, workforce development, and consulting skills. This alternative session combines panel presentations and roundtable discussions to explore these blind spots and equip students to proactively address them. Aimed at an introductory to intermediate audience, this session offers practical insights for students, early-career practitioners, and educators.

Closeness at a distance: Creating and maintaining social connections while working remotely. Remote work has grown exponentially over the past few years. Despite its benefits, concerns have arisen regarding the well-being of remote workers, especially in terms of impaired social relationships, recovery experiences, and access. The present session brings together emerging research and theory on remote worker well-being, including social well-being, job stress, sleep, and health behaviors, that illustrate a nuanced understanding of remote worker well-being and provides practical suggestions for improvement.

This session will present relevant, real-world experience from recent (within the last 3-5 years) masters and doctoral I-O graduates across a variety of fields and specialties. Topics include navigating a challenging job market, breaking into the field, experiences unique to new professionals, and adjusting from grad school to a career. Students, educators, mentors, and those looking for diverse perspectives on early career experiences are well-suited to this introductory discussion on the realities of navigating I-O careers post-covid.

High Impact HiPo programs in Large Enterprises: Evidence, Practice, & Future Trends. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. High-potential (HiPo) programs remain one of the most widely used and debated talent strategies in large organizations. They promise to secure leadership pipelines, accelerate business impact, and strengthen engagement. Yet, the evidence shows that programs are often inconsistently defined, unevenly applied, and insufficiently evaluated. This panel explores current and future applications of HiPo programs from seasoned practitioners in enterprise companies while also sharing results from a global 2025 HiPo study.

Invisible disabilities pose unique identity challenges at work because disclosure is optional, yet these conditions can influence behavior and performance. This symposium examines how employees manage invisible disabilities and the associated costs and benefits. Papers explore (a) daily masking among women with ADHD, (b) recovery as a buffer for ASD/ADHD employees, and (c) boundary management in chronic illness. Together, authors offer theory, methods, and practices for more inclusive workplaces.

Me, myself, and AI: How I-Os are using AI for personal development [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial intelligence isn’t just coming: It’s already here and reshaping everything. The I-O field can’t afford to sit on the sidelines. Yet, few SIOP presentations have explored how AI can fuel personal growth for practitioners themselves. In this session, you’ll hear how I-Os are experimenting with AI to supercharge their own learning, streamline their work, and expand their impact. In IGNITE format, presenters will share what sparked their curiosity, how they discovered AI’s potential, and the tangible ways these skills have transformed them both personally and professionally.

This panel will explore the opportunities and challenges of measuring creativity and innovative thinking within organizations. Panelists will represent academic, applied, and government perspectives, addressing questions of validity, fairness, and practical application. Discussion will highlight current evidence, emerging technologies, and contexts in which creativity measures may or may not be appropriate.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Statistical training remains central to I-O psychology, yet most curricula still rely on null hypothesis significance testing (NHST). This session advocates a model-based approach, positioning the general linear model as the conceptual foundation for teaching, research, and practice. Participants will explore how this framework unifies statistical methods, fosters deeper reasoning, and aligns with the data-rich demands of modern organizations. An interactive CPD exercise will guide attendees in developing actionable plans to enhance model-based fluency.

Pregnancy Loss at Work: Disenfranchised and Embodied Grief Amid Organizational Norms and Policies Contrary to how research and policy focus on pregnancy and postpartum transitions, other maternal and reproductive experiences of working women remain overlooked. Across qualitative and quantitative studies, the papers examine how employees navigate disclosure, emotions, and organizational support when facing these experiences. Together, they reveal critical gaps in workplace policies and current literature, and with the discussant, the symposium seeks to integrate these contributions, pointing toward the need for more inclusive practices and a broader research agenda.

title: Partner Showcase: Pinsight - AI is Ready. Are your Leaders? What 14,000 Leadership Assessments Reveal About Why AI Adoption Stalls
speakers: Martin Lanik | Amy Huber
speaker_affiliations: Pinsight
description: Organizations are investing heavily in Artificial Intelligence (AI), yet many struggle to move beyond pilots and demonstrations to achieve enterprise-scale impact. Most conversations focus on technology and data readiness, but our research suggests a different constraint: leadership capability. Drawing on a global sample of 14,000 leaders assessed through behavioral simulations and personality measures, this session examines how leadership readiness for AI varies across senior executives, mid-level leaders, and frontline managers. The findings reveal a consistent pattern: organizations can define AI strategy but struggle to translate it into coordinated execution at each level. Senior executives often face challenges leading large-scale transformation, mid-level leaders struggle to translate AI strategy into operational change, and frontline managers lack the skills to guide workforce adoption. By highlighting patterns across industries and regions, attendees will leave with a data-driven view of what leadership capabilities must shift to turn AI investment into sustained business impact.

Impact of Reframed Instructions and Practice Testing on Test Performance of Diverse Candidate Groups Preparing for employment tests is a growing area of research that recognizes test performance depends not only on ability, but also on preparation and context. Practice can enhance performance by reducing anxiety and increasing familiarity. Yet preparation is influenced by test characteristics and individual differences, raising important questions about fairness and validity. Understanding how, when, and for whom practice helps remains essential to equitable assessment and ensuring valid hiring decisions are made.

This session will address the traditionally distinct “I” and “O” sides of the field, in which the I-side has historically focused on the assessment and measurement of KSAOs, and the O-side has focused on the context of assessment and measurement in relation to the social, emotional, and health consequences of individuals. Presenters will provide applied examples of how the traditionally “distinct” sides of our field are heavily integrated through their work. This IGNITE! session highlights the interconnection between the “I” and “O” in practice, showing how insights from both sides are used by practitioners to address workplace challenges.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will bring together a panel with expertise in coaching and AI in human development for an interactive discussion on the evolving practice of coaching in this era of AI. Topics will include discussion of best practices on how to incorporate AI into coaching to drive impact for clients, without casting aside our core capabilities. Authors will cover lessons learned and how coaches can create and measure their impact, plus a comparison of coaching interventions with AI compared to other talent development approaches.

Drawing on research and lived experience, panelists share what worked, what didn’t, and practical guidance for organizations starting or redesigning their engagement programs.

Panelists will discuss what defines job hopping, how it has evolved over time, gender, generational, and industry differences, perceptions surrounding it, and its impact on careers. This panel aims to offer a comprehensive understanding of job hoping by blending research on this topic with personal experiences of job hopping.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will provide an update on the work that has been completed in the creation of a certification in I-O psychology. This work includes endorsement from SIOP, the results of a Job Task Analysis survey, and the progress of an item writing committee. Additional discussion will include how the certification exam content aligns with SIOP’s Education and Training Guidelines, as well as the extent that those practicing I-O psychology may harm the public. Finally, future steps in the completion of the I-O psychology certification exam, along with an expected launch date, will be presented.

(2026, April 30 – May 2). When helping hurts: The behavioral impact of support imbalance. Social support is intuitively beneficial, but the research around support in the workplace is complex and inconsistent. Social support that is actually unhelpful (e.g., undermining confidence, unwanted) or imbalanced can even have negative effects. Three papers highlight nuances of how unhelpful social support can affect workers, increasing feelings of loneliness, depriving workers of core psychological needs, and reducing engagement. In addition, authors present how imbalanced support relates to fewer organizational citizenship behaviors and more counterproductive workplace behaviors.

Advancing the Science of Team Assessment: Tools and Insights for High-Stakes Teams [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. title: Board Characteristics' Diverse Impacts on Sustainability (Session ID 987)
speakers: Stephan Dilchert | Phoebe Hessen | Sydney Higley | Yagizhan Yazar
speaker_affiliations: Zicklin School of Business | University of Minnesota | College of Liberal Arts, University of Minnesota Twin Cities | Tapestry Inc.
description: Dilchert, S. (Co-Chair & Discussant), Ren, Z. (Non-Speaking Co-Chair), & Ones, D.S. (Non-Speaking Co-Chair). (2026, April 30 - May 2). Board Characteristics' Diverse Impacts on Sustainability [Sympoisum]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conferene, New Orleans, LA, United States. Four studies analyzing several thousand organizations worldwide indicate that board gender diversity enhances resource conservation and stakeholder engagement but not transformative innovation or harm prevention. Furthermore, these effects reverse across cultures: board gender diversity predicts greater sustainability in Asia and Anglo countries but reduced sustainability in Latin Europe. Larger boards enhance change-oriented initiatives but not compliance behaviors. CEO duality shows negligible effects. These findings provide guidance for organizations designing boards to embed social and environmental sustainability.

title: Cognitive Ability in Personnel Selection: What Is Its Place Today? (Session ID 479)
tracks: Selection/Assessment/Individual Differences | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Dawn Lambert | Andrew Solomonson | Jeffrey Cucina | Joseph Abraham | Leaetta Hough | Nancy Tippins | Paul Sackett
speaker_affiliations: Talogy | Delta Air Lines | U.S. Customs and Border Protection | Dunnette Group | The Nancy T. Tippins Group, LLC | University of Minnesota
description: Lambert, D. D. (Chair), Abraham, J. D., Cucina, J. M., Hough, L. M., Sackett, P. R., Solomonson, A., & Tippins, N. T. (2026). Cognitive Ability in Personnel Selection: What Is Its Place Today? [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Recent meta-analytic research examining cognitive ability and job performance, focused on data from contemporary studies, has produced validity estimates far lower than the traditionally accepted value of .51 produced by Schmidt and Hunter (1998). Researchers agree, however, that regardless of one’s perspective on the magnitude of the estimate, cognitive ability continues to play an important role in the overall world of personnel selection. Panelists will discuss perspectives from research and practice regarding when and how cognitive ability may best be utilized in selection systems today.

title: Consulting Gumbo: Stirring Up Clarity on Internal and External Consulting Roles (Session ID 567)
speakers: Andre Hennig | Briana Squires | Casey Witherspoon | Desmond Leung | Sophie Kay | Jennifer Garrow | A. Silke McCance | Tonya Baker

(Panelist). (2026, April). Consulting Gumbo: Stirring Up Clarity on Internal and External Consulting Roles [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Industrial-organizational (I-O) practitioners often refer to themselves broadly as an external or an internal consultant, but the true nature of these roles varies significantly depending on many factors. In this alternative session, panelists with broad applied experiences will shed light on internal and external I-O consulting by sharing their experiences and cover career topics rarely covered in graduate school. Following a panel question and answer segment, the audience will be invited to join breakout groups to learn more about consulting career paths they are interested in exploring.

title: Context Matters: Determinants of Work-Relevant Learning Across the Career Span (Session ID 441)

Context matters: Determinants of work-relevant learning across the career span [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This symposium comprises 4 timely empirical papers that seek to contribute to current understanding of how workers and job seekers are engaging in work-relevant self-directed learning inside and outside organizations. Using a diverse set of quantitative and qualitative methodological approaches, presenters will discuss how educational, technological, and social contextual factors influence work-relevant self-directed learning outcomes across the career span. The session includes an expert discussant in the field of workplace learning synthesizing takeaways for research and practice.

title: Fear and Loathing in Leadership Research (Session ID 681)

(2026, April 30 – May 2). Fear and Loathing in Leadership Research: Where We Are Now and Where We Need to Go [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Leadership research is currently undergoing a period of fear and loathing, similar to that described in Hunter Thompson’s classic 1970s novel. In recent years, leadership research has been widely critiqued for its lack of theoretical cohesion and methodological shortcomings. Yet, although many have raised problems with leadership research, the path forward is unclear. This session addresses these concerns by bringing together some of the field's most accomplished leadership scholars to summarize the current problems with leadership research and provide a vision for how they can be addressed.

title: From Art to Science: Rethinking the Discipline of Organizational Design (Session ID 1173)
speakers: Aaron Sorensen | Courtney Paynter | Lane Siedor | Christopher Mason
speaker_affiliations: Lotis Blue Consulting | AbbVie | orgvue | Amylu Foods

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizational design has long been treated as an art rather than a science. This panel examines how I-O psychology can enhance the rigor of organizational design—linking structure, roles, and decision-making rights to behavioral outcomes and business performance. Through research and real-world redesigns, panelists will demonstrate how data-driven, human-centered design can align strategy, culture, and capabilities to build agile, high-performing organizations and bridge the micro-meso-macro gap, as called for in the literature and currently missing in the I-O community discourse.

title: From Disruption to Direction: Charting the Future of Organizational Practices (Session ID 478)
speakers: Breanna Green | Gabriela Sedotto | Laura Joiner | Maryam Ahmad Kazmi | Nikola Fedorowicz | Susan D'Mello

From Disruption to Direction: Charting the Future of Organizational Practices. [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. With the ever-evolving landscapes of economics, geopolitics, and technology, the organizations that are flexible and agile remain ahead of the rest. Organizations can develop resilience through strategic enhancements to recruitment and selection, leadership development, talent management, employee listening, and people analytics. This IGNITE session offers both data- and research-driven approaches to implementing these practices, with an emphasis on how evolving practices in these areas foster resilience, maintain stability, and allow organizations to succeed amid external challenges.

title: From Insights to Impact: Building Dashboard Suites That HRBPs Actually Use (Session ID 1320)

From Insights to Impact: Building Dashboard Suites That HRBPs Actually Use [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In today’s data-driven organizations, HR dashboards often fall short of driving action. This 50-minute cross-company panel brings together experts from HP Inc., DICK’S Sporting Goods, Comcast, and AbbVie to explore why dashboards go unused—and what to do about it. Through real-world examples and interactive discussion, panelists share proven strategies to build dashboard suites that HR Business Partners actually use to make informed, impactful decisions.

title: Generative Artificial Intelligence in Workplace Assessment Development: Pros and Cons (Session ID 1254)

Generative artificial intelligence in workplace assessment development: Pros and cons [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology, New Orleans, LA, United States. Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) holds promise for many research and practical applications in workplace settings, but remains understudied in many important fields such as workplace psychological assessment. In this symposium, we demonstrate the use of GenAI to develop and categorize multiple item types for 6 psychological constructs, discuss the advantages and limitations of GenAI in developing and validating psychological assessment items for the workplace, potential next steps and, in lieu of a discussant, reserve ample time for audience discussion.

title: Guiding Aligned Career Decisions: Assessment, Feedback, and Social Context (Session ID 470)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Making informed and well-fitting career decisions depends on a multitude of factors. This symposium focuses on the roles of holistic and precise career assessments, meaningful career feedback, and social and relational considerations in guiding career decisions. Together, these studies highlight ways to support choices that are both informed and personally aligned.

title: Leading Well-Being at Work: Nine Unique Paths to Support Employees (Session ID 799)

Leading wellbeing at work: Nine unique paths to support employees [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Leaders who model well-being and recovery practices foster psychological safety, reduce burnout, and enhance performance. Building on last year’s popular session, this panel explores nonprofit leadership and burnout prevention, sleep leadership, recovery-friendly practices, healing communities, support for nature, psychophysiology, leadership for employed minors and graduate students, and applied leadership interventions. Through flash talks and interactive roundtable discussions, participants will gain actionable tools to reimagine leadership as a driver of health and resilience.

How susceptible are open-ended personality assessments to faking and the use of GenAI. Increased accessibility to generative artificial intelligence (AI)-based tools make it possible for candidates to leverage these tools in unproctored prehire assessments. It is unclear from research whether and how this will impact validity and quality of hire. The 5 projects in this symposium aim to sharpen focus on the impact of generative (AI) in selection assessments by examining impact on validity and differences in responses when AI is used, and evaluating potential ways to either reduce applicants’ opportunity to use these tools or more effectively detect it when they do.

title: Motivation, Attitudes, and Emotions Posters

My Worst I-O Day Ever: Turning Nightmares into Wisdom [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Setbacks are unavoidable in organizations, from failed initiatives to major crises. However, adversity can also spark growth, innovation, and deeper engagement. In this impactful panel, experienced practitioners will share their “worst I-O day ever,” including unexpected disasters, leadership shifts, and personal setbacks—the tough challenges that grow us. Attendees will hear honest stories, lessons learned, and practical strategies for turning setbacks into opportunities that help individuals and organizations to not just recover but also learn and move forward.

title: One Big Beautiful Presentation: EEOC/OFCCP/DEI Annual Update (Session Id 394)

One Big Beautiful Presentation: EEOC/OFCCP Annual Update. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In a year of regulatory whiplash, organizations face major shifts in civil rights enforcement. From the revocation of EO 11246 to OFCCP’s funding rollercoaster, and new EEOC-DOJ guidance on “illegal DEI,” compliance professionals must adapt quickly. This panel offers updates on EEOC and OFCCP enforcement, pay equity, AI regulation, and election-related impacts. Legal experts, practitioners, and employers will share insights on navigating the science-law intersection amid political uncertainty and preparing for what’s next.

title: Partner Showcase: Deloitte - Work Design in the Age of Humans x Machines: AI Value Realization
speakers: Sarah Szpaichler Stough
speaker_affiliations: Deloitte Consulting
description: Organizations are increasing AI investment, yet many aren’t seeing expected returns because technology-first strategies are under-emphasizing the human advantage, a key driver of competitive differentiation. This session explores an approach to Humans × Machines collaboration including how to orchestrate unique human skills and machine capability to deliver exponential outcomes. Grounded in tipping points highlighted in Deloitte’s 2026 Global Human Capital Trends , we’ll share activities that accelerate value realization through Work Design Engineering: clear outcomes, trustworthy decisioning, data/process readiness, adoption by design, and intentional work design. We’ll include a brief visualization of Deloitte’s Work Design Canvas to illustrate how teams re-balance work across automation, augmentation, and uniquely human strengths.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Best practices are more accessible than ever, but adoption remains one of the I-O psychologist’s greatest challenges. Four practitioners with experience in various talent and analytics disciplines will share practical recommendations (e.g., collaboration with HR business partners, actionable design) for driving adoption of programs such as goal-setting, engagement surveys, and people analytics. Following these presentations, the session will shift to a panel discussion and audience questions.

title: Recharging the Human Workplace: Insights From Movement, Mindfulness, and Mirth (Session ID 711)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Four speakers will describe how they use lessons from movement, mindfulness, and mirth to breathe life into organizational practices. Using both the IGNITE presentation and small group discussion formats, audience members will hear examples of restorative practices and reflect on how they can use similar practices in their applied work.

title: Reimagining Meetings: Sharing Research and Practical Lessons to Inspire Interventions (Session ID 1118)

Reimagining meetings: Sharing research and practical lessons to inspire organizational interventions [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Employees spend a lot of time in meetings, with organizations questioning whether they drive value or drain productivity. Scholars have highlighted the costs of poor quality meetings, whereas organizations like Shopify have gone so far as to cancel meetings to reset norms, with new technology like AI expanding the possibilities. This session will bring together meetings experts and I-O practitioners to share research and real-world examples of interventions that improved meeting quality, followed by small-group discussions where participants will propose their own interventions.

title: Strategies for Building An AI-Literate Workforce (Session ID 150)

(Panelist), Prabhu, N. (Panelist), & Theys, E. R. (Panelist) (2026) Strategies for Building an AI-Literate Workforce [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial intelligence (AI) is a rapidly emerging technology that is quickly changing how work is done in the 21st century. Due to its increasingly vital role, it is essential that employees understand how AI works, how it is most effectively applied, and how to leverage it responsibly. This session gathers accomplished I-O psychologists to discuss how their organizations are building AI literacy. The conversation will focus on creating an AI-literate workforce through training, development, and personnel selection, offering attendees practical insights for navigating the evolving workplace.

Bowling, N. A., Lyons, B. D, Sexton, D., Mullaney, K., & Wood, D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Developing and Validating a Context-Specific CWB Scale in the U.S. Military. Mercado, B. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Patient mistreatment and CWB among nurses: The roles of exhaustion and core self-evaluations. Brummel, B. J., Fezzey, T. N. A., & Harms, P. D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Unique features of the academic counterproductive work behavior ecosystem. Counterproductive work behavior (CWB) research has often treated context (e.g., occupation or industry) as incidental. This symposium addresses that limitation by examining CWB across diverse settings, including the military, law enforcement, nursing, and academia. Presenters will illustrate how context shapes the nature, antecedents, and consequences of CWB, revealing both commonalities and unique patterns. Collectively, these insights highlight the theoretical and practical value of situating CWB within the work environment.

title: Turning Data Into Decisions: How I-O Psychology Makes People Analytics Actionable (Session ID 304)
