Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Industrial-organizational (I-O) psychologists are well-positioned to close the gap between analysis and action in People Analytics. This session highlights projects where insights were translated into structural change: redesigning succession planning to emphasize readiness and accountability, using predictive models to guide stock-based retention, rebuilding leadership frameworks with behavioral clarity, tailoring dashboards to decision-making, and standardizing quality-of-hire metrics. Together, these examples show how I-O psychology ensures analytics is not just descriptive but actionable.

This master tutorial focuses on the use of data visualization to properly communicate information and persuade public audiences. Authors will discuss best practices in data visualization and potential pitfalls. They will also conduct hands-on demonstrations of Tableau, a powerful software used for data visualization and dashboards but rarely taught in psychology programs. Participants will leave the workshop with enough experience to build basic static and dynamic data visualizations using a sample dataset from the organizational sciences.

title: When Science Meets Software: Applying I-O Skills in An HR Tech World (Session ID 587)
speakers: Anna Hulett | Colby Nesbitt | Allie Wehling | Marian Pitel-Wali
speaker_affiliations: Qualtrics | Netflix | Cisco | Workera
description: Hulett, A.L., Nesbitt, C.K., Pitel-Wali, M., Wehling, A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). When science meets software: Applying I-O skills in an HR tech world [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The scientist–practitioner model is colliding with HR tech. Most tools weren’t built by I-Os—or for the strategies I-Os champion—yet they must still make them work. How do you uphold I-O principles when the tech isn’t a perfect fit? This panel convenes I-Os working inside HR tech companies —in product, customer-facing, and internal people roles—in a conversation moderated by an HR tech customer. This panel session equips I-Os to overcome real-world limitations, influence roadmaps, and navigate vendor positioning—while keeping scientific integrity and business impact front and center.

This symposium brings together 3 papers that examine the burdens, underlying mechanisms, and potential interventions associated with organizationally mandated nonstandard schedules across diverse occupational contexts.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI and emerging tech are reshaping collaboration across academia, industry, and government. Yet, differing views on rigor, relevance, and success pose challenges and opportunities. This panel unites leaders from all 3 sectors to explore how to leverage strengths, navigate tensions, and co-create impactful solutions. Panelists will share insights on building trust, transparency, and innovation, offering practical strategies for sustainable, high-impact partnerships in an AI-driven world.

This symposium examines moral injury as a values-based trauma mechanism linking organizational failures to employee harm. Authors show that moral transgressions and organizational dehumanization function as potentially morally injurious events. These events elicit destructive moral emotions (e.g., shame), which, when amplified by psychological proximity or an antiwork orientation, accelerate strain and reduce organizational commitment. These studies provide novel, evidence-based mechanisms for researchers and practitioners to address moral hazards embedded in contemporary work life.

Rethinking Fit, Judgment, and Responsibility in SEM [Panel] Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Structural equation modeling (SEM) is a common tool in organizational science because it unites theory, latent variables, and complex designs in one framework. Yet its popularity can come with recurring problems: weak reporting, overfit “pretty” models, cross-sectional designs interpreted casually and thin handling of missing data and power. This panel brings methodologists and applied researchers together to discuss (a) where SEM uniquely advances organizational theory (b) common missteps and pragmatic fixes and (c) additional design and argument clarification for causal claims and replication.

Breaking the Continuum: Addressing Bullying and Sexual Harassment for Young Entrants in Research and Innovation. Cassino, Y. B. (2026, April 30 - May 2). Part-Time Youth Labor: Harassment, Problems, and Solutions. Liang, Y., Vranjes, I., Kim, S., McCauley, R. & Ponce, L. (2026, April 30 - May 2). Protecting Young Workers from Sexual Harassment: Fostering Effective Bystander Intervention. Mishra, V., Davison, H. K., Stewart, S. M., Fiorentino, S. R. & Schreiber, J. (2026, April 30 - May 2). Evaluating the Effectiveness of Sexual Harassment Training for Young Workers. Hitchener, M. (2026, April 30 - May 2). Supporting Young Workers in Retail: A Practical Framework for Safety, Development, and Engagement. Sexual harassment has been addressed in company training and policies, but the focus has often been on adult workers, whereas sexual harassment targeted towards young workers has been a hidden risk that needs to be evaluated. This symposium focuses on the psychological, physical, and behavioral effects on young victims. Presenters will share empirical, theoretical, and practical results using a variety of methodologies (i.e., surveys, interviews, videos), settings (i.e., high schools, universities, businesses) and countries (i.e., America, Australia, UK). A discussant will review the papers.

title: A Panel About Panels: New Trends in Crowdsourced Research Panels (Session ID 398)
speakers: Manuel Gonzalez | I-Heng (Ray) Wu | Aaron Moss | Bradley Pitcher | Melissa Keith | Richard Landers | Louis Tay
speaker_affiliations: Montclair State University | University of New Mexico- Anderson School of Management | CloudResearch | APTMetrics | Bowling Green State University | Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology (APA Div #14) | Purdue University
description: Gonzalez, M. F. (Co-Chair), Wu, I.-H. (Co-Chair), Ravid, D. (Co-Chair), Keith, M. G., Landers, R. N., Moss, A. J., Pitcher, B. D., & Tay, L. (2026). A Panel About Panels: New Trends in Crowdsourced Research Panels [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Crowdsourced research platforms have gained prominence in organizational research. However, both the research landscape and technologies are continually evolving, which may in various ways impact how I-Os should use these platforms to ensure data quality. A panel of 5 researchers—representing academia, industry, and internal platform work—is convened to discuss what new opportunities and challenges may emerge with crowdsourcing platforms. In doing so, the session will contribute to continued, creative, and rigorous research using these platforms.

title: Applications of Different Modifications of the Angoff Approach for Cut Score Setting (Session ID 448)
speakers: Dr. Paul D. DeKoekkoek | Lorin Mueller | Craig Reddock | Susie Nugent | Yin Lin
speaker_affiliations: SHL | Healthcare Regulatory Research Institute | Amazon | American Nurses Association
description: DeKoekkoek, P. (Chair), Mueller, L. (Discussant). (2026). Applications of Different Modifications of the Angoff Approach for Cut Score Setting [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Nugent, S., Zhang, X., Li, I., & Myers, T. (2026). Applying the Rasch Measurement Theory to Evaluate Judgments in Standard Setting. Reddock, C. & Tomeh, D. (2026). Setting Score Bands for a Technical Assessment Using Angoff Ratings. Lin, Y., Beazley, C., & DeKoekkoek, P. (2026). From Complex Models to Clear Decisions: Proficiency Banding in IRT-Based Assessments of Behavioral Skills. With an increased focus on skills, criterion-referenced cut scores set using the Angoff approach offer a way for organizations to make hiring decisions based on whether a candidate has a skill. The Angoff approach has limitations. This symposium discusses modifications to the Angoff approach to address different limitations like unexplained variance in ratings and SMEs underestimating the difficulty of items. The approach can also be modified for soft skills. This session will be informative to individuals unfamiliar with criterion-referenced cut score setting and experienced practitioners.

title: Beyond AI Angst: Early I-O Adopters Share Experiences and Perspectives (Session ID 455)
speakers: Julian Allen | Eric Sydell | Jürgen Bank | Jared Weintraub | Riley Morrison
speaker_affiliations: Alnylam Pharmaceuticals | Vero AI | FoundrsFuel | Deloitte | MiTek
description: Allen, J., Bank, J., Eatough, E., Morrison, R. Sydell, E. and Weintraub, J. (2025). Beyond AI Angst: Early I-O Adopters Share Experiences and Perspectives [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, CO, United States of America As of October 2025, 94% of US workers use AI to get work done, and yet 77% of them suffer from AI angst - broad concerns over job security, privacy, lack of confidence, and loss of control - which stifles the potential value of GenAI. The Panelists - self-identified GenAI Early adopters - will share their experiences across large, medium, and small enterprises, discussing practical strategies and experiences across industries. The session aims to inspire curiosity, encouragement and confidence for using GenAI and to equip I-Os to lead effective and responsible implementations in the workplace.

title: Brains First, Bots Second: Lessons Learned From AI-Assisted Qualitative Analysis (Session ID 147)
speakers: Makai Ruffin | Leilani Seged | Maria Lytell | Teresa Ober
speaker_affiliations: Rice University | RAND Corporation | ETS
description: Ruffin, M. A., Lytell, M., Ober, T., & Seged, L. (2026, April 30 - May 2). Brains first, bots second: Lessons learned from AI-assisted qualitative analysis [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology 41st Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial intelligence (AI) tools are widely adopted across academia and practice to assist with qualitative data analysis. Experienced research practitioners in industrial-organizational and educational psychology will discuss project, personnel, and social considerations driving their decisions to incorporate AI into their qualitative analysis workflow. Attendees will gain insights into where panelists have benefited from using AI as well as unexpected challenges. This session will offer guidance for researchers navigating the evolving landscape of AI-assisted qualitative analysis.

title: Fact or Fiction? Testing What You Know About Training Evaluation (Session ID 1391)
speakers: Amy DuVernet | Alyssa Kaszycki | Dr. Eric Surface
speaker_affiliations: Training Industry, Inc. | ALPS Insights
description: DuVernet, A. M. (Chair), Kaszycki, A., Surface, E. A.. (2026, April). Fact or Fiction? Testing What You Know About Training Evaluation. [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. How much do you really know about evaluating learning and development? In this interactive, game-show-style session, participants will test their knowledge of training evaluation myths. Using live polling, we’ll challenge common misconceptions about training evaluation methods, models and metrics, and share research that refutes them. Participants will have the opportunity to benchmark against lay L&D professionals’ knowledge of training evaluation and will walk away with evidence-based strategies to enhance their own evaluation practices and better demonstrate L&D impact.

title: Future-Ready Leaders: Build Them or Buy Them? (Session ID 733)
speakers: Jessie McClure | Christa Bupp | Jaclyn Menendez | Kyla Holcombe | Lauren Wallace
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessments | Visa | Google
description: McClure, J., Bupp, C., Holcombe, K., Menendez, J., & Wallace, L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Future-ready leaders: Build them or buy them? [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As businesses navigate accelerated change, the concept of "future-ready leadership" has emerged as a critical capability. This panel explores a fundamental question facing talent management professionals: Can future-ready leadership competencies be developed through targeted interventions, or do they represent relatively stable individual differences that organizations must identify and select for? Panelists will draw from research on leadership development, personality psychology, and change management to reach a conclusion on whether future-ready leadership should be bought or built.

title: Immigrants in the Workplace: New Directions for Research and Practice (Session ID 858)
speakers: Ana-Gabriela Osorio | Jo Alanis | Lisa Baranik | Thamengie Richard | Tatiana Guevara
speaker_affiliations: University of Georgia | Rice University | University at Albany, SUNY | Auburn University
description: Richard, T. (Co-chair), Osorio, A.-G. (Co-Chair), Robertson, M. (Co-Chair), Alanis, J. M. (Panelist), Baranik, L. E. (Panelist), Guevara, T. (Panelist). (2026, April 30 - May 2). Immigrants in the Workplace: New Directions for Research and Practice [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Immigrants remain an overlooked population in I-O psychology, despite being vital to the global workforce. This session brings together experts studying immigrant, refugee, and undocumented worker experiences to discuss research on barriers to employment and equity. After brief presentations on the state of the science, attendees will participate in breakout discussions to generate research questions for advancing immigrant inclusion in organizations. This session will serve as an introduction to immigrant research and a space for scholars to generate new ideas and develop new collaborations.

title: Intersection Between Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Disability in the Workplace (Session ID 488)
tracks: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Aaron Tay | Eduardo Velazco | Felix Wu | Michelle (Mikki) Hebl | Joshua Prasad | Laura Barron
speaker_affiliations: Operational Technologies | Alterman Inc. | HumRRO | Rice University | Colorado State University | US Air Force
description: Velazco, E. (Co-chair), Tay, A. (Co-chair), Hebl, M., Prasad, J., Wu, Y.F., Willis, C., Laura, G.B., Intersection between artificial intelligence (AI) and disability in the workplace [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States of America. This panel will present research and applied experience on how artificial intelligence currently impacts disability inclusion in the workplace. Exploration of its interaction will include selection, accommodations/accessibility, and job engagement. Topics will also include the future of AI and its impacts, organizational implementation, and potential dangers of AI. This panel will examine how mental/cognitive disabilities, as well as physical disabilities, are affected, provide practical guidance, and discuss current research to assess the current state of interaction and its direction.

title: Leadership/Leader Development; Occupational Health/Work and Family; Groups/Teams; Selection/Assessment/Individual Differences Posters
tracks: Poster | Leadership/Leader Development | Occupational Health/Work and Family | Groups/Teams | Selection/Assessment/Individual Differences

title: Machine Learning for I-O 8.0: Pushing the LLM Application Frontier (Session ID 782)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Putka, D. J., Liu, J., Wu, F., & Lewis, P. (2026). How well do LLMs compare to experts for identifying personality-related job requirements? Speer, A. B., Asay, S., Lee, J., Hong, Q., & Perrotta, J. (2026). Development of a comprehensive collection of biographical inventory items using automatic item generation. Sylvara, A., Seitz, C., Wang, P., Sun, T., Hickman, L., & Lievens, F. (2026). Automating assessment center role-play exercises: Development of an artificial intelligence chatbot Thompson, I., Zolaktaf, Z., & Kitching, M. (2026). Scalable LLM evaluations. Campion, E. D., Wang, Y., Yu, J., & Fan, W. (2026). Augmenting career counseling: A coaching agent to identify careers options and opportunities for development. The emergence of large language models (LLMs) presents many opportunities for industrial-organizational psychology research and practice. However, research has been limited to its application in the selection and assessment domain. This symposium presents 5 papers that research and apply LLMs in a novel set of research and practice scenarios, including job analysis, biodata item generation, assessment centers, LLM evaluation for interview notes, and career counseling.

title: Next Evolution of HR: Forging Closer Relationships With IT (Session ID 845)
tracks: I-O Psychologist Career Development/Teaching | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Alexis Fink | Kerya Ball | Ketaki Sodhi | Matthew Lampe | Megan Steckler | Rica Young | Tyler Weeks
speaker_affiliations: Propeller Insight, LLC | Kroger | Microsoft | Business Oregon | Perceptyx, Inc. | Uber | Marriott International
description: Fink, A. A., Ball, K., Brown, M., Lampe, M., Sodhi, K., Steckler, M., Weeks, T, & Young, F. (2026). Next evolution of HR: Forging closer relationships with IT. Panel discussion presented at the 41st Annual Conference of the Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology, New Orleans, Louisiana. There’s an emerging trend in HR and People Analytics teams: we’re seeing tighter connections between these functions and IT. This practitioner-focused panel brings together professionals who are navigating these new, closer relationships - through accountability that spans these functions in a variety of ways. Each panelist will briefly share how IT and HR have come together in their organizations, followed by a group discussion of trends, successes and pitfalls.

title: Optimizing Quality of Hire: Insights From Best-In-Class Fortune 500 Companies (Session ID 326)
speakers: Talya Bauer | Maged Natanios | Maggie Collins | Steven Hunt
speaker_affiliations: Portland State University | Marriott International | Delta Air Lines | Crosschq
description: Hunt, S.T., Bauer, T., Collins, B., Gleber, K, & Natanios, M. (2025). Optimizing quality of hire: insights from best-in-class Fortune 500 companies. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Authors discuss a new measure of corporate quality of hire developed and validated using publicly available data on new hire outcomes and corporate financial performance. They hear from 3 organizations with exceptionally high scores on this metric. Marriot International, Delta Airlines and Expedia Group discuss aspects of their company culture, job design, recruiting practices, and onboarding methods that contribute to their exceptional quality of hire outcomes. The panel concludes with insights from a leading researcher on methods for capturing new hire value.

title: Practitioner Best Practice Sharing Event
description: Join experienced practitioners from a wide variety of organizations to learn about their best practices in areas like employee listening, AI, performance management, leadership development, and analytics. Come prepared with a specific issue you’d like to discuss, or simply ask questions to learn more about how those companies manage these topics, make decisions, and benchmark. Participants will join small group table discussions and will be able to move freely between tables to learn about multiple topics. This event is designed for I-O practitioners with 5+ years of full-time experience.

title: Pragmatic Best Practices: Balancing Practicality and Rigor in Applied Qualitative Work (Session ID 647)
speakers: Jesse Caylor | Emilija Kovacevic | Ellen Hagen | Hemang Vaidya | Kelly Dray | Nicole Baker | Vanessa Gaskins | Xin Wei Ong | Virginia Nasrallah
speaker_affiliations: Procter & Gamble | Spellman High Voltage Electronics Corporation | Phillips 66 | The University of Akron | Lennar | University of Akron | The University of Akron Graduate School · Part-time
description: Vaidya, H. (Chair), Dziuba-Liu, S.-N., C (Co-Chair), Ong, X. W. (Co-Chair), Nasrallah, V. (Co-Chair), Dray, K., Caylor, J., Gaskins, V., Hegan, E., Baker, N. (2026). Pragmatic best practices: Balancing practicality & rigor in applied qualitative work [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Qualitative methods are innovative and underused tools for I-O psychologists to address organizational challenges in both research and practice. They are especially valuable for capturing employee experiences and uncovering key processes, though their use often involves trade-offs due to organizational constraints. This session brings together 6 I-O psychologists with practitioner and research experience for a combined panel and roundtable discussion on balancing rigor and practicality when applying these methods in real-world settings.

title: Product Showcase: Deloitte - Agentic Change: Using AI to Personalize Adoption and Elevate Human Leadership
speakers: Chloe Domergue
speaker_affiliations: Deloitte
description: Organizations invest in transformation, yet adoption stalls because change approaches are static and disconnected from how people work. Domergue’s work introduces a “new change paradigm” and an agentic change model that embeds AI agents into the flow of work to deliver personalized nudges and adoption interventions grounded in behavioral science. These agent networks detect friction points, tailor support to motivation and context, and adapt based on response. The goal is not to replace change leaders, but to augment them. By automating behavioral interventions at scale, agentic systems free practitioners to focus on higher-value work: aligning leaders, strengthening relationship capital, shaping employee experience, and making context-sensitive decisions. Use cases will be woven into the dialogue, showing application in regulated environments while preserving trust, engagement, and psychological safety. Rooted in I-O principles such as reinforcement and job design, this approach reframes change as a human–AI partnership designed to build adaptive organizations.

title: SIOP Executive Board: Practitioner Needs Workshop: Cocreating SIOP Support for Practitioners (Session ID 1442)
tracks: I-O Psychologist Career Development/Teaching | Special Session
speakers: Amanda Valone | Heather Flattery | Jackie Martin Kowal | Kelsey Byerly | Dr. William Shepherd
speaker_affiliations: PDRI by Pearson | PDRI | Elevation Leadership, LLC
description: Martin Kowal, J. (Chair), Shepard, W., Byerly, K. R., Valone, A, & Flattery, H. (2025). Practitioner needs workshop: Co-creating SIOP support for practitioners [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This interactive working session invites attendees to directly shape how SIOP supports its practitioner community, with a critical focus on early career practitioners who are known to be at a higher risk of attrition. The session will begin with a brief overview of the current efforts from SIOP’s Professional Practice Committee. The remaining time will be dedicated to small group discussions where attendees are invited to share their suggestions on what they need and want from SIOP. Insights gathered will be synthesized and shared with the Executive Board to directly inform future strategy.

title: Shaping Work in the Modern Era: Expanding Perspectives on Job Crafting Across Context (Session ID 901)
speakers: Dr. Lucille S. Headrick | Zhonghao Wang | Cort Rudolph
speaker_affiliations: San Jose State University | University of Houston-Downtown | Wayne State University
description: Headrick, L. (Co-Chair), Wang, Z. (Co-Chair) & Rudolph, C.W. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Shaping work in the modern era: Expanding perspectives on job crafting across contexts. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As contemporary work is transformed by technological innovation, evolving identities, and heightened performance demands, employees increasingly engage in job crafting to align their work with personal and organizational needs. This symposium integrates 3 papers examining generative AI use in job crafting, relational crafting amid stigmatized identity management, and demands crafting under accountability. Collectively, these studies advance theoretical understanding of job crafting as a mechanism of proactive adaptation, self-regulation, and meaningful work construction in dynamic contexts.

title: Standardizing Excellence: Change Strategies for High-Skill Environments (Session ID 42)
tracks: Organizational Processes/Development/Culture | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Cheryl Jackson | Robert Ulrich
speaker_affiliations: GP Strategies | Gibson, Inc.
description: Jackson, C.L. & Ulrich, R.M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Standardizing Excellence: Change Strategies for High-Skill Environments [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In companies where craftsmanship, pride, and autonomy are deeply embedded in the culture, introducing standard work creates a paradox: driving consistency without losing the artistry that defines the work. This Master Tutorial, for intermediate to advanced audiences, explores that tension through Gibson Guitars, a legacy manufacturer known for artisanal quality and long-standing tradition of excellence. Attendees will gain a people-centered framework for aligning operational excellence with culture to drive behavior change and consistency in technically complex, high-skill environments.

title: Talent Assessment in the Age of AI: Research, Challenges, and Solutions (Session ID 634)
speakers: Matthew Bennett | Nidhal Mazza | McKenzie Specht | Yashna Shah
speaker_affiliations: SHL | Texas A&M University | Amazon
description: Jenkins, D., Bennett, M., Mazza, N., Shah, Y. J., & Specht, M. (Chair). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Talent Assessment in the Age of AI: Research, Challenges, and Solutions [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session brings together multiple perspectives on the implications of Generative AI (GenAI) for talent assessment, offering both research insights and practical guidance. Speakers will share lessons from recent studies and applied projects examining how AI interacts with assessment content, scoring models, and candidate behaviors. By integrating evidence from both research and applied practice, this session equips I-O practitioners and assessment professionals with actionable insights for maintaining effectiveness in assessments in an AI-driven landscape.

title: The Elephant (and Donkey) in the Room: National Politics and the World of Work (Session ID 641)
speakers: Ian Hughes | Nathan Bowling | Denise Vesper | Lindsay Denecker | Xiao Xiao

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Hughes, I. M., Bowling, N. A., Keith, M. G., Brossoit, R. M., & Bazzoli, A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The work-related consequences of employees’ political media consumption. Vesper, D. & Zacher, H. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Uncertain jobs, certain votes? Examining the relations of job insecurity, union attitudes and political behavior. Denecker, L. & Zickar, M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Speaking up or staying silent? The role of political ideology fit in employee voice behavior. Xiao, X., Arena Jr., D. F., Landay, K., & Smith, S. G. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Political ideology dissimilarity, conflicts, and incivility in organizations. This intermediate-level symposium examines how national politics and work intersect, highlighting both politics? Work and work? Politics spillover effects. Across diverse samples and methods, the papers explore how political media, job insecurity, and ideological (mis)fit shape employee attitudes and behaviors. Together, they underscore politics as a persistent, ambient force that influences well-being, workplace dynamics, and organizational life.

title: The Future of Management Development (Session ID 171)
speakers: Mariangela Battista | Anna Tavis | Gena Cox | Jane Wu | Meisha-Ann Martin | Sheri Feinzig
speaker_affiliations: Sirena Consulting LLC | NYU | Feels Human, LLC | IBM | Workhuman | Marsh McLennan
description: Battista, M., Martin, M., Wu, J., Tavis, A., Cox, G., Feinzig, S. (2026, April 30-May 2). The Future of Management Development. Panel Discussion. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The world of work has greatly shifted and with that so has the role of a manager. It is no longer enough to manage workflow, achieve results through others, gather resources, and develop team members. Today managers also need to navigate complex challenges like cost pressures, dwindling team morale and the relentless need for organizational growth. And yet this still does not fully describe what managers face on a daily basis. This panel will discuss what the future of management development will entail and end with a call to action for researchers and practitioners.

title: The Impact of GenAI on Interviewing Programs: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly (Session ID 816)
speakers: Jamie Winter | Kate LaPort | Nathan Mondragon | Nick Koenig | Tyler Mirando
speaker_affiliations: APTMetrics | Amazon | ProboTalent | Walmart | Citizens Bank
description: Winter, J. (Chair), Koenig, N., LaPort, K., Mirando, T., Mondragon, N., Reburn, Z., (2026). The Impact of GenAI on Interviewing Programs: The good, the bad and the ugly [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The impact of GenAI on talent processes is a hot topic. A common target of AI transformation is interviewing programs. Join the panel of experts with extensive experience developing and implementing interviewing programs for an exciting discussion of the most recent applications of GenAI on interviewing programs, where NOT to apply GenAI, best practices leveraging GenAI to enhance your interviewing process; and critical research questions I-O psychologists need to address regarding GenAI and interviewing.

title: Understanding Alcohol's Impact on Work and Well-Being (Session ID 595)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Garcia, S. C., Calderwood, C., Wiese, C. W. & Sarkar, N. (2026). Work recovery and employee alcohol use: A theoretical synthesis and systematic review. Nguyen, D., Figueroa-Negrón, A. S., Paradiso, E., Klinefleter, Z., & Key-Roberts, M. (2026). Leader behaviors and unit climates as preventative levers for alcohol abuse in the army. Allen, S. J. & Swanberg, J. E. (2026). Mitigating the impact of perpetrator threats at work: Coworker empowerment buffers threat-driven coping with alcohol and drugs. Du, Q. (2026). The paradox of workplace drinking: how newcomer alcohol use builds and undermines supervisory trust. Employee alcohol use is a persistent and complex topic within organizational science. This symposium presents new theoretical and empirical research that advances understanding of alcohol's multifaceted role in the workplace. The papers explore alcohol's intersection with work recovery processes, how leadership and climate can mitigate alcohol abuse in high-stress contexts, the buffering effect of coworker support on maladaptive coping, and the paradoxical function of social drinking in newcomer socialization and trust formation.

title: Walking a Tightrope: Navigating the Line Between Personal Expression and Professional (Session ID 1176)
speakers: Luiza Flores | Shelby Davis | Arturia Melson-Silimon | Silvia Bonaccio | Christine Nittrouer
speaker_affiliations: Colorado State University | Virginia Tech | University of Ottawa | Texas Tech University
description: Flores, L. (Co-Chair), Davis, S. N. (Co-chair), French, K. (Co-Chair), Melson-Silimon, M., Nittrouer, C., Chawla, N., Bonaccio, S. (2026). Walking a Tightrope: Navigating the line between personal expression and professionalism. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores how employees navigate the tension between personal expression and professionalism in increasingly diverse workplaces. Panelists will address strategies such as code-switching and masking, the influence of organizational norms like professionalism and the “ideal worker,” and the boundaries employees draw between personal and professional life. Together, the discussion highlights the need to challenge existing norms, broaden boundary research beyond work–family, and identify future directions for inclusive organizational practices.

title: Whose Classroom Is It Anyway? Reflecting on Dos and Don’ts of Experiential Learning (Session ID 318)
speakers: Kristie Campana | Amanda Barnes | Bree Cox | Jacob Drexler | Paulina Lopez | Zachary Baker-Salmon
speaker_affiliations: Minnesota State University, Mankato | University of Maryland, Baltimore | JupitHer Consulting · Self-employed | Schaefer Center for Public Policy, University of Baltimore | University of Baltimore | City of Baltimore Department of Human Resources
description: King, K., Lopez, P., Baker-Salmon, Z., Barnes, A. N., Cox, B., Drexler, J. & Campana, K.L. (Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Whose Classroom Is It Anyway? Reflecting on Do’s and Don’ts of Experiential Learning [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores the Classroom-as-Organization model, an experiential learning approach in which courses are structured to function like organizations. Student panelists who participated in a graduate-level course will share first-hand reflections on the benefits, challenges, and lessons learned from this method. By discussing both “do’s” and “don’ts,” the session provides practical guidance, highlights common pitfalls, and encourages faculty to consider how this approach can enrich teaching and learning.

title: Words at Work: Applications and Best Practices of Archival Text Data in I-O Psychology (Session ID 946)
speakers: Michael Campion | Hanyi Min | Juan Madera | Lauren Landon | Lia Crowley
speaker_affiliations: Purdue University | University of Houston | NASA (KBR) | Baruch College/The Graduate Center
description: Crowley, L. M. (Chair), Fleyshmakher, D. (Co-Chair), Aronson, S. (Co-Chair), Cohen-Charash, Y. (Co-Chair), Campion, M., Dwivedi, P., Landon, L. B., Madera, J. M., Min, H., & Pyram, R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Words at Work: Applications and Best Practices of Archival Text Data in I-O Psychology [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. A data explosion in the past decade has created opportunities for I-O psychologists to leverage archival text as a data source for research. Archival text research draws on data originally collected for reasons other than answering a researcher's current research question. In I-O psychology, such sources range from job reviews to work emails to social media posts. These sources offer unique strengths and weaknesses. In this session, authors will showcase how I-Os have used these sources to study work, highlighting opportunities for future research and providing guidance for navigating limitations.

title: A Question of Conscience: Clarifying Moral Harms at Work (Session ID 603)
speakers: Spencer Garcia | Joel Lefkowitz | Logan Watts | Rodney L. Lowman | Dr. Shane Connelly
speaker_affiliations: Georgia Institute of Technology | Baruch College and the Graduate Center, CUNY | University of Texas at Arlington | Lowman & Richardson/Consulting Psychologists, PC | University of Oklahoma
description: Garcia, S. C. (chair), Wiese, C. W., Connelly, S., Lefkowitz, J., Lowman, R., & Watts, L. L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). A question of conscience: Clarifying moral harms at work [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Moral and ethical challenges are a prevalent source of workplace stress that harm well-being, negatively impact job attitudes, and predict employee attrition. This panel of experts will discuss what moral harm is, its effects on employees, and how organizations and I-O psychologists can address related challenges. The goal is to build the audience's understanding of the nature and dynamics of these challenges in the workplace and how they may be addressed.

title: Awards: Experience to Evidence: Reflections on a Work–Family Research Career (Session ID 1470)
tracks: Occupational Health/Work and Family | Special Session
speakers: Tammy Allen
speaker_affiliations: University of South Florida
description: My early experiences as a first-generation college student and graduate student parent offered a lens that shaped a research program spanning work-family issues, mentoring/career development, and workplace flexibility. This talk reflects on how experience and evidence informed one another and what these insights mean for the future of creating supportive, inclusive work systems.

title: Being Significant With Nonsignificant Data (Session ID 1151)
speakers: Leann Caudill | Melanie Standish | Ron Landis
speaker_affiliations: Coastal Carolina University | Lenovo | Wilbur O. and Ann Powers College of Business at Clemson University · Full-time
description: Caudill, L. E. (Co-Chair), Standish, M. P. (Co-Chair), & Landis R. S. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Being significant with nonsignificant data. [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-Os spend countless hours theorizing and designing research studies in hopes of shedding light on interesting workplace phenomena. But what happens when results are “nonsignificant?” I-Os might need to think outside the box because contributions can still be significant. This alternative session will provide numerous examples of how to move forward with nonsignificant data. Attendees will organize into small discussion groups for most of the session to share experiences with null results to find paths forward, in hopes of fostering collaboration and accountability.

title: Beyond the Episode: Considering Enduring Forms of Emotions at Work (Session ID 413)
speakers: Ami Shah | Manuel Gonzalez | Nikitha Mohan | Paul Spector | Heewon Jang | Renee Leger | Sara Stavely
speaker_affiliations: Montclair State University | Tampa General Hospital | George Mason University | Baruch College / Graduate Center | The Port Authority of New York & New Jersey
description: Gonzalez, M. F. (Co-Chair), Mohan, N. S. (Co-Chair), Shah, A. (Co-Chair), & Spector, P. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Beyond the episode: Considering enduring forms of emotions at work [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Jang, H., Kaplan, S., Kuykendall, L., Liao, X., & Maguire, L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). When the Weekend Ends: How Sunday Affective Thoughts Shape Monday Work. Stavely, S. M., & Gray, E. C. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Grief at Work: A Systematic Review of Bereavement and Organizational Support. Leger, R., & Cohen-Charash, Y. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Forgiving and Forgetting? Easier Said than Done: Hate as a Sentiment at Work Research on emotions has continued to be prominent over the last few decades; however, most of it focuses on episodic emotions, moods, and affective traits. This symposium highlights 3 research papers investigating affect, extending beyond brief episodes and dispositional traits. The papers explore emotional spillover to new contexts, transformation of short-term emotions into chronic affective patterns, and the conceptualization of emotions as sentiments. Collectively, the current symposium illustrates exciting new research avenues for studying affect in organizations.

title: Creativity Meets Large Language Models: Generative AI in Creativity Research (Session ID1061)
speakers: Alisha Silkey | Bao Ho | Melissa Keith | Alexis d'Amato | Ares Boira Lopez | Austin Swanson
speaker_affiliations: Bowling Green State University | McKinsey and Company | University of Nebraska, Omaha | University of Nebraska, National Counterterrorism Innovation, Technology and Education (NCITE) Center | University of Nebraska at Omaha
description: Ho, B. G. (Co-Chair), Silkey, A. J. (Co-Chair), & Keith, M. G. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Creativity meets Large Language Models: Generative AI in creativity research [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Silkey, A. J., Ho, B. G., & Keith, M. G. (2026, April 30 – May 2). An interpretable AI approach to human vs. AI-generated responses to creativity tasks. Swanson, A., Linnell, A., Laverghetta Jr., A., Luchini, S., Beaty, R., & Reiter-Palmon, R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Old tests, new tricks: Using LLMs to expand creativity assessment items Boira-Lopez, A., Theobald E., D’Amato, A., Hunter S., Rygg, J., & Elson, J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). When Generative AI helps and when it hurts: Sequencing and levels of analysis in creative problem solving. d’Amato, A. L., Theobald, E., Rygg, J., Elson, J. S., & Hunter, S. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Outside the guardrails: A dual-study exploration of AI-enhanced malevolent creativity. Creativity researchers are currently contending with whether and how large language models (LLMs) inform what I-Os know about human creativity. This symposium moves beyond the question of whether generative AI can be creative. Across 4 studies, the authors examine questions of whether human and AI creativity can be differentiated, how to leverage LLMs to assess creativity and enhance creative problem solving, and what guardrails should be in place considering the potential for malevolent creativity.

title: Demonstrating the Use of Escape Rooms as a Novel Research Method (Session ID 391)
speakers: Takudzwa Chawota | Nissa Gongora | Steven Zhou
speaker_affiliations: Wayne State University | Claremont McKenna College
description: Zhou, S., Chawota, T. A., Gongora, N., & Dickson, M. W. (2026, April 29 – May 2). Demonstrating the use of escape rooms as a novel research method [Alternative session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This alternative session provides a live, interactive demonstration of an escape room used as a novel research method for data collection on topics related to leadership, teams, motivation, and strategic planning. Escape rooms, which became popular in recent years, are real-life adventure games where teams of participants work together to solve puzzles in order to “escape” a simulated game. Authors argue that escape rooms offer a controlled environment to test I-O-related theories while maximizing participant engagement in a way that will attract people to participate in research studies.

title: Feedback Anonymity: Institutionalized Gossip or Useful Information? (Session ID 203)
speakers: Eric Marcus | Paul Levy | Laura Finfer | Nicole Baker | Steve Walker
speaker_affiliations: University of New Haven | The University of Akron:Department of Psychology | Leadership Excellence Consulting | University of Akron | Touro University
description: Levy, P. (Co-Chair), Marcus, E. (Co-Chair), Baker, N., Finfer, L., & Walker, S. (2027, April 30 – May 2). Feedback Anonymity: Institutional Gossip or Useful Information? [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Practitioners with internal and external consulting experience will be joined by subject matter experts from academia to review the tradeoffs of implementing anonymous and authored feedback mechanisms in the workplace. Use cases, best practices, and lessons learned will be shared by the panelists as a way to stimulate discussion with the audience. Relevant research will be discussed and presented where appropriate. The focus will be on what the current issues are as identified by the audience – this should be a lively, interactive, and fast-paced session.

title: From Chaos to Clarity: Navigating Leadership During Crisis (Session ID 64)
speakers: Sarah Synder | Tina Madani | Dan Fisher | Eileen Linnabery | Ginevra Drinka | Thor Legvold | Thaddeus Rada-Bayne
speaker_affiliations: San Diego State University | Contemporary Leadership Advisors | Vantage Leadership Consulting | Victoria’s Secret & Company | Legvold Consulting | Culture Amp
description: Madani, T. (Co-chair), Missler, C. (Co-chair), Snyder, S. (Co-chair), Rodriguez, W. A. (Co-chair), Weglarz, E. (Co-chair), Busse, K. (Co-chair), Drinka, G., Fisher, D., Legvold, T., Linnabery, E., & Rada-Bayne, T. (2026, April 30 - May 2). From chaos to clarity: Navigating leadership during crisis [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel brings together practitioners and researchers to explore evidence-based strategies for effective leadership during times of crisis. This will include topics such as fostering psychological safety, transparent communication, and adaptive decision making. Attendees will gain actionable insights to buffer stress, promote resilience, and strategies to guide teams successfully through periods of uncertainty and instability.

title: From Potential to Performance: Individual Differences That Enable AI Readiness (Session ID 126)
speakers: James Meaden | Julia King | Josh Allen | Julia Walsh | Kristin Allen | Tara McClure
speaker_affiliations: Founder @ The Alive Institute | SHL | Walmart | General Mills | Aon
description: McClure Johnson, T., King, J., Meaden, J., Walsh, J., Allen, J. (Discussant), & Allen, K. (Chair). (2026, April 30-May 2). From Potential to Performance: Individual Differences that Enable AI Readiness [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session brings together researchers and practitioners to explore the individual differences that shape readiness for thriving in the era of AI-enabled work. Drawing on evidence from academic research and applied studies, presenters will discuss what differentiates employees who excel in AI-enabled environments. A discussant will facilitate thought-provoking conversation, focused on practical implications for organizations. By bridging science and practice, this session aims to describe how to leverage research to unlock potential and drive performance in the AI-powered future of work.

title: Invisible Strains, Real Consequences: Breaking Barriers to Support Vulnerable Workers (Session ID 416)
speakers: Jacquelyn Brady | Shalene Allen | Hoda Vaziri | Jazmin Argueta-Rivera | Kaushik Ram | Marian Hernandez
speaker_affiliations: San Jose State University | Kansas State University | University of North Texas | Rice University
description: Allen, S. J. (Co-Chair), Brady, J. M. (Co-Chair), & Smith, N. A. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Invisible strains, real consequences: Breaking barriers to support vulnerable workers [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Workers experiencing vulnerability often face “invisible strains” rooted in stigma, marginalization, and personal hardship, leading to tangible consequences. This symposium examines how diverse forms of vulnerability influence workers’ experiences and how organizations can either mitigate or exacerbate harm. Together, these papers deepen understanding of how strains emerge and highlight the need to foster inclusive, safe, and supportive workplaces through theoretical and practical insights focused on creating a more equitable workforce.

title: LLMs-Based Methods for More Reliable and Efficient Structured Interviews (Session ID 744)
speakers: Georgi Yankov | Josh Liff | David Mayers | Isaac Thompson | Zihan Yan
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessments | HireVue | Knockri | Amazon | University of illinois
description: Yankov, G. P. (Chair) (2026). LLMs-based methods for more reliable and efficient structured interviews [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Structured interviews are strong predictors of job performance but exhibit substantial variability in validity. This symposium examines how large language models (LLMs) can reduce this variability by enhancing score reliability, optimizing interview length, and ensuring raters provide accurate, evidence-based feedback. The 4 studies address sources of rater noise, the relationship between interview length and its reliability, an IRT-inspired method for shortening interviews, and automated tools for verifying interviewer debriefs.

title: Leadership and the Human Capital Advantage in Private Equity (Session ID 932)
speakers: Robert Kaiser | Kevin Impelman | Matthew Lemming | Ryne Sherman
speaker_affiliations: Kaiser Leadership Solutions | Summit Leadership Partners | Hogan Assessments
description: Kaiser, R. B. (Chair) (2026). Leadership and the Human Capital Advantage in Private Equity [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Private equity (PE) is a huge and growing industry with over $15 trillion in assets under management globally. PE firms buy and grow companies to increase their value and sell them for a profit. It is an intensely competitive industry, with few ways for firms to differentiate themselves. However, one source of competitive advantage concerns human capital and talent management. This session features presentations that illustrate the application of the science of talent management and development, focused on leadership, in PE-backed companies.

title: Lightning Tutorial: How to (Actually) Influence and Drive Decisions With Data (Session ID 386)
speakers: Alyssa Marshall | Caitie Jacobson Mikulis | Morgan Depenbusch | Elisabeth Stocking
speaker_affiliations: Meta | Insight222 | Morgan Depenbusch LLC | Google
description: Stilson, F. R. B. (Chair), Depenbusch, M., Head, B, Marshall, A. D., Jacobson Mikulis, C., Stocking E. & Head, B. (2026). Lightning Tutorial: How to (Actually) Influence and Drive Decisions with Data. [Alternate Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This Lightning Tutorial equips I-O professionals with practical methods to move beyond “letting the data speak for itself” and truly influence decisions. Panelists will share research-grounded strategies and case studies on persuasion, credibility, storytelling, and networking, while demonstrating how generative AI can be used to practice these skills. Attendees will leave with actionable tools to translate insights into impact and drive organizational change.

title: Machine Learning for I-O 8.0: Large Language Models for Assessments (Session ID 812)
speakers: Louis Hickman | Mengqiao (MQ) Liu | Brent Stevenor | Sarah Nakamoto | Pengda Wang | Weiwen Nie | Charlene Zhang
speaker_affiliations: Amazon | NREMT | University of Minnesota | Rice University | Hogan Assessments
description: Liu, M. (Co-Chair), & Hickman, L. (Co-Chair). (2026). Machine learning for I-O 8.0: Large Language Models for Assessments [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Nakamoto, S., Lee, V., Cavanaugh, K. J., Bach-Lefler, I., & Landers, R. N. (2026). Multimodal LLM assessment of personality and job performance from social media. Li, W., McNeney, D., Barr, R., Prabhu, N., Zhang, C., Capman, J. F., & Boyce, A. S. (2026). Identifying requisition-specific knowledge, skills, and abilities with large language models. Wang, P., Hou, D., Sun, T., & Tay, L. (2026). Investigating the psychometric properties of multimodal large language model-based performance evaluation. Stevenor, B., Anyanwu, C., & McBride, N. L. (2026). Predicting item difficulty with AI. Nie, W. & Dabdoub, A. (2026). Less is more? Testing single-item and AI-based predictors of personality in short video interviews. The emergence of large language models (LLMs) presents many opportunities for industrial-organizational psychology research and practice. However, empirical research is limited regarding the capacity of LLMs to conduct various assessment-related tasks. This symposium presents 5 papers that research and apply LLMs to core problems of scoring constructs and scale development. The papers provide nuanced validity evidence for using LLMs to assess personality and vocational interests, extract competencies from job requisitions, and predict item difficulty.

title: Not Taught in Grad School: Realities of Internal and External Consulting (Session ID 1187)
speakers: Alyssa Green | Mounica Reddy | Jessica Zalewski | Samantha Getzen | Sydney Begerowski | Lauren Zervos
speaker_affiliations: Lotis Blue Consulting | PepsiCo | DICK'S Sporting Goods | APTMetrics | NASA (KBR) | CIBC
description: Reddy, M. (Co-Chair), Green, A. (Co-Chair), Zervos, L., Zalewski, J., Begerowski, S., & Getzen, S. (2026). Not taught in grad school: Realities of internal and external consulting [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This fast-paced panel aims to provide a “realistic job preview” of consulting careers for I-O psychologists, combining panelists’ experiences with evidence from professional practice. Panelists will contrast internal and external roles, reflect on what was learned on-the-job versus during graduate school, and share strategies for conveying the value of I-O psychology. This session is intended to provide graduate students and early-career professionals with insights to better navigate and shape their careers.

title: Pressure-Tested Engagement: What Keeps Manufacturing Workers Connected (Session Id 44)
speakers: Ashlyn Elick | Robert Ulrich
speaker_affiliations: GP Strategies Corporation | Gibson, Inc.
description: Jackson, C.L., Elick, J. A., McManus, A., Ulrich, R.M. (2026, April 30 - May2). Pressure-Tested Engagement: What Keeps Manufacturing Workers Connected [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Manufacturing workforces face unique engagement challenges, particularly when under pressure. This tutorial shares findings from GP Strategies’ engagement data and a Gibson Guitars case study to reveal what drives and sustains engagement when stress peaks. Intermediate-level attendees will explore key drivers of engagement and resilience, discover scalable strategies for front-line workers, and examine how embedded, institutionalized systems reinforce performance within an industrial workforce. The session bridges data, practice, and narrative psychology to drive lasting impact.

title: Rethinking Recovery: Novel Approaches to Recovery Experiences, Antecedents, Outcomes (Session ID 233)
speakers: Didar Zeytun | Ryan Grant | Allie Gabriel | Robert Ridder | Ze Zhu
speaker_affiliations: Penn State University | University of North Carolina at Charlotte | Purdue University, Mitch Daniels School of Business | Baylor University | Texas A&M University
description: Zeytun, D. (Co-Chair), Grant, R. (Co-Chair) & Gabriel, A. (Discussant). (2026). Rethinking Recovery: Novel approaches to recovery experiences, antecedents, and outcomes. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Recovery from work stressors has attracted increasing attention from both scientists and practitioners. This symposium presents novel approaches to understanding recovery processes by introducing new methods for their operationalization and objectively measuring both antecedents and outcomes.

title: Rethinking Retirement: HRM Retention Strategies for Sustaining Work Ability (Session ID 404)
speakers: Alexander Jackson | Donald Truxillo | Gigi Petery | Katrina Burch | Susanne Bruyere
speaker_affiliations: Middle Tennessee State University (MTSU) | Kemmy Business School, University of Limerick | Western Kentucky University | Cornell University
description: Petery, G. A. (Chair). (2026). Rethinking Retirement: HRM Retention Strategies for Sustaining Work Ability [IGNITE+ Panel Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session explores how longer lifespans and shifting demographics are reshaping retirement. Presenters examine outdated “cliff” retirement models, phased retirement policies, retention strategies for older workers, and work ability as a key late-career metric. Together, they highlight how organizations can design flexible policies and practices that retain talent, support well-being, and promote successful transitions to retirement.

title: Saving Time While Maintaining Technical Rigor: New Uses of AI/ML for People Analytics (Session ID 718)
speakers: Anoop Javalagi | Felix Wu | Fred Oswald | Charles Matthews | Nathaniel Voss
speaker_affiliations: HumRRO | University of California, Irvine | Appalachian State University | Human Resources Research Organization (HumRRO)
description: Wu, F. Y. (Co-Chair), & Javalagi, A. A. (Co-Chair) (2026). Saving Time while Maintaining Technical Rigor: New Uses of AI/ML for People Analytics [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This symposium examines artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) methods for optimizing personnel selection, measurement validation, and taxonomy development. Three studies showcase how these technologies deliver reliable and valid results with greater efficiency and scalability compared to conventional applied statistical approaches. The session features cutting-edge examples of empirically grounded and ethically responsible human-in-the-loop AI/ML integration in people analytics for intermediate to advanced audiences.

title: Situational Judgment Test Innovations by and for Humans: No AI Required (Session ID 556)
speakers: Harrison Kell | Michelle Martin-Raugh | Jeffrey Conway | Thomas Rockstuhl
speaker_affiliations: The University of Texas at Arlington | Roblox | Nanyang Business School, NTU
description: Kell, H. J. (Co-Chair), & Martin-Raugh, M. P. (Co-Chair). (2025). Situational judgment test innovations by and for humans: No AI required [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Amid growing enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) in assessment, this symposium highlights 5 advances in situational judgment testing that demonstrate the enduring value of human expertise. Each presentation illustrates how theory-driven design, empirical evidence, and psychological insight continue to advance assessment quality. The papers address construct fidelity, contextual validity, faking mitigation, candidate experience, and large-scale implementation, all without relying on AI. Together they show that meaningful innovation arises from thoughtful science, not automation.

title: Text to Talent: Practical Applications of LLMs for Evaluating Constructed Responses (Session ID 211)
speakers: Cameron Beazley | Gerianne De Klerk - van Someren | Kevin Rutherford | Matthew Bennett | Michael Fetzer | Ben Porr | Yin Lin
speaker_affiliations: SHL | OccPsyence Ltd. | Welliba | Aon | Harver
description: Beazley, C. (Chair), Bennett, M., de Klerk, G., M., Fetzer, Lin, Y., Porr, B., Rutherford, K. (2026, April 30 – May 2) Text to talent– Practical applications of LLMs for evaluating constructed responses [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Although the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) has exploded across industries, using LLMs for evaluating responses is a novel approach in talent assessment and talent development. There are open questions around the accuracy, fairness, and quality of the approach. This introductory IGNITE session showcases practical applications of using LLMs for evaluating a range of constructed response types. Presenters will outline real world case studies that investigate the accuracy, reliability, explainability, security, and fairness of LLM evaluation.

title: The I-O Edge: Supercharge Your Non-I-O Job With Applied Strategies (Session ID 356)
speakers: Breanna Thomas | Andre Hennig | Anthony Auriemmo | Xinyu (Judy) Hu | Tatana Olson
speaker_affiliations: SHL | National Basketball Association | Northern Trust | United States Navy
description: Thomas, B., Henning, A., Hu, J., Auriemmo, A., Olson, T. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The I-O Edge: Supercharge Your Non-I-O Job with Applied Strategies. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. During this exploratory session, panelists will share experiences, practical strategies, and expert perspectives on leveraging I-O psychology knowledge and skills in roles beyond the traditional I-O domain. The discussion will include insights into how to prepare for non-conventional I-O positions, exploring the mindset adjustments necessary when working in a non-traditional or I-O adjacent role, and examining how the value of the I-O skillsets may evolve in response to ongoing technological advancements within the industry.

title: The I-O Practitioner’s Playbook: Insights From Work in Elite Sports (Session ID1041)
speakers: Charles Scherbaum, Ph.D. | Gina Thoebes | Harold Goldstein | Isabel Bilotta | Meredith Damore | Nadine Maliakkal
speaker_affiliations: Baruch College | Arizona Cardinals | The Graduate Center & Baruch College, CUNY | Deutser | Elevate | Hogan Assessment Systems
description: Scherbaum, C. (Chair), Maliakkal, N. T., Thoebes, G. P., Goldstein, H., Bilotta, I., & Damore, M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The I-O practitioner’s playbook: Insights from work in elite sports [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will highlight the application of industrial/organizational (I-O) psychology to elite sports organizations. Panelists will discuss the unique context of working in elite sports, covering topics that range from working with elite athletes and coaches “on the field” to working “in the business” with front-office and other organizational staff. Panelists will emphasize the use of psychometrically sound assessments, share compelling research findings, and shed light on the evolving role of I-O psychology in this highly competitive, pressurized environment.

title: The Mind Behind the Machine: I-O Psychologists as Architects of AI Tools (Session ID 451)
speakers: Carolyn Kalafut | Evan Theys | Justin D. Purl | Kate LaPort | Nathan Hundley | Nicholas Martin
speaker_affiliations: Microsoft | Palo Alto Networks | Archer | Amazon | CodeSignal | Meta
description: Kalafut, C., Hundley, N., LaPort, K., Martin, N., Purl, J., & Theys, E. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The Mind Behind the Machine: I-O Psychologists as Architects of AI Tools [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As AI transforms people analytics, the role of I-O psychologists is increasingly complex and critical. This panel features experts who’ve led AI initiatives across organizations, discussing how I-O psychologists should be embedded in AI development to protect data integrity, uphold ethics, and ensure models reflect human behavior. Real-world examples will highlight successful collaborations with technical teams and the value of cross-disciplinary fluency.

title: Diversity & Inclusion Reception
location: Hilton NOLA Riverside: Grand Ballroom B
tracks: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility | Events & Receptions
description: Join us for a special reception honoring our Diversity & Inclusion Committee volunteers and celebrating a year of meaningful impact, mentorship, and partnership. This gathering is an opportunity to connect, reflect, and recognize the collective efforts that continue to strengthen and advance our community. We are also proud to feature poster presentations from the Diversifying I-O Psychology (DIP) Program Scholars and warmly welcome their mentors to join us in supporting and celebrating their work.

title: Practitioner Reception
location: Hilton NOLA Riverside: Grand Ballroom A
description: We invite you to a New Orleans–inspired meet-and-greet reception designed for practitioners to connect and celebrate. Honor this year’s practitioner award recipients while building relationships and engaging with colleagues in a relaxed, welcoming setting.

title: Workscapes 2.0
description: After a well-received inaugural exhibit at SIOP 2025, Workscapes is back! Workscapes, an art exhibition curated by I-Os and for I-Os, was designed to give attendees a restorative space to pause, recharge, and connect through creative expression amid the demands of conference week. Workscapes 2.0 builds on last year's event with more viewing time, greater visibility, and enhanced interactivity, deepening its commitment to Gentle SIOP values and the belief that celebrating the whole person is what makes our SIOP community stronger.

title: SIOP Talent Showcase
description: An evening celebrating creativity within our I-O community, featuring stand-up comedy, music, dance, poetry, and more. Whether you want to perform or simply enjoy the show, you’ll be part of a joyful celebration of the many talents that enrich our field. It’s not just a talent show, it’s a space for expression, connection, and remembering that I-O psychologists are creative humans too.

title: Continental Breakfast
General Conference Registration
Academic Assessments in An Era of Evolving Technology: Panel and Workshop (Session ID 956)
speakers: Alexandra Henderson | Afra Ahmad | Claire Smith | Joshua Prasad | Shiyang Su
speaker_affiliations: Zayed University | George Mason University | University of South Florida | Colorado State University | University of Central Florida
description: Henderson, A. A. (Chair), Ahmad, A. S., Prasad, J., Smith, C. E., & Su, S. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Assessments in an Era of AI and Evolving Technology: Panel and Workshop [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In an era of evolving technology, educators face a challenge to ensure learning and integrity while preparing students for technology integrated workplaces. This session brings together faculty and academic leaders to discuss how technologies are changing traditional assessment approaches followed by an interactive workshop in which participants will collaborate to design I-O assessments that both leverage and safeguard against technology. Attendees will leave with actionable strategies and access to a shared repository of ideas that can be used across academic and organizational settings.

title: Approaches to Conducting Leadership Research in the Military (Session ID 574)
speakers: Ann-Louise Holten | Benjamin Elliott | Ingrid Covington | Ivana Igic
speaker_affiliations: Forsvarsakademiet · Full-time | University of Minnesota | K U Leuven | Military Academy ETH Zurich
description: Euwema, M. C. (Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Approaches to Conducting Leadership Research in the Military [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Covington, I. K. & Igboanusi, G. (2026) From Isolation to Community: Inclusive Leadership in NATO. Holten, A.L, & O. H., Rahbek (2026) Political communication . Morf, M., Igic, I., Avolio, B & Inceoglu, I (2026) What Makes a Moment Matter? Rethinking Leadership Development Through the Eyes of Military Leaders. Elliott, B., Kuncel., N, (2026) Muddied Boots: Rater Source Effects in Military Academy Recommendation Letters. This symposium, Leadership Research in the Military, presents 4 complementary studies exploring contemporary leadership within complex defense contexts. Covington and Igboanusi examine an inclusive program at NATO SHAPE enhancing belonging and resilience through social identity and psychological safety. Holten and Rahbek analyze leadership as linguistic performance shaping legitimacy and collective identity. Morf, Igic, Avolio, and Inceoglu identify pivotal experiences driving leader development and adaptability. Elliott and Kuncel assess validity across leadership raters, revealing context-dependent predictors. Together, these studies redefine military leadership as adaptive, inclusive, and socially constructed.

title: Assessment Consulting Challenges: What Would You Do? (Session Id 955)
speakers: Emily Solberg | Jill May | Joseph Stewart | Kimberly Wrenn
speaker_affiliations: SHL | City of Chicago | The Home Depot
description: Solberg, E. (Chair), May, J., Stewart, J., Wrenn, K.A. (2026). Consulting Challenges: Assessment Edition - What Would You Do? [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will present challenging early career consulting scenarios regarding the use of assessments. Small groups will brainstorm and engage in group discussion as to how best to address the challenges. A panel of experienced consultants will then provide their advice and insight on each scenario. Topic areas discussed will include setting oneself up for success, getting results, and navigating challenges. This session is geared towards graduate students and others considering the consulting path, as well early career internal and external practitioners.

title: Building Assessment Center Stimuli With the Help of AI (Session ID 1264)
speakers: Dr. Gregory J. Pool | Gabriel Reyes
speaker_affiliations: St. Mary's University | St. Mary’s University
description: Reyes, G., & Pool, G. J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Building Assessment Center Stimuli with the Help of AI [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This interactive tutorial builds participant knowledge for integrating AI and Big Data into Assessment Center methodology using "vibe coding" approaches. Through hands-on demonstrations, participants learn to create browser-based simulations, collect behavioral traces using JavaScript event listeners, and integrate chat APIs. This accessible method enables I-O practitioners without programming backgrounds to modernize assessment practices.

title: Community of Interest: Evaluating Wellness at Work—Evidence, Privacy, and Care (Session ID 1454)
tracks: Occupational Health/Work and Family | Special Session
speakers: Jenna McChesney | Lauren Moran | Dr. Jacob Seybert | Sarah Haidar
speaker_affiliations: Meredith College | Georgia Institute of Technology | Roblox
description: Haidar, S., McChesney, J., Moran, L., & Seybert, J. ( 2026). Community of Interest: Evaluating Wellness at Work: Balancing Evidence, Privacy, and Care. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizations increasingly offer wellness benefits, ranging from yoga and mindfulness to nutrition programs, as a way to boost employee well-being and organizational outcomes. Yet evaluating the effectiveness of these initiatives raises sensitive challenges around data privacy, ethics, and cultural considerations. This Community of Interest session invites practitioners and academics to share experiences, frameworks, and strategies for responsibly assessing wellness programs while balancing rigorous evidence with employee trust and care.

title: Comparing Modern Qualitative Analysis Methods for Insights in Industry and Research (Session ID 1103)

Comparing modern qualitative analysis methods for insights in industry and research [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will contrast modern methods for securing insights from qualitative open-ended response data, including thematic analysis, content analysis, natural language process (NLP), semantic analysis, large language model generative artificial intelligence (LLM genAI), and a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) AI tool. Panelists from industry and academia will present use cases and discuss benefits and drawbacks of various approaches, as well as decisions made to balance efficiency and accuracy. Panel discussion and audience Q&A will take place after brief presentations of use cases.

P., Prather, H., Price, N., Squires, B., Stelman, S., & Winterberg, C. (2026, April 30 – May 2). DIY KSAOs 3.0: Career Exploration for I-O Graduate Students [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Back by popular demand, this alternative session will address the challenge of selecting an I-O career path by facilitating micro-mentoring conversations between experienced I-O professionals and graduate students. Specifically, the session will empower attendees to explore the necessary knowledge, skills, abilities, and other characteristics (KSAOs) for various I-O careers, identify their interests, develop strategies for skill development, and seek guidance from experienced professionals.

title: Do Great Teams SYNC Alike? Understanding Collective Constructs in Teams (Session ID 1429)
speakers: Janet Okoroji | Tyree Mitchell | Mikhail Wolfson | Caroline Deal | Nale Lehmann-Willenbrock
speaker_affiliations: Louisiana State University | University of Kentucky | University of South Florida | University of Hamburg
description: Okoroji, J. C. (Co-Chair), Mitchell, T. D. (Co-Chair), & Wolfson, M. (Discussant). (2026). Do Great Teams SYNC alike? Understanding Collective Constructs in Teams [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Much of the team and leadership research has relied on limited static methods to measure team interactions; however, this limits how I-Os understand the temporal development and measurement of collective constructs (e.g., affect, coordination, synchrony, and leadership). This symposium brings together 3 papers that explore understanding of team interactions and leadership through alternative approaches in methodology and theory, which can provide deeper insights on teams. The discussant will synthesize insights and provide recommendations for research and practice.

title: Examining Workplace Ostracism, Microaggression, and Pathways to Inclusiveness (Session ID 1141)
speakers: Cong Liu | Jun Michelle Yang | Huiwen Lian | Lindsay Pyc | Oyindamola Adebo | Vi Phung
speaker_affiliations: Rutgers University · Full-time | UNC at Greensboro | Texas A&M University | McKinsey & Company | Rutgers university
description: Liu, C. (Co-Chair), Yang, J. (Co-Chair) & Lian, H. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Examining workplace ostracism, microaggression, and pathways to inclusiveness [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Most research on workplace ostracism and microaggression has been conducted by academic scholars. However, these subtle forms of workplace mistreatment remain pressing issues that affect organizational health and productivity. This symposium, designed for both scientists and practitioners, brings together 4 presentations from academic and industry professionals to explore subtle forms of workplace mistreatment (e.g., task ostracism, ostracism toward managers, microaggression toward female physicians) and to highlight how organizations address exclusion and subtle forms of mistreatment, and to foster company-wide inclusiveness through global employee attitude surveys.

title: From Misuse to Best Use: Rethinking Assessments for Inclusive Talent Management (Session ID 937)
speakers: Damion Jones | Terence Bostic
speaker_affiliations: CMA Global, Inc. | CMA Global
description: Bostic, T. J., Gfeller, J. D., Jones D. S.., & Webster, I. D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). From misuse to best use: Rethinking assessments for inclusive talent management [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This intermediate-level tutorial examines common pitfalls in applying assessment data to talent management and offers evidence-based alternatives grounded in contemporary research. Authors highlight how misuse of assessments can unintentionally disadvantage historically marginalized groups and constrain organizational outcomes. Participants will gain practical strategies for leveraging assessments to advance equity, enhance selection decisions, and support the development of diverse talent pipelines.
