title: Into the Dark: Zombie Leadership, False Weapons, and You: Our Only Hope (Session ID 1420)
speakers: Alex Haslam | Jackie Sahm | Jocelyn Hays | Robert Kaiser
speaker_affiliations: University of Queensland | Hogan Assessments | Kaiser Leadership Solutions
description: Hays, J. (Chair), Haslam, A. (Panelist), Kaiser, R. (Panelist), Sahm, J. (Panelist), & Viswesvaran, V. (Panelist). (2026, April 30–May 2). Zombie leadership: Dead ideas that still walk among us [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will transport the audience through a cinematic journey of realization that people have been living in a zombie apocalypse. In Act I, Haslam unmasks the undead—executives who look, walk, and talk like leaders but are animated by infected practices. In Act II, Sahm cuts the lights: Executive performance data are nearly meaningless, leaving people in total darkness. Act III brings Kaiser with a plot twist— flimsy weapons are useless against the enemy. In Act IV, the audience joins the fight in a race against time to save the world from the zombie invasion.

title: LLMs for I-Os 2.0: A Features and Applications Masterclass (Session ID 207)
speakers: William Camden | Yizhen Egyn Zhu | Eva Riddervold Sandberg | Bao Ho | Karim Badr | Matthew Bennett
speaker_affiliations: SHL | Amazon | Aon | McKinsey and Company
description: Bennett, M. (Co-Chair), Badr, K. (Co-Chair), Camden, L., Ho, B., Riddervold Sandberg., E., & Zhu, Y.E. (2026, April 30 – May 2). LLMs for I-Os 2.0: A Features and Applications Masterclass [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The field of large language models (LLMs) is evolving rapidly, and many I-Os are still learning how to integrate these tools into their jobs and fully harness their potential. Many do not know where to get started on this journey. Join us for a tutorial to demystify LLMs and learn practical LLM skills. This session offers valuable insights and demonstrations for beginners and intermediates. We'll start with basics and cover many topics, including prompt engineering, using tools and Agent Mode, LLM automation, and even RAG. By the end, you'll be well equipped to integrate LLMs into your work.

title: Moral Injury at Work: Translating a Clinical Construct for I-O Psychology (Session ID 566)
speakers: Ho Kwan Cheung | Matt Crayne | Deborah DiazGranados | Ashley Durbin | Dr. Maureen E. McCusker | Trevin Glasgow
speaker_affiliations: University of Calgary | University at Albany, SUNY | Virginia Commonwealth University | The Crane & The Bull, Corp | Marriott International | University of Virginia
description: Crayne, M.P. (Chair), DiazGranados, D., Glasgow, T., Stephens, A., McCusker, M.E., & Cheung, H.K. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Moral injury at work: Translating a clinical construct for I-O psychology [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel session introduces moral injury to I-O psychology, clarifying definitions and boundaries, translating clinical insights, and illustrating how potentially morally injurious events arise in everyday work. Panelists will discuss theory, measurement, and practice, highlighting prevention, moral repair, and research directions that distinguish value-driven harm from resource-driven strain.

title: No Experiment, No Problem? Causal Inference in Applied Quasi-Experimental Settings (Session ID 830)
speakers: Ludek Stehlik | Marissa Post
speaker_affiliations: Sanofi
description: Post, M. L., Laurentsyeva, N., Stehlik, L., & Ushakov, I. (2026, April 30 – May 2). No Experiment, No Problem? Causal Inference in Applied Quasi-Experimental Settings [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Evaluating organizational programs without experiments (A/B tests) can make causal inference seem out of reach. This practice-oriented tutorial designed for a beginner to intermediate technical audience highlights usage of quasi-experimental methods and econometric/ML techniques - Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs), Generalized Estimating Equations (GEE), Coarsened Exact Matching (CEM), and Double Machine Learning (DML) - to estimate impact when randomization is infeasible. Using R and Python, participants learn to design, analyze, and communicate credible causal evidence in applied settings.

Retirement Adjustment: Subjective and Social Well-Being of Retirees in Response to The Preretirement Work-Nonwork Interface. Supporting an age-diverse workforce and facilitating retirement transitions are becoming more important for organizations. This symposium advances understanding of successful aging at work and into retirement by examining subjective age, job crafting, age-inclusive HR practices, knowledge sharing, leader uncertainty regulation behaviors, and the work–nonwork interface. Together, these studies illustrate the roles of both individual factors and organizational practices in fostering engagement, motivation, and adjustment across the later career and post-retirement journey.

title: Preparing Future I-O Psychology Professionals for Consulting: Bridging the Skills Gap (Session ID 1228)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will include 3 phases aimed at building critical consulting skills and competencies. First, authors will present research indicating what skills and competencies are important in internal and external consulting in I-O-related roles. Then, audience members will engage in an exercise focused on translating science into practice, targeting the identified skills and competencies. Finally, audience members will hear from I-O professionals working across internal, external, and public sector work contexts—their stories, experiences, and teachable moments navigating their careers.

Addressing cheating and faking threats with experimental test development methods. Butera, H., Baytalskaya, N., Robie, C., & Wingate, T. G. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The impact of honesty agreements on generative AI use in pre-employment assessments. Canagasuriam, D., & Roulin, N. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Shaban, T., Rose, M., & Reid, F. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Preserving test validity in the AI era: Behavioral approaches to online assessment integrity. The rise of AI and growing faking concerns pose serious risks to the fairness and validity of personnel selection. This symposium brings together cutting-edge approaches to safeguarding assessment integrity. Papers address new measurement tools for AI cheating and AI use, benchmarking machine learning against legacy faking indices, behavioral frameworks for secure testing, innovative personality test design, and honesty agreements as deterrence. Collectively, these studies highlight practical, evidence-based strategies for preserving fair and valid assessments.

title: Safeguards or Barriers? A Discussion on the Role of Minimum Qualifications (Session ID 663)
speakers: Lauren Mason | Michael Blair | Rob Calderon | Christina Curnow | Jennifer Flaig | Sara Trevino
speaker_affiliations: SkillEdge Research and Consulting | U.S. Office of Personnel Management | Fors Marsh Group | Emergency Services Consulting International | Human Resources Research Organization (HumRRO)
description: Cheban-Gore, Y. M. (Co-Chair), Mason, L. (Co-Chair), Blair, M. D., Calderón, R., Curnow, C., Flaig, J., & Trevino, S. (2026). Safeguards or barriers? A discussion on the role of minimum qualifications [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Minimum qualifications, such as degrees, licenses and certifications, and prior experience, are intended to safeguard competence and quality. Yet these requirements may also restrict access to employment and reduce workforce diversity. This panel brings together experts from research, policy, and practice to examine the role of minimum qualifications across industries. Discussion will focus on when such requirements are essential, when they act as barriers, and how they can be aligned with skills-based approaches to hiring and workforce development.

title: Striking the Balance: Trust, Integrity, and Expertise at the Human–AI Boundary in I-O (Session ID 1310)

Striking the Balance: Trust, Integrity, and Expertise at the Human-AI Boundary in IO. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel brings together expert I-O psychologists to explore how organizations can balance technological innovation with human expertise in client solutions. Drawing on real-world experience, panelists will share insights on implementing AI in deliverables, addressing successes and challenges. Discussion will focus on maintaining trust, integrity, and transparency while defining the human–AI boundary. Attendees will gain actionable guidance for ethical, client-centered solutions.

title: Taking Stock of PM: Overcoming the Past and Present for a High-Performance Future (Session ID 1421)
tracks: Job Performance/Withdrawal/Misbehavior | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Alan Colquitt | David Futrell | Shonna Waters
speaker_affiliations: Independent Consultant | Consulting | Fractional Insights
description: Colquitt, A.; Brock, S.; Futrell, D., & Waters, S. (2026). Taking Stock of Performance Management: Overcoming the Past and Present for a High-Performance Future. Panel Discussion at the 2026 Conference for the Society of Industrial-Organizational Psychology. This panel explores why performance management (PM) and the pay-for-performance reward systems tied to it continue to fail after 70 years of research and reform. Despite constant redesign, most organizations and employees remain deeply dissatisfied with these processes. Despite this dissatisfaction, organizations have no plans to abandon their processes. Panelists will examine the flawed paradigms and practices behind PM, the forces reshaping organizations and HR practices, and how to re-imagine PM as a process that enables individual, team, and organizational performance.

title: The Science of Flow at Work: Emerging Insights and Unresolved Questions (Session ID 1203)

The Science of Flow at Work: Emerging Insights and Unresolved Questions [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Despite continuous growth in flow research over the past decade, understanding of how to cultivate and sustain it remains fragmented. In an effort to guide future flow research and practice, this panel synthesizes and highlights major developments in theory, methodology, and practice from the past decade, aiming to ensure that flow becomes a vital component of our work experience.

title: This Is How We Do (Did) It: Integrating GenAI for Organizational Impact (Session ID 384)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA., United States. This panel showcases practitioners who have scaled Generative AI (GenAI) for organizational impact. While individual adoption grows, organizations struggle with systematic implementation. Panelists will share frameworks for strategic GenAI deployment—including when and when not to use these technologies, drawing from diverse experiences in work redesign, change management, and capability building. Discussion will emphasize building organizational trust through appropriate use case selection, addressing GenAI's limitations, and achieving scalable integration while managing cultural change.

A Second-Order Quantitative Review and Synthesis of Neuroticism and Its Correlates This symposium examines the dynamic nature of individual differences, focusing on when, why, and how individual attributes may fluctuate over time. Challenging traditional trait-based assumptions, authors highlight emerging theory and methods that capture within-person variability and contextual influences on key constructs related to self-concept, personality and cognitive schema. By integrating systematic reviews, meta-regression, and network-analytic techniques, the session advances understanding of individual differences as dynamic constructs shaped by contextual and situational dynamics.

The emergence of artificial intelligence (AI) represents a profound shift in the way people learn, develop and receive support at work. This symposium examines the current state of knowledge and open questions regarding the use of AI conversational agents and aids in skill acquisition, mentoring, coaching, and socioemotional support.

title: Alliance: Bringing Decent Work to the Next Generation of IWOP Professionals (Session ID 1435)
tracks: I-O Psychologist Career Development/Teaching | Special Session

Bringing decent work to the next generation of I-O Professionals (Panel discussion). Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Professors from 5 universities and 3 nations will discuss how they address decent work and methods they employ to prepare future generations to improve workers’ well-being. Decent work was first advocated by the International Labor Organization and is now a part of the UN’s sustainable Development Goals. Decent work is productive, delivers fair income, security, social protection, chances for development, freedom of expression, the right to organize, and equal opportunity for women and men. It is congruent with the I-O principles but is rarely in textbooks or taught to students.

The debate over employee surveys is intensifying as organizations seek faster, less intrusive ways to understand their workforce. In this IGNITE! session, 6 presenters will cover a range of perspectives challenging the future of both traditional survey methods and emerging passive, AI-enabled technologies. Time will be held at the end for further debate and audience Q&A. The goal is to walk away with (1) a more nuanced understanding of where critical disruption is – or is not – needed alongside (2) practical ideas for utilizing the best of both traditional and emerging methods.

With the professionalization of collegiate athletes, record-high valuations of sports franchises, the globalization of sports, and advancements in analytics and talent evaluation, sports organizations have been called to transform their talent management practices. Although I-O research in this context is sparse, the field is well-positioned to offer scientific, strategic, people-centered approaches to selecting and developing talent. To extend research, authors present papers on elite athletes, emphasizing typical yet critical I-O constructs for predicting behavior and performance on the job.

Helping behavior at work is not uniformly beneficial; its effects depend on the motivations, interpretations, and experiences of both helpers and recipients. This symposium includes 3 papers that examine the complex exchanges between providers and recipients of help. Outcomes for help recipients (e.g., help acceptance, perceived help quality) and help providers (e.g., motivations for and barriers to helping, work engagement, meaningfulness, exhaustion) are examined using various study designs, including qualitative interviews, experimental vignette studies, and daily diary surveys.

Remote and hybrid work are often framed as liberating — free from commutes, rigid schedules, and in-person oversight. Yet, without clear boundaries, work often bleeds into personal life. Participants will leave with insights and practical tools to help themselves and their organizations navigate hidden constraints of working from home.

AI can cluster job roles based on JTA data, facilitate skills-based competency modeling, and inform various practices. Additionally, AI-generated drafts of knowledge, skills, and abilities (KSAs) enable subject matter experts to validate and refine competencies efficiently. Implications for reducing administrative burden and advancing evidence-based talent management are discussed.

The focus on "star" performers is challenged by research showing performance is cyclical and context-dependent. This exposes a "knowing-doing gap": research has shown many ingredients for a high-performance culture, but practitioners still struggle with workable recipes. This panel confronts this by exploring & discussing 3 practitioner dilemmas: the complexity of integration, the nuances of measurement, and the challenge of sustainability. The session provides actionable strategies for building performance systems that are not only effective but truly enduring.

This symposium comprises 3 papers describing how basic interests—more elemental dimensions subsumed by each of the RIASEC dimensions (Su et al., 2019)—are being used to refine and expand understanding of vocational interests.

title: Beyond the Glass Ceiling: Dominance Stereotypes and Spillover Barriers for Women Lead (Session ID 1344)

description: Deepak, P (Chair), (2026, April 30 – May 2). Beyond the Glass Ceiling: Dominance Stereotypes and Spillover Barriers for Women Leaders [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Women face unique barriers after reaching leadership. This symposium integrates 4 studies examining how dominance stereotypes, stigma spillover, and identity threats restrict women's progress in leadership roles. Authors investigate which stereotype dimensions constrain representation, how individual failures produce group penalties, and which organizational interventions work. Findings reveal dominance (not competence) predicts bias, one woman's failure affects others' opportunities, public failures trigger guilt-by-association, and visible male allyship outperforms diversity statements.

Tech, financial services, consumer products, and healthcare comprise over 70% of S&P 500 value, yet less than 10% of SIOP members indicate working in these areas, suggesting underrepresentation in key industries. As such, panelists with unusual roles, such as founder coaching, GenAI adoption at Deloitte, and sales operations at MongoDB and Uber, will share how they reposition I-Os from being critical of business to more business critical. Using Google’s validated design sprint methodology, participants will develop strategies for advancing personal growth and SIOP's strategic priorities.

[Community of Interest]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As organizations undergo significant changes across sectors, employees face increased uncertainty and workplace trauma, challenging their feelings of psychological safety. Through a panel discussion and open forum, attendees will discuss how best to support employees and organizations navigating strain amid unprecedented change. This panel examines EP through the theory and research, highlighting how social identities influence perceptions of leadership potential. Return-to-office (RTO) decisions are as much about communication as policy. This panel explores how the framing of RTO — whether as a loss of flexibility, a path to culture, or a business necessity — shapes employee sentiment, trust, and engagement. Internal practitioners share lessons from their organizations, and an external researcher offers workforce data and client insights. The discussion highlights timely, evidence-based guidance for leaders navigating hybrid work.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This isn't your typical SIOP panel. Modeled after the show Hot Ones, features prominent I-O psychologists answering thought-provoking questions while eating progressively spicier hot wings. The audience will hear leaders discuss what I-O psychology has gotten right, its necessary growth areas, and the future of the field. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. People analytics is racing ahead on 2 tracks: one pulled by rapidly evolving tech stacks (cloud data platforms, AI/ML, self-serve BI), the other by human-centered strategy (I-O rigor, EX design, change and ethics). This session stages a fast-paced debate where panelists rotate positions on core prompts (e.g., org placement, build-vs-buy, skills mix, and governance). Authors pressure-test when technology intensity creates scale without impact—and when strategy depth wins influence but hits capacity limits.

This Ignite session highlights the full lifecycle of employee surveys, from conception and design to deployment, analysis, communication, and follow-through. Panelists from diverse organization settings will share best practices, challenges, and real-world examples demonstration have intentional survey design can transform feedback from data into meaningful organizational change.

The session emphasizes practical instruction and I-O relevance, equipping participants with tools to incorporate sensor-based measurement into research and practice.

Organizations increasingly rely on new hire evaluation surveys to assess quality of hire but face challenges in obtaining meaningful differentiation while maintaining high response rates. This symposium presents recent research on improving these critical measurement tools. Papers examine rating scale optimization, competency selection, completion rates, and timing considerations across organizational contexts. Results demonstrate how thoughtful survey design can address measurement limitations while maintaining practical utility.

Although AI has enabled employee experience (EX) insights to be delivered faster and at scale, AI alone does not create a consistent EX nor its desired outcomes (productivity, retention, etc.). In fact, handled poorly, AI has the ability to harm the quality and impact of actioning. Geared toward intermediate practitioners and drawing on the latest research, this panel, which includes real-world application from organization EX leaders, will discuss how and where to harness the capabilities AI offers, and where human touch remains essential.

Panelists discuss construct clarity, measurement, analytics, and expert testimony in advancing psychosocial safety, supporting emerging legislation, and guiding organizational accountability to reduce misconduct and foster respectful, psychologically safe workplaces.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. There is increasing attention in the media and industry reports on the notion of purpose and meaning. Despite the topic not being new, research within the work domain remains limited, particularly with respect to work stress. In response, this symposium showcases new empirical and theoretical research on the role of meaning in work and life in the experience of stressors and strains at work. In particular, the presentations explore meaning as an appraisal orientation or a process variable that has protective qualities to thwart stressors and strains. This session offers relevant, fresh perspectives for scholars and practitioners alike.

Panelists will share findings from recent studies along with practical guidance for organizations navigating the shift to AI-enhanced leadership. Topics will include trust in AI systems, effects on feedback and decision-making, risks to well-being, and strategies for ethical and effective implementation.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As work and home boundaries blur, understanding their interplay is crucial for sustainable well-being and performance. This symposium presents new insights into the work–home interface. Hammer et al. show that sustained supervisor and coworker support reduces work–family conflict. Lezcano et al. reveal how dual-earner couples jointly manage boundaries under remote work. Demerouti et al. find that family–work facilitation enhances engagement and creativity, whereas job crafting buffers conflict effects. Bakker et al. demonstrate that leisure crafting replenishes energy when family demands are high.

title: Climbers Versus Performers: Disentangling Executive Confidence From Competence (Session ID 249)
speakers: Reece Akhtar | Ted Hayes | Mina Morris

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Many executives labeled “high potential” rise quickly due to confidence and ambition—traits that predict emergence but not long-term success. This panel examines the bias favoring “climbers” over true “performers” with data from global employers, showing how judgment and learning agility better predict effectiveness. Authors share evidence-based assessment and coaching practices, including AI-enabled approaches, to help organizations reward competence rather than confidence.

title: Cutting Through the Jangle: Methods for Addressing Construct Proliferation (Session ID 426)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Construct proliferation is a common problem across many fields, including industrial and organizational psychology. Although construct proliferation is a recognized problem, relatively few methods have been applied to assess it comprehensively. The purpose of this session is to showcase a variety of traditional and innovative methods that can be used to determine if 2 or more constructs are redundant. Although the presenters draw on their own research topics (team cognitions, job attitudes, leadership, and workplace aggression), these will serve as the “ground” while the focus will be on the methods used.

title: Dark Traits in Leadership: From Situational Triggers to Follower Outcomes (Session ID 181)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Dark traits such as narcissism, Machiavellianism, and psychopathy are a common cause of derailment in leaders, but their effects are not uniformly destructive. This symposium showcases research that moves beyond static views on leaders’ dark traits to examine their dynamic expression, situational triggers, and effects on follower and team outcomes. Spanning within-person, dyadic, and team-level analyses, the papers reveal when and how dark traits harm or help. An expert discussant integrates insights, offering a more nuanced perspective on dark leadership.

Although workplace mistreatment includes bullying, harassment, incivility, and abusive supervision, scholars argue that workplace mistreatment cannot be understood through isolated perspectives. Thus, getting at the nuances of workplace mistreatment requires various perspectives. This symposium addresses this call by presenting research that relies on different methods—individual differences, dyadic relationships, team dynamics, and intrapersonal diaries—to broaden understanding of the negative outcomes associated with workplace mistreatment.

Across these works, authors highlight both the challenges and opportunities of eldercare, examining how individual, relational, and organizational factors shape outcomes such as thriving, relationship quality, and well-being. Together, the papers underscore the importance of understanding eldercare as a multifaceted work–family issue with significant implications for organizations, employees, and families.

I-O psychologists are uniquely positioned to do tremendous good in their autism at work research and practice. However, traditional autism frameworks rooted in clinical paradigms can cause unintended harm when applied without critical examination. This tutorial provides a practical framework for designing research and applications that honor autistic people's worth, voice, and expertise. Through case analysis examining both what goes wrong and what works well, participants would develop skills to recognize dignity risks and create work environments that enable autistic people to thrive.

Drawing on data from major multinational companies, authors examine the magnitude of cultural differences, whether they reflect genuine behavioral variations or biases, and discuss best practices for equitable comparisons through audience participation. Expert panelists will discuss practical solutions and engage attendees on strategies that balance ideal approaches with real-world applications.

This master tutorial addresses this challenge by providing hands-on experience with Agentic AI implementation. Participants will learn distinctions between Generative and Agentic AI, set up essential tools, and complete practical exercises. The tutorial bridges the technical skills gap among I-O psychologists, enabling them to effectively leverage these technologies while addressing common implementation barriers.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychologists span diverse industries yet share a common educational foundation. This session shows how practitioners can adapt core skills to tackle similar challenges across sectors. In breakout groups, attendees from fields like military, healthcare, government, and technology will explore cross-industry problem-solving. The goal is to highlight shared competencies, debunk role misconceptions, and empower I-O professionals to lead and thrive across contexts through influence, expertise, and strategic impact.

This symposium shares examples and lessons for implementing responsible AI in I-O research and practices, including scoring narrative responses, automated interviewer debrief preparation process, and competency modeling. The studies use a variety of methods to enhance responsible AI usage, such as Chain-of-Thoughts prompting, input and output sanitization, LLM-as-Judge, and human-in-the-loop.

Featuring 4 presentations at the cutting edge of this field, and providing dedicated time for networking among the presenters and attendees, this session holds relevance for multiple audiences (academic, research, and practitioner).

However, recent research positions personality as more dynamic and changes in tandem with work-related experiences. This symposium presents 4 studies that examine personality change and dynamics within organizational contexts, offering several insights on personality change related to underemployment, job characteristics, turnover, and person-team fit.

Working parenthood is not a uniform experience but is shaped by diverse, intersecting identities and varied strategies parents use to manage them. This symposium brings together 4 papers that advance understanding of these complexities: 2 papers examine parenthood through an intersectional lens, and 2 focus on identity management processes in the workplace. Together, this symposium advances understanding of the diverse realities of parenthood at work, and offers practical insights on how organizations can better support and retain working parents across diverse identities. Dr. Winny Shen will serve as the discussant, providing an integrative commentary.

I will illustrate the technique through a publicly available or a valid AI-generated Python code and synthetic/simulated dataset with numeric (not text) variables.

Authors hope attendees will come with open minds and critical questions to help chart a new path for the future of diversity training.

This symposium showcases research aimed at understanding how organizational DEI policies are perceived throughout the employment lifecycle, from prospective applicants to labor market participants. In doing so, authors shed light on how DEI messaging impacts perceivers and why some remain resistant to DEI policies.

Authors discuss how workplace rituals and narratives normalize harm and spill into personal life. Participants will gain research-based insights and strategies for recognizing, escaping, and preventing toxic CULT-ure.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and computational psychometrics are transforming personality assessment to be more adaptive, fair, and engaging. This symposium highlights 3 innovations: image-based assessments that enhance accessibility and candidate experience; large language models (LLMs) that audit fairness by detecting semantic and cultural bias; and AI-driven adaptive testing using non-linear, forced-choice models to reduce faking and improve validity. A closing commentary examines the ethical and theoretical implications of AI-enabled assessment for transparency and responsible practice. Together, these advances show how AI can enhance psychometric rigor and fairness in modern testing.

Childhood adversity and adult work lives: Implications for I-O research and practice [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) have lasting effects on health, development, and life outcomes, yet their influence in work settings remains understudied in I-O psychology. This panel brings together experts to examine how early-life adversity can shape adult work lives, well-being, and career trajectories. Panelists will discuss conceptual and applied challenges, highlighting the importance of ACEs research in a workplace context, and how trauma-informed and life-course perspectives can play a hidden role in various workplace outcomes.

title: Connecting the Dots: Network Perspectives on Organizational Attitudes and Behavior (Session ID 1017)
speakers: Gary Burns | Steven Holthouser | Nathan Carter | Ryan Thibodeau | Alexander DeChurch
speaker_affiliations: Florida Institute of Technology | Northrop Grumman | PepsiCo
description: Burns, G. N. (Co-Chair), Holthouser, S. A. (Co-Chair), & Carter, N. T. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Connecting the dots: Network perspectives on organizational attitudes and behavior [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This symposium highlights how network analysis offers a unifying framework for understanding organizational attitudes and relationships. Using both psychometric and egocentric approaches, 3 studies examine organizational commitment, newcomer socialization, and training evaluation through the lens of interconnected systems. Together, they demonstrate how mapping these networks reveals the central links that shape behavior, engagement, and organizational outcomes.

title: Cultural Humility: The Courage to Stay Curious (Session ID 1273)
speakers: Kristine Reeves | Alyssa Lopez | Scott Campanario
speaker_affiliations: Washington State House of Representatives | Seattle Pacific University | ORS Impact
description: Reeves, K. M., Campanario, S., Lopez, A. & Yost, P. R. (2026, April 30–May 2). Cultural humility: The courage to stay curious [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As workplaces become more diverse and interconnected, cultural humility (Tervalon & Murray-García, 1998; Foronda, 2020) offers a framework for inclusive leadership grounded in self-reflection, openness, and lifelong learning. This panel explores theory, evidence, and applications of cultural humility across individual, team, and organizational levels. Panelists will bridge research and practice to highlight how humility fosters authentic leadership, equity, and organizational cultures rooted in curiosity, empathy, and accountability.

title: Embedding Neuroinclusion Into Organizational Effectiveness Programs (Session ID 1180)
speakers: Maria Sakata | Dejannae Lang | Lynette (Herbert) Killian | Tiffany Jameson | Michael Moon | Elizabeth Follmer
speaker_affiliations: RethinkFirst | Carter Development Group | Neuro Consulting Solutions | grit & flow | AbbVie | Wells Fargo Center for Neurodiversity & Inclusive Employment at UConn
description: Sakata, M. (Chair), Ahmed Kazmi, M., Fountaine, M., Herbert, L., Jameson, T., & Lang, D. (2025, April). Embedding neuroinclusion into organizational effectiveness [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel showcases practitioners embedding neuroinclusion and equity into organizational systems. Panelists will share case studies illustrating how neuroinclusive practices are integrated into organizational effectiveness efforts. Attendees will gain practitioner insights, frameworks, and strategies for shifting from individual accommodations to systemic program design that fosters belonging and performance.

title: From Screen to Science: Finding I-O in TV, Movies, and Games (Session ID 1011)
speakers: Amy Grubb | Allen Kamin | James Gallman | Sandra Lionetti
speaker_affiliations: Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) | Oracle
description: Kamin, A., Lionetti, S., Walsh, M., Grubb, A., and Gallman, J. (2026). From Screen to Science: Finding IO in Movies, Games, and Sports. [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LO, United States. Presenters compete in a fast-paced “I-O in the Wild” showdown! Through live polling, audience voting, and playful debate, participants will gain practical examples and creative tools to explain I-O concepts through relatable, real-world analogies. Leave with fresh ways to connect the science to everyday work, confidently explain what I-Os do beyond our discipline, and inspire others to see and teach I-O in new ways. Cast your vote for the presenter with the best real-world portrayal of I-O psychology in popular culture!

title: From in the Closet to in the Office: Experiences of LGBTQ+ Employees at Work (Session ID 981)
speakers: Renee Leger | Yochi Cohen-Charash | David Arena | Damon Stango | Evan Nault | Shreya Cho

From in the Closet to in the Office: Experiences of LGBT+ Employees at Work [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Cho, S., Rodriguez, W. A., & Zhou, Z. (2026). LGBTQ+ Microaggressions and Silence: The Roles of PCV and Oppositional Courage. Stango, D., Rodriguez, M, & Solis, L. (2026). Trans and Nonbinary Worker Well-Being: A Scoping Review. Nault, E., Jaramillo, K, Castro, M. D., & Sabat, I.E. (2026). Social and Occupational Stereotypes of American Nonbinary Employees. Disclosing a marginalized identity is no small decision, as it may have lasting impacts, no matter what the identity is. This symposium highlights the nuanced experiences and challenges unique to LGBTQ+ employees navigating professional environments. Authors begin with experiences that affect the entire LGBTQ+ community before narrowing in on the trans and nonbinary community.

title: Future Storming Insights: Emerging Trends and the Future of I-O Psychology (Session ID 527)
speakers: Matthew Tonken | Amanda Shull Tomkoria | Angela Lane | Christina Norris-Watts | Lynn Collins, Ph.D.
speaker_affiliations: BTS | Nike | IE University | Johnson & Johnson
description: Collins, L., Tonken, M., Lane, A., Tomkuria, A., & Norris-Watts, C. (2026, April). Future storming insights: Emerging trends and the future of I-O psychology [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel will share findings from the 2026 Future Storming workshop, where talent leaders and I-O psychologists engaged in future storming and explored scenarios on future trends. In 2023 authors ran a similar workshop and forecasted 5 shifts that have since begun to reshape talent and leadership, demonstrating the value of future-back thinking. Panelists will highlight fresh insights, explore implications for I-O psychology, and invite audience engagement. This session will spark dialogue on how the field will evolve to stay ahead of change.

title: Has GenAI Taken My Job Yet? I-O’s Role in the GenAI Workplace (Session ID 698)
speakers: Robert Brusso | Ann Kwak | Daniel Schmerling | Anne Scaduto
speaker_affiliations: Amazon | Capital One | GE Aerospace
description: Brusso, R. C. (Chair), Kwak, A., Scaduto, A., & Schmerling, D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Has GenAI taken my job yet? What’s changed for I-Os with the emergence of GenAI in the workplace [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As GenAI continues to reshape the workplace, I-O psychologists face pressing questions about their professional future. This panel brings together industry experts to demonstrate how GenAI serves as a powerful tool to enhance rather than replace I-O capabilities. Through real-world examples, panelists will showcase the critical intersection of human expertise and AI capabilities, highlighting areas where I-O psychology expertise remains irreplaceable. Attendees will gain practical insights into effectively leveraging GenAI while maintaining core I-O competencies.

title: Leveraging I-O Psychology During Times of Uncertainty and Ambiguity (Session ID 160)
speakers: Mariangela Battista | Elizabeth Kolmstetter | Gena Cox | Mathian Mat Osicki | Sara Weiner | Seth Kamen | Dr. William Shepherd
speaker_affiliations: Sirena Consulting LLC | Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (Retired) | Feels Human, LLC | GE Aerospace | UN Committee Chair | People Solutions | Elevation Leadership, LLC
description: Battista, M., Kolmstetter, E., Cober, R., Cox, G., Kamen, S., Osicki, M., Shephard. W., Weiner, S. (2026, April). Leveraging I-O Psychology During Times of Uncertainty and Ambiguity. Roundtable. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA. The current global work environment is presenting challenges for I-O psychologists. I-Os are asking questions and looking for guidance as everyone is trying to navigate this new world. I-Os are living in unprecedented times. How can I-O psychologists manage through all this uncertainty? How can they continue to leverage the science/evidence-based research to do work as I-O psychologists? This roundtable provides a forum for SIOP members to discuss various topics impacting I-O psychologists today whether they are in-house practitioners, consultants, or academics.

Smart Tech, Healthy Workers: Exploring Wearable Technology in Occupational Health [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores how wearable technologies are advancing occupational health research and practice. Experts from diverse fields will share lessons learned from applying wearables in occupational health related research. Panelists will discuss successes, challenges, and practical strategies for using wearable data to improve worker well-being, performance, and safety, as well as emerging trends shaping the future of wearable technology in the workplace.

title: The Future Called—It Wants Its Ethics Back: Rebooting Ethics in the Tech-Forward Work (Session ID 1013)
speakers: Emi Barresi | Payton Stewart
speaker_affiliations: HEARTH Team Leadership | Leadership Development Institute - LSU
description: Barresi, E. (Presenter), Stewart, P. (Presenter), Dave, P. (Facilitator). (2026, April 30 – May 2). TITLE HERE [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As technology automation reshapes the workforce, organizations face a growing cost of ethical oversight: loss of trust, empathy, and accountability in tech-mediated systems. Grounded in socio-technical and human-AI teaming research, this session introduces the HEARTH model (Human Ethics Approach for Remote and Tech-Hybrid) for ethical leadership. Through a micro-case and interactive activity, participants explore how to preserve human ethics and adaptability while collaborating with intelligent systems to lead with integrity in the digital age.
